Additionally, he exhibits referential delusions, perceiving ordinary events as having personal significance, and tactile hallucinations, involving sensations of being touched on the skin. These psychotic symptoms are reported to occur exclusively within the context of his major depressive episodes, which have led to significant functional impairment.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    D. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
answer: D. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

question: A 41-year-old single female salon owner presents with a one-month history of a distinct period of persistently elevated mood, accompanied by marked functional impairment. During this time, the patient has exhibited increased psychomotor activity, reporting an uncharacteristic surge in work output and initiation of multiple simultaneous projects. Her speech has become notably pressured and voluminous, often continuing without pause. She describes an increased distractibility, with her attention frequently shifting in response to external stimuli. Socially, she has been more disinhibited and outgoing than is typical for her. Reports from collateral sources indicate out-of-character engagement in sexual indiscretions. These symptoms represent a significant change from her baseline functioning.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Bipolar I Disorder
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)

question: A 48-year-old married male subway driver presents with a 2-year and 4-month history of distress and functional impairment. He experiences involuntary, intrusive dissociative reactions, feeling as though past traumatic events are recurring, and actively avoids distressing internal reminders of these events. He reports a generalized amnesia regarding the traumatic event itself, alongside pervasive negative cognitions, such as a belief that the world is inherently dangerous and life is without meaning. He also tends to attribute blame to others for the trauma's occurrence or its consequences. Physiologically, the patient complains of middle insomnia, manifesting as frequent nocturnal awakenings. He exhibits heightened arousal, evidenced by constant environmental scanning for threats and occasional episodes of physical aggression.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    D. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder

question: A 45-year-old married female, who works as a bank branch manager, presents with a four-month history of a recurrent eating disorder characterized by episodes of binge eating. During these episodes, she consumes an objectively large amount of food within a discrete period, accompanied by a subjective sense of loss of control over her eating. These episodes are followed by compensatory behaviors, specifically fasting, to mitigate perceived weight gain. The patient also exhibits significant preoccupation with her body shape and weight, reporting a distorted body image where she perceives herself as much larger than her actual physical appearance. The binge eating and inappropriate compensatory behaviors have occurred, on average, at least once a week for the past four months.
option:
A. Bulimia Nervosa
    B. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    C. Binge-Eating Disorder
    D. Anorexia Nervosa

question:
A 47-year-old married female insurance claims adjuster presents with recurrent mood symptoms. Over the past 6 months, she has experienced a distinct 1-month period characterized by persistently elevated and expansive mood with markedly increased energy and activity. During this interval, she reported a markedly reduced need for sleep without associated fatigue, pressured and excessive speech, and subjectively racing and crowded thoughts leading to disorganized goal pursuit. She displayed increased sociability and unusually high levels of engagement with others. There were also grandiose, overvalued ideas regarding having a special role or mission. Despite these changes, she maintained occupational and social functioning without clear objective impairment.

This was followed by a 5-month period of pervasive low mood and loss of interest in previously enjoyable activities. She describes markedly reduced appetite, early morning awakenings with inability to resume sleep, and significant physical exhaustion with minimal exertion. Observed psychomotor agitation has included restlessness and difficulty remaining seated. She reports excessive self-blame out of proportion to events and notable difficulty sustaining attention and concentration at work and in daily activities. These depressive symptoms have resulted in significant impairment in occupational performance and household responsibilities.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Major Depressive Disorder
    C. Bipolar II Disorder
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question: A 32-year-old married female, working as a wedding planner, presents with a psychiatric syndrome marked by a persistent decline in functioning over the past 1 year and 2 months, with observable deterioration in occupational performance and interpersonal engagement. For the past 4 months, she has exhibited overt psychotic features, including complex referential ideation in which neutral environmental stimuli, comments, or events are interpreted as having personal and often symbolic significance directed toward her. She intermittently reports unexplained bitter or metallic tastes in the absence of corresponding gustatory stimuli, occurring in the context of full wakefulness and awareness. Her behavior has become increasingly idiosyncratic, with noticeable mannerisms—exaggerated, circuitous movements and gestures that disrupt the natural flow of goal-directed activity. Social withdrawal is prominent, with marked reduction in desire for social interaction, diminished emotional expressiveness, and prolonged periods of self-isolation. There is no recent change in marital or occupational status that temporally coincides with symptom onset; these life circumstances predate symptom emergence by several years. Functional capacity has progressively declined, evidenced by difficulty maintaining work responsibilities and sustaining close relationships. Cognition remains partially intact, but is encumbered by the presence of non-bizarre and bizarre perceptual and ideational disturbances.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Schizophrenia
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)

question: A 57-year-old married female, who works as a school principal, presented with an 11-month history of symptoms characterized by significant inattention and executive dysfunction. The patient reports chronic difficulty with sustained attention, often struggling to maintain focus during tasks. She frequently appears not to listen when directly addressed and has a history of not following through on instructions, leading to unfinished work-related duties and household chores. Marked forgetfulness in daily activities, such as returning calls, paying bills, and keeping appointments, is also noted. Furthermore, she frequently misplaces essential items. This constellation of symptoms has resulted in demonstrable functional impairment. Historical information indicates symptom onset prior to 12 years of age.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    C. Acute Stress Disorder
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)

question:
A 24-year-old single female, employed as a social media manager, presents with a 7-month history of a persistent depressive episode associated with significant functional decline. The patient describes pervasive emotional blunting, with marked reduction in the capacity to experience positive or negative affect. She reports pronounced social withdrawal, with diminished engagement in usual relationships and activities. There is a documented weight gain exceeding 5% of her baseline over the course of one month. Psychomotor activity is notably slowed, with visible reduction in movement speed and general motor responsiveness. She endorses marked loss of energy, requiring substantial effort to complete basic daily tasks and work-related responsibilities.

During this same depressive period, the patient exhibits fixed, non–mood-congruent grandiose beliefs involving having exceptional abilities and special importance, without corresponding evidence. She also reports recurrent olfactory perceptions of odors not present in the environment. These psychotic symptoms have occurred exclusively in the context of the current depressive episode. Overall, symptoms have led to clear impairment in occupational performance and social functioning.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    D. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
answer: A. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

question: A 48-year-old married male employed as a Waste Management Officer presents with a 1-year and 2-month history of functional impairment. For the past four months, he has reported somatic delusions, expressing a baseless conviction of serious bodily abnormalities. Concurrently, he has experienced visual hallucinations, characterized by perceiving individuals and objects that are not present. Clinical examination noted waxy flexibility. The patient also reports anhedonia, marked by a decreased capacity to experience pleasure or interest in previously enjoyable activities.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Schizophrenia
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)

question:
**Clinical Case Summary**

A 65-year-old male, married and employed as a building janitor, presents with a six-month history of recurrent episodes of binge eating characterized by the consumption of objectively large amounts of food in discrete time periods, during which he reports a persistent sense of loss of control over his eating behavior. These episodes are followed by marked distress and engagement in inappropriate compensatory behaviors aimed at preventing weight gain. Specifically, the patient reports regular self-induced vomiting and periods of prolonged fasting following binges.  

He demonstrates significant disturbances in body image, consistently perceiving himself as overweight despite a normal body mass index, and fixates on specific body regions as being excessively large when viewed in the mirror. A notable fear of weight gain is present, with particular anxiety centered around consuming high-calorie foods, which he believes will cause immediate fat accumulation.  

The pattern of binge episodes and compensatory behaviors has occurred, on average, more than once per week over the past six months. There is no evidence of concurrent anorexia nervosa. The patient’s symptoms are not attributable to medical comorbidities or substance use, and cognitive functioning remains intact. Psychosocial history reveals marital stability and no recent psychosocial stressors; longstanding patterns of disordered eating are reported, though symptom escalation began six months ago.
option:
A. Anorexia Nervosa
    B. Bulimia Nervosa
    C. Binge-Eating Disorder
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)

question:
A 49-year-old single female librarian presents with a 3-month history of markedly elevated and irritable mood accompanied by significant behavioral and functional changes. She reports a pronounced increase in physical and mental energy, with difficulty remaining still and a persistent drive to stay active. Speech has become markedly accelerated and pressured, making interruption challenging. She demonstrates frequent shifts between multiple concurrent activities without completion, describing numerous new projects and plans started but abandoned.

The patient exhibits increased sociability, engaging in unusually frequent social contacts and interactions compared to her baseline. There is a clear pattern of disinhibited behavior, including engagement in uncharacteristically risky sexual encounters during this period. She describes fixed beliefs that she has a unique role and exceptional abilities, consistent with grandiose delusions; these beliefs are temporally confined to the current elevated mood state. No hallucinations are reported.

These symptoms have led to substantial impairment in occupational functioning and interpersonal relationships. There is no indication that occupational status or marital status are temporally associated with the onset of the described symptoms, which emerged well after those life circumstances were established.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Bipolar I Disorder
    D. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
answer: D. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
A 29-year-old male, married and working as a fund manager, presented with a prolonged course of intentional dietary restriction and progressive weight loss. Over several months, the patient has exhibited consistent avoidance of main meals, regularly skipping breakfast, lunch, and dinner despite no medical indication for such restriction. He has maintained a deliberate pattern of reduced caloric intake, particularly avoiding foods perceived as high in calories, driven by a marked fear that consumption of these foods will result in rapid weight gain.

The patient has experienced continuous, self-induced weight loss despite being underweight on serial measurements. Physical manifestations consistent with low body weight, including recurrent dizziness and noticeable hair thinning, have been reported; however, the patient minimizes these symptoms and does not perceive them as concerning or warranting medical intervention. No recent changes in marital or occupational status were noted. The onset of eating-related behaviors preceded any associated physical symptoms by several months, with initial food restriction beginning well before the emergence of physiological complications. Cognitive preoccupation with weight, body shape, and food intake remains elevated, with persistent perception of overweight despite objective underweight.
option:
A. Specific Phobia
    B. Bulimia Nervosa
    C. Schizophrenia
    D. Anorexia Nervosa

question: A 68-year-old married retired civil servant presents with a 15-month history of pervasive apprehension. He experiences significant difficulty in sustaining concentration due to intrusive worrisome thoughts. These worries often follow a 'chaining' pattern, where one concern rapidly escalates into a catastrophic sequence of 'what if' scenarios. He reports a profound intolerance of uncertainty regarding future events. Physiologically, the patient describes persistent physical fatigue, noting his body feels heavy and becomes easily exhausted even with minimal exertion. He also reports early morning awakenings with an inability to return to sleep, contributing to his overall distress. These symptoms have led to notable functional impairment in his daily life.
option:
A. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    B. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    C. Major Depressive Disorder
    D. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
A 55-year-old divorced male pest control specialist presents with a 1 year and 1 month history of trauma-related symptoms causing significant functional impairment. He reports exposure to a traumatic event, the details of which he is largely unable to recall, describing substantial gaps in memory for most aspects of the incident, consistent with generalized amnesia.

Since the event, the patient experiences marked physiological arousal (including palpitations and tremulousness) when confronted with internal or external cues reminiscent of the trauma, with prolonged difficulty returning to baseline after being startled. He engages in persistent avoidance of external reminders such as specific locations, situations, and conversations associated with the event.

There is pronounced social withdrawal, with diminished interest in maintaining interpersonal relationships. He endorses ongoing feelings of shame and humiliation related to the trauma and describes emotional numbing, including inability to experience affection or love toward family or romantic partners. Sleep is disturbed by early morning awakening with inability to resume sleep. He reports using alcohol and/or other substances in a self-destructive pattern to cope with distress. These symptoms collectively impair occupational functioning and social engagement.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Bipolar I Disorder
    D. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder

question:
A 62-year-old married male security guard presents with a 10‑month history of mood and psychotic symptoms associated with significant functional decline at work and home. For approximately 8 months, he has exhibited disorganized speech characterized by idiosyncratic, self-invented word usage, along with markedly childlike, inappropriate joking and behavior in adult social contexts. He has become notably socially withdrawn, with diminished interest in interactions outside of required contacts.

Over the past 6 months, he has had a sustained period of abnormally elevated and expansive mood with markedly increased energy, decreased need for sleep, and pronounced distractibility, such that he is unable to maintain attention on tasks. During this time, he has engaged in excessive, imprudent financial activities, including unreviewed business ventures, and has demonstrated increased, intrusive sociability. These mood symptoms have been present for the majority of the illness course and overlap temporally with the psychotic features.

In addition, he reports persistent, fixed false beliefs of serious internal bodily disease without medical corroboration, and recurrent abnormal taste sensations in the absence of external stimuli, which have occurred for at least 2 weeks in periods without prominent mood symptoms.
option:
A. Schizophrenia
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question:
A 30-year-old single male oil rig worker presents with a 1 year and 1 month history of pervasive anxiety accompanied by significant functional impairment. He reports persistent, excessive apprehension occurring on most days, described as a constant mental preoccupation with multiple life domains. Worry frequently escalates in a sequential manner, where one concern leads to another and culminates in increasingly catastrophic expectations.

He describes heightened psychophysiological arousal, including feeling persistently on edge and being easily startled by relatively minor environmental stimuli. His tolerance for everyday frustrations is markedly reduced, with minimal delays or inconveniences provoking disproportionate irritability and loss of patience.

Sleep is non-restorative despite adequate opportunity and duration, with the patient awakening unrefreshed and experiencing daytime fatigue and reduced stress tolerance. These symptoms have contributed to difficulties concentrating at work, increased errors, and strained interactions with coworkers. He denies any temporal association between his occupational status and symptom onset. There is no report of acute precipitating events, and symptoms have remained relatively continuous over the described period.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Specific Phobia
    C. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)

question:
A 42-year-old married female journalist presents with a 1 year and 1 month history of prominent attentional difficulties associated with functional impairment at work and at home. She reports a longstanding pattern of similar symptoms beginning in childhood, with onset prior to age 12 years, though she was not formally evaluated at that time.

Current symptoms include frequent errors in written and administrative tasks attributed to insufficient attention to detail, and she often appears not to register information when others speak to her directly. She describes marked difficulty planning and sequencing tasks, meeting deadlines, and maintaining an organized workflow. She frequently misplaces objects required for work and daily activities. She is easily diverted by environmental stimuli and by internally generated, unrelated thoughts, leading to incomplete tasks and prolonged time to completion.

In terms of hyperactivity/impulsivity-related features, she denies overt motor restlessness such as running or pacing but endorses a persistent internal sense of restlessness. She has difficulty engaging in quiet, restful leisure activities, often feeling compelled to multitask or seek additional stimulation. These symptoms interfere with occupational performance and household responsibilities. There is no evidence that her occupational or marital status temporally precipitated the current symptom pattern.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    D. Bipolar II Disorder

question:
A 47-year-old single female, employed as a department store sales manager, presents with a history of mood disturbances.
For a duration of two weeks, the patient experienced a distinct period characterized by significant mood lability, manifesting as rapid and unpredictable shifts between euphoria and irritability. Concurrently, she demonstrated psychomotor agitation, an overestimation of her abilities, and a notably reduced need for sleep despite feeling refreshed. Her attention was highly responsive to extraneous stimuli, and she engaged in occupational hyperactivity, initiating an increased number of projects. This acute presentation resulted in functional impairment.
Preceding this, approximately one month prior, the patient endured a month-long depressive episode. During this time, she reported a persistently depressed mood, hypersomnia, and chronic fatigue. She also expressed feelings of worthlessness, experienced reduced concentration, and harbored recurrent thoughts of death, though without active suicidal intent. This depressive phase also led to significant functional impairment.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    D. Bipolar I Disorder

question: A 32-year-old married male sales representative presents with a 2-year and 4-month history of functional decline. For the past 3 months, he has reported experiencing tactile hallucinations, specifically a sensation of being touched or feeling movement on his skin without an external stimulus. His presentation also includes significant diminished emotional expression, characterized by reduced affect in facial expressions, eye contact, and speech intonation. Additionally, he exhibits mannerisms, described as odd and circumstantial caricatures of normal actions. The patient’s symptoms have led to a marked impairment in his daily functioning.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Schizophrenia
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Delusional Disorder

question:
A 42-year-old divorced female custom furniture maker presents with a 6-day history of marked psychological and behavioral changes following exposure to a traumatic event. During the event, she experienced pronounced alteration in time perception, describing the episode as unfolding in an abnormally slowed and fragmented manner. Since then, she reports intense emotional distress when encountering internal or external reminders that resemble aspects of the trauma.

She describes active efforts to push away trauma-related thoughts and memories, attempting to suppress associated emotions. Despite objectively successful outcomes in her work, she reports diminished capacity to experience satisfaction or pride in her achievements. Over the same period, she has been persistently irritable, with a low threshold for frustration and frequent episodes of heightened tension.

She demonstrates exaggerated startle responses, particularly to unexpected physical contact, which elicits marked autonomic arousal and anxiety. These symptoms have significantly interfered with her occupational functioning and interpersonal interactions. There is no indication that her divorced status temporally contributed to the onset of symptoms, as the marital dissolution occurred long before the traumatic event and the emergence of current complaints.
option:
A. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    B. Bipolar I Disorder
    C. Acute Stress Disorder
    D. Schizophrenia

question:
A 49-year-old single female librarian presented with an 8-month history of prominent hyperactivity and impulsivity causing functional impairment at work and in social settings. She reports longstanding patterns of similar behaviors beginning in childhood, well before age 12, with academic teachers noting difficulty remaining seated and frequent interruptions during class.

Currently, the patient demonstrates frequent motor restlessness, including persistent fidgeting with her hands and feet and shifting in her chair during meetings. She has difficulty remaining seated during situations that typically require sustained sitting, such as staff conferences and patron consultations. She reports being unable to engage in quiet leisure activities for extended periods and tends to seek constant activity, describing feeling compelled to be continually moving or doing something.

Colleagues have observed that she often talks at length and at a rapid pace, requiring redirection in group discussions. She frequently interrupts others during conversations and intrudes into ongoing interactions or tasks, including interjecting into patrons’ discussions and co-workers’ projects, which has led to interpersonal strain and concerns about her workplace performance.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    D. Delusional Disorder

question:
**Clinical Case Summary**

A 48-year-old married male, employed as a subway driver operating underground transit services, presents with a recurrent pattern of mood disturbances. Over the past several years, the patient has experienced distinct periods of altered mood and functioning. He reported one episode lasting approximately one month characterized by persistently elevated mood, increased goal-directed activity, and augmented energy. During this period, he displayed pressured speech, racing thoughts, and a decreased need for sleep without subjective fatigue. His self-reported confidence markedly increased, with expressed beliefs in exceptional personal capabilities. He undertook multiple additional work-related tasks and projects beyond his usual responsibilities, though this period was not associated with marked functional impairment or hospitalization.

This episode was preceded by a five-month depressive episode marked by persistent emptiness, anhedonia, hypersomnia, and increased appetite. He exhibited psychomotor slowing, manifested by reduced walking speed and delay in initiating movements, along with pervasive physical fatigue and cognitive dulling, describing difficulty concentrating and mental fogginess. He endorsed feelings of worthlessness and a diminished sense of self-worth. This depressive episode was associated with clinically significant functional impairment in occupational and social domains. There is no indication of psychotic features during either phase. The patient’s marital status and occupational role have remained stable, with the onset of symptoms occurring well after these life circumstances were established.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Major Depressive Disorder
    C. Bipolar II Disorder
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question: A 58-year-old male, widowed and working as a night watchman, presents with a duration of 11 months of unusual beliefs. The patient exhibits persistent conviction that neutral events and remarks hold a specific, personal significance for him. These referential beliefs have been consistently present for nearly a year. Although these beliefs are fixed, his overall psychosocial and occupational functioning remains largely unimpaired outside of the direct sphere of these specific convictions. There is no evidence of thought disorder or other prominent psychotic symptoms.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Delusional Disorder
    D. Schizophrenia

question:
A 57-year-old married female school principal presents with a 14-month history of significant difficulties with attention and hyperactivity/impulsivity, with symptom onset reported prior to 12 years of age. She frequently demonstrates inattention to detail, often making careless errors, and reports chronic forgetfulness in daily activities, including managing chores and appointments. She describes challenges with following through on instructions and completing tasks at work, along with a tendency to misplace essential items. Distractibility by extraneous stimuli, including unrelated thoughts, is also noted.

Regarding hyperactivity and impulsivity, the patient experiences an internal sense of restlessness and difficulty engaging in quiet leisure activities. She describes often feeling "on the go," as if "driven by a motor." Socially, she reports frequently blurting out answers prematurely and experiencing difficulty waiting her turn in conversations. These symptoms have resulted in notable functional impairment across multiple settings.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    C. Bipolar II Disorder
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)

question: A 46-year-old married female, working as a matchmaker and couple manager, presents with a 2-year and 3-month history of psychiatric symptoms meeting criteria for a chronic psychotic and mood disorder. Over the past 2 years and 1 month, she has exhibited a major depressive episode characterized by persistent loss of motivation, psychomotor retardation, non-restorative sleep, ruminative guilt, and significant weight loss exceeding 5% of her body weight within one month without intentional dieting. Anhedonia has been prominent throughout this period. Concomitantly, she has experienced psychotic symptoms for 1 year and 8 months, including persecutory delusions and gustatory hallucinations. These psychotic features persisted for over two weeks in the absence of prominent mood symptoms at several points during the course of her illness. Neurological examination revealed waxy flexibility, consistent with catatonic features. The depressive episode has been present for the majority of the illness duration, with functional impairment evident in occupational and interpersonal domains. There is no indication that marital status or occupation preceded or triggered symptom onset, both of which predate symptom emergence by several years. The clinical course satisfies criteria for sustained mood and psychotic disturbance with interspersed periods of psychosis independent of mood episodes.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question: A 45-year-old married male M&A consultant presents with a clinical picture characterized by intrusive and persistent thoughts, as well as repetitive behaviors, that have been present for approximately one year. He reports distressing, forbidden thoughts of an aggressive or taboo nature. These thoughts are ego-dystonic and cause significant anxiety. To mitigate this distress, the patient engages in compulsive acts of ordering and arranging objects in a precise and specific manner. This ritualistic behavior is performed in an attempt to alleviate the anxiety provoked by the obsessional thoughts. The patient reports that these symptoms cause significant functional impairment in his professional and personal life.
option:
A. Schizophrenia
    B. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    C. Body Dysmorphic Disorder
    D. Bulimia Nervosa

question: A 38-year-old single male, employed as a tattoo artist, presents with a three-month history of significant mood and behavioral disturbances. He reports a pervasive irritable mood, characterized by a low frustration tolerance and aggressive outbursts to minor stimuli. Clinically, he demonstrates marked hyperactivity, taking on numerous projects simultaneously, far exceeding his usual capacity. He exhibits an inflated sense of self-confidence, expressing a belief in limitless capabilities and invulnerability. The patient's speech is notably pressured and excessive, and he struggles with sustained attention, frequently shifting focus. Socially, he has become uncharacteristically outgoing and gregarious. He has also engaged in impulsive and risky behaviors, including reckless driving, without appropriate consideration of consequences. These symptoms have led to significant functional impairment. Concurrently, he reports fixed, unfounded beliefs that his romantic partner has been unfaithful. These delusional symptoms occur exclusively in the context of the described mood disturbance.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Bipolar I Disorder
    D. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
answer: D. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question: A 26-year-old single male, employed as a smartphone app developer, presents with a 9-month history of persistent anxiety and related symptoms. The patient reports difficulty modulating excessive worry, primarily concerning work performance and future life events, despite repeated attempts to redirect or suppress these thoughts. He exhibits marked intolerance of uncertainty, frequently engaging in rumination involving repetitive “what if” scenarios that prolong distress. Physical manifestations include chronic muscular tension, with generalized bodily stiffness and an inability to achieve relaxation, even in resting states. Concentration is notably impaired; the patient describes an inability to sustain attention on tasks, as his focus is frequently interrupted by intrusive anxious cognitions. Sleep is further disrupted by recurrent, vivid nightmares of falling or being chased, which result in nocturnal arousal and difficulty returning to sleep. The symptom cluster has led to measurable functional impairment, affecting both occupational productivity and personal well-being. No acute stressors coincide with symptom onset; the patient reports being single and previously divorced approximately three years prior, with no significant life changes in the preceding year. Symptoms are not attributable to substance use or another medical or psychiatric condition.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Acute Stress Disorder
    C. Specific Phobia
    D. Generalized Anxiety Disorder

question: A 42-year-old single male veterinarian presents with a four-month history of significant mood disturbance. He reports frequent tearfulness, profound anhedonia characterized by an indifference to his professional achievements, and a pervasive loss of energy that impairs his daily functioning. He expresses prominent feelings of worthlessness and experiences sudden, intrusive suicidal impulses, such as urges to jump from heights or run into traffic, though without specific planning. Objectively, he exhibits psychomotor retardation with notable latency in his speech. Concurrent with these symptoms, the patient describes visual hallucinations, specifically seeing individuals and objects that are not present. These perceptual disturbances are exclusively observed during the depressive episodes.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
answer: D. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

question: A 27-year-old single male presented with a history of intentional restriction of dietary intake over the past ten months. He reports consuming extremely small portions of food, often limited to one or two bites per meal, and expresses significant apprehension regarding high-calorie foods due to an intense fear of associated weight gain. The patient has experienced continuous and significant weight loss. His self-worth and perception of success are noted to be predominantly contingent upon his body shape and weight.
option:
A. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    B. Binge-Eating Disorder
    C. Anorexia Nervosa
    D. Bulimia Nervosa

question:
A 22-year-old single female, employed part-time as a barista, presents with a 2-year-3-month history of mood and psychotic symptoms associated with significant functional decline in occupational and social domains. For approximately 2 years and 1 month, she has exhibited a persistent depressive episode characterized by markedly reduced motivation, pronounced slowing of motor activity, non-restorative sleep despite adequate duration, significant unintentional weight loss exceeding 5% over 1 month, and pervasive, repetitive self-blaming thoughts centered on minor past errors. These depressive symptoms have been present for the majority of the course of illness and are temporally concurrent with psychotic features.

Over the past 1 year and 8 months, she has endorsed fixed beliefs of being watched and targeted by others, as well as episodes of abnormal taste perceptions in the absence of external stimuli. Psychotic symptoms have also occurred for periods of at least 2 weeks without accompanying mood symptoms. On examination, mild waxy flexibility is noted, with slight, consistent resistance to repositioning. She reports diminished capacity to experience pleasure in previously enjoyable activities. The overall clinical picture has resulted in clear and persistent impairment in daily functioning.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question:
A 52-year-old married male real estate developer presents with a 4-month history of recurrent episodes of markedly increased food intake. During these episodes, he consumes quantities of food clearly exceeding what most individuals would eat in a comparable time frame and situation, accompanied by a pronounced sense of diminished control over starting or stopping eating and over the amount consumed. 

He reports eating to the point of significant physical discomfort and continuing to eat in the absence of physiological hunger. Episodes are frequently conducted in isolation due to shame about the volume of food consumed, and he actively avoids eating in the presence of others during these times. The episodes occur, on average, at least once weekly over the past 3 months, sometimes more often, with intervening periods of relatively typical intake. 

There is no report of regular compensatory behaviors such as self-induced vomiting, laxative misuse, excessive exercise, or fasting aimed at preventing weight gain. The pattern of behavior is associated with notable distress and preoccupation with eating, but no acute medical instability is reported at this time.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Binge-Eating Disorder
    C. Bulimia Nervosa
    D. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
A 38-year-old male construction worker who is single presents with a chronic and persistent preoccupation regarding the appearance of his skin. He reports excessive concern over perceived defects in his facial skin, particularly related to minor acne lesions and faint scarring, which are not readily apparent to others. These concerns have persisted for several years and are associated with significant time consumption and psychological distress. He frequently engages in repetitive behaviors, including repeated questioning of coworkers and acquaintances to obtain reassurance about the appearance of his skin, often asking whether his face looks "normal" or "disfigured." Despite receiving affirmations, he remains unconvinced and often reinitiates the inquiry with others or returns to the same individuals for repeated validation.

The patient’s preoccupation and associated behaviors have led to clinically significant functional impairment. He reports difficulty maintaining consistent focus at work, avoids social interactions when he feels his skin appears worse, and has declined opportunities for advancement that involve increased visibility or interpersonal engagement. There is no evidence of other primary psychotic or mood disorders that better account for the clinical picture. Neurological examination is unremarkable, and cognitive screening reveals intact reality testing. The timeline of symptom onset clearly follows his being single by over a decade, indicating no proximal causal link to his current relationship status.
option:
A. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Body Dysmorphic Disorder
    D. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder

question:
A 52-year-old married female head nurse presents with a 2-year and 2-month history of persistent low mood and diminished vitality. She reports frequent episodes of crying that are difficult to control, often occurring in response to minor stressors or without clear provocation. Throughout this period, she has experienced a sustained reduction in energy, describing marked fatigue that impairs her ability to carry out both occupational duties and household responsibilities at her previous level of functioning.

The patient describes a long-standing pattern over this interval of viewing herself as less capable and less competent than colleagues and peers, with pervasive self-critical comparisons. She endorses a chronic, pessimistic outlook regarding her circumstances and future, characterized by a sense that her efforts are unlikely to result in meaningful improvement. This has contributed to reduced engagement in previously valued activities and diminished initiative at work and home.

Despite ongoing employment in a demanding role, these symptoms have led to significant subjective distress and functional impairment in social, occupational, and personal domains. There is no indication that her occupational or marital status initiated the current symptomatology.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Bipolar I Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Persistent Depressive Disorder

question:
A 38-year-old married male crane operator presents with a 2-week history of trauma-related symptoms following exposure to a traumatic event. Since the incident, the patient reports marked difficulty making even simple day-to-day choices, describing pronounced indecisiveness that has interfered with routine functioning at home and work.

He endorses recurrent, involuntary, and distressing recollections of the traumatic event, accompanied by conscious efforts to suppress or block related memories, thoughts, and emotions. He describes episodes of feeling detached from his body and mental processes, as if observing himself from outside, consistent with dissociative experiences.

The patient reports heightened physiological arousal, including pronounced tachycardia and tremulousness after being startled, with prolonged time required to regain baseline calm. There is a history of episodes of physical aggression since the trauma, including striking objects and aggressive acts toward others, representing a change from his prior baseline behavior.

These symptoms have been present for approximately 2 weeks and cause significant impairment in occupational functioning and marital/family relationships. No prior history suggests a similar symptom pattern before the traumatic event.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Acute Stress Disorder

question:
A 35-year-old single female PhD candidate presented with a 10-month history of recurrent episodes of excessive food consumption under non-fasting conditions. The episodes occur, on average, more than once per week and are consistently associated with a subjective sense of lack of control over eating behavior, including an inability to stop or regulate the quantity or type of food consumed during the episodes.  

During these discrete periods, the patient reports consuming a significantly larger amount of food than most individuals would under comparable circumstances. She eats markedly faster than normal, often continuing until she feels physically uncomfortable. These episodes frequently occur in the absence of subjective hunger and persist beyond caloric need. Due to intense embarrassment regarding the quantity consumed, she consistently eats in isolation, avoiding others during these episodes.  

The patient denies compensatory behaviors such as self-induced vomiting, excessive exercise, or misuse of laxatives. There is no evidence of another medical condition or concurrent psychiatric disorder that better accounts for the presentation. The behavior is not associated with regular use of inappropriate compensatory measures as seen in other eating disorders. The course has been persistent, with symptom stability over the past several months.
option:
A. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    B. Binge-Eating Disorder
    C. Specific Phobia
    D. Bulimia Nervosa

question: An 80-year-old retired, widowed male presents with an 8-month history of significant mood disturbance and functional impairment. He reports a pervasive sense of inner emptiness and a profound anhedonia, noting an inability to experience pleasure or positive emotions. He is troubled by recurrent distressing nightmares. The patient expresses persistent feelings of worthlessness and endorsed transient suicidal impulses, specifically urges to jump from high places or run into traffic, though he denies any concrete plan or intent. His symptoms have led to marked impairment in his daily functioning.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder
    B. Major Depressive Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)

question:
A 37-year-old married female pharmacist presents with a 1 year 11 month history of prominent attentional difficulties causing functional impairment at work and home. She reports a longstanding pattern of similar symptoms beginning in childhood, with onset clearly prior to age 12.

The patient describes frequent oversight of details and recurrent careless errors in professional and household tasks. She has persistent difficulty structuring and sequencing activities, leading to problems managing workload, deadlines, and routine responsibilities. Tasks requiring prolonged concentration are experienced as aversive, and she tends to delay or avoid activities that demand sustained mental effort, including documentation and complex planning.

She is readily drawn off task by external stimuli as well as by internally generated, unrelated thoughts, resulting in fragmented work sessions and incomplete activities. She is consistently forgetful of everyday obligations such as returning messages, paying bills on time, and keeping scheduled appointments, despite intact general cognition. Additionally, she tends to speak at length in conversations and professional interactions, sometimes exceeding the situational demands.

These symptoms collectively interfere with occupational performance, household management, and role functioning, despite the patient’s stable psychosocial circumstances and absence of recent major environmental stressors.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    D. Adjustment Disorder

question: A 28-year-old single female, working as a forensic science technician, presents with a three-week history of distressing symptoms following a traumatic event. The patient reports experiencing hypersomnia, characterized by prolonged sleep periods both nocturnally and diurnally. Concurrently, she describes a persistent state of heightened alertness and tension, even in secure environments, making relaxation difficult. She has also endorsed dissociative reactions, specifically flashbacks, where she re-experiences aspects of the traumatic event. Memory disturbance is noted, with fragmented recall of the event, wherein certain details are vivid while others are inaccessible. The patient reports a pervasive lack of satisfaction, even following positive achievements, and sudden episodes of impulsive anger lacking clear antecedents. These symptoms have resulted in significant functional impairment.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Acute Stress Disorder
    C. Bipolar I Disorder
    D. Persistent Depressive Disorder

question:
A 33-year-old single female flight attendant presents with a 15-month history of continuous psychiatric symptoms marked by a persistent manic episode and concurrent psychotic features. For the past 11 months, she has exhibited a sustained irritable mood, psychomotor hyperactivity, and markedly increased goal-directed activity, often initiating multiple projects without completion. She demonstrates pressured, rapid speech that others frequently find difficult to interrupt or follow. There is a significant reduction in sleep need, with periods of only 2–3 hours of sleep per night without reported fatigue. Her self-appraisal includes marked inflation of self-confidence, with beliefs of exceptional abilities and invulnerability to failure.

Concurrent with these mood symptoms, and persisting for 10 months, she experiences persistent psychotic symptoms including fixed delusions of partner infidelity despite lack of evidence, and recurrent gustatory hallucinations involving tastes not linked to actual stimuli. These hallucinations occur both during periods of mood disturbance and for over two weeks in the absence of prominent manic symptoms. Spontaneous speech is notably diminished at times, with reduced output and poverty of content consistent with alogia. The overall course has led to marked functional decline, affecting occupational performance and interpersonal relationships. Psychotic features and mood symptoms have overlapped extensively, with the manic presentation dominating the majority of the illness course.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Schizophrenia
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question:
A 30-year-old married female wedding photographer presents with a 1-month history of behavioral changes temporally associated with an identifiable psychosocial stressor. Symptoms began within 3 months of the onset of this stressor. There is no history suggesting prior similar episodes related to earlier life events.

The clinical picture is characterized by disturbances in conduct, including recurrent engagement in rule-breaking behaviors, episodes of reckless behavior such as unsafe driving, and involvement in confrontational situations with risk of physical altercations. There have been instances of property-related misbehavior suggestive of vandalism and a disregard for customary social and legal boundaries. These behaviors represent a clear deviation from her premorbid functioning.

The disturbance is associated with significant functional impairment, including compromised reliability and performance in her occupational role, increased interpersonal conflict within close relationships, and difficulty fulfilling usual social and household responsibilities. There is no evidence that the symptom pattern is better explained by a chronic mood, psychotic, or neurocognitive disorder. The symptoms are expected to resolve within 6 months after the cessation of the stressor or its consequences.
option:
A. Adjustment Disorder
    B. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    C. Acute Stress Disorder
    D. Bipolar I Disorder

question: A 30-year-old single male, employed as an oil rig worker, presents with a three-year history of persistent emotional blunting. The patient reports experiencing a diminished capacity for emotional response, accompanied by chronic early morning awakenings. He describes significant functional impairment due to a pervasive reduction in concentration, impacting his daily tasks and interactions. Additionally, he endorses persistent feelings of worthlessness and inferiority.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Bipolar I Disorder

question:
A 46-year-old male, married, working as a truck driver, presents with a history of recurrent mood disturbances. For a period of three weeks, the patient exhibited increased energy, heightened talkativeness, and a reduced need for sleep without associated fatigue. During this time, he demonstrated elevated self-assurance, bordering on grandiosity, with beliefs of superior competence in personal and professional domains. His mood was notably irritable, with reactivity to minor provocation, and he engaged in increased social outreach, initiating contact with acquaintances and participating in conversations with strangers while on work routes. These behaviors represented a noticeable change from his typical demeanor but did not result in marked functional decline or require hospitalization.

This episode was followed by a depressive period lasting approximately three months, characterized by emotional blunting, early morning awakening without ability to return to sleep, and a persistent increase in appetite. The patient exhibited psychomotor agitation, manifesting as restlessness, an inability to remain seated, and repetitive movements such as hand-wringing. He reported significant indecisiveness, struggling even with routine choices regarding meals or clothing, which impaired his occupational performance. There was no reported substance use or medical illness accounting for the symptom pattern. The temporal course and symptom profile are consistent with distinct episodic mood shifts.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Bipolar II Disorder
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question:
A 54-year-old married male warehouse manager presents with a 1 year and 5 month history of mood and psychotic symptoms associated with significant functional decline at work and home. For approximately 1 year and 2 months, he has experienced a persistent depressive episode characterized by frequent crying spells, marked withdrawal from family and social activities, unintentional weight loss exceeding 5% over one month, and noticeable psychomotor agitation including restlessness and difficulty remaining seated. He reports subjective decline in memory, particularly misplacing objects and difficulty concentrating, and recurrent thoughts of death and self-harm without a formulated plan.

Over the past 1 year and 1 month, he has demonstrated disorganized thought processes with tangential responses that do not address questions directly, and observable mannerisms that represent exaggerated, idiosyncratic versions of ordinary gestures. He also reports fixed somatic beliefs of having a serious bodily disease despite lack of corroborating medical findings. Psychotic symptoms have been present concurrently with the depressive episode, and there has been a period of at least 2 weeks of persistent delusional symptoms in the absence of a full depressive episode. Mood symptoms have occupied the majority of the illness course.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Major Depressive Disorder
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question:
"It's been... well, over a year now, maybe a year and four months if I'm being precise. Things started changing subtly, but now it's undeniable. I've always been a bright student, very dedicated to my research, but lately, it's like I've tapped into something more. I feel like I'm on the cusp of a major breakthrough, something that will revolutionize my field and perhaps even reshape our understanding of the universe. I know it sounds grand, but I truly believe I possess a unique insight, a different way of seeing connections that others miss. It's not a hallucination, nothing like that. It's just... a profound knowing.

But it’s also made things a bit complicated. Group projects, for example, are frustrating because I feel like I'm always holding back, waiting for others to catch up to my thought process. They just don't grasp the magnitude of what I'm working on. It hasn't derailed my studies – my grades are still good, I'm attending classes – but I do find myself a bit socially isolated. It's hard to connect with people when you feel like you're operating on a completely different intellectual plane. It sometimes feels like the world is moving too slowly for me."
option:
A. Schizophrenia
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    C. Delusional Disorder
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)

question:
It’s been a little over a year now that I’ve felt this way. I wake up every day with this heavy, sunken feeling, like there’s no point to anything. I’m tired all the time, even if I sleep through the night. Just getting dressed or making breakfast feels exhausting, so I often just sit in my chair and stare at the television without really watching it. I used to enjoy gardening and going to church groups, but none of that seems worth the effort anymore.

For the last 11 months or so, I’ve been convinced that everything is coming to an end. Not just my life—everything. I feel certain that the world is collapsing, that some terrible disaster is already underway and we’re just waiting for the final blow. Sometimes, even when I’m not especially low, that thought is still there, like a fact I can’t shake.

There are moments when I get a sudden urge to step in front of a bus or throw myself down the stairs. I don’t plan it out, but the thought rushes in and feels strangely tempting. I live alone since my husband died, and it’s getting harder to manage the house and even basic chores because of all this.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder
    B. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question:
I’ve been having more trouble these past seven months, though honestly I’ve always been a bit this way, even as a boy in school. Teachers used to scold me for fidgeting and talking out of turn.  

Lately it’s gotten harder to keep things straight. I start paying a bill, then get up to make tea, and the envelope ends up under a newspaper. I misplace my glasses, my keys, even my pillbox several times a week. I’ll promise my daughter I’ll call her back, then only remember two days later. I’ve missed a couple of appointments because I got distracted puttering around the house.  

At the senior center, I can’t seem to sit through the whole card game — I stand up, wander to the window, then come back. When the men are playing chess or doing puzzles, I talk too much or tap my fingers; it’s hard for me to do anything “quietly.” In line at the pharmacy or waiting at the bank, I get restless and impatient, cutting ahead accidentally in conversations because I can’t wait for my turn.
option:
A. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)

question:
It’s been about seven months now where it feels like someone turned the volume down on my emotions. I used to get excited about new gigs or nailing a character, but now even landing a decent contract just feels…flat. I’m not crying all the time or anything, I just feel kind of blank, like I’m watching my life instead of living it.

Sleep doesn’t help. I can be in bed eight, nine hours and still wake up feeling like I pulled an all-nighter in the studio. My body feels weighed down, like I’m moving through water. Even setting up my mic or editing a few takes wipes me out, so I’ve been missing deadlines and turning down jobs.

There’s also this thing I’m ashamed to talk about. I’m not even dating anyone seriously right now, but I keep getting these fixed ideas that whoever I’m talking to is lying to me, secretly seeing someone else. I’ll reread texts for hidden meanings, stalk their socials, and I can’t shake the certainty they’re cheating, even when I know it doesn’t make sense. This all really started together with the low mood and exhaustion and hasn’t let up since.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
answer: B. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
I’ve been feeling… empty for about four months now. Not sad exactly—just numb, like I’m underwater and the world is muffled. It’s hard to care about anything, even the exhibition I’ve been planning for years. I used to find peace in the quiet of the galleries, but now I can’t even stand to be there. I keep thinking I’ve ruined everything—not just at work, but… bigger things. Like that storm last month that flooded the riverfront? I know it’s irrational, but I can’t shake the feeling it was my fault. That I somehow deserved it. That *I* caused it.

And my fiancé—well, we’re not really speaking. I know he’s cheating on me. I can *taste* it—bitter, like metal—every time I think about him. It’s always there on my tongue, even when I haven’t eaten. I tell myself he’s with someone else, that they’re laughing at me while I sit alone in the dark.

I’ve thought about ending it. There’s a spot near the old train bridge—if I time it right, no one would even see me. It feels like the only way to stop the weight. I can’t work, can’t sleep, can’t eat. I used to love arranging the displays, making sense of history. Now I can’t make sense of anything.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
answer: A. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

question: For the past two years and some change, it’s like I’ve been running on empty. My wife notices it too. Nothing really gets to me anymore, good or bad. It’s not like I’m sad, just… flat. Even at the shop, I used to enjoy the puzzle of a broken engine, but now it’s just another job. I’ve barely been eating; food just doesn't sound good, which is weird for me. And don't even get me started on making decisions. Picking out what to wear in the morning, or what to have for dinner, feels like moving mountains. It’s gotten so bad that sometimes I just stand there in the grocery aisle, completely lost. It’s making everything harder, at work and at home.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    C. Bipolar I Disorder
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question:
I’ve been feeling down for as long as I can remember—like, seriously, it’s been over two years now. At first, I thought it was just grad school stress. But even when things aren’t overwhelming, I still wake up with this heavy sadness, like a fog I can’t shake. It’s not that I burst into tears all the time, but nothing really feels joyful, you know? Just… flat.  

I’m constantly tired, even after a full night’s sleep. Walking to class feels exhausting, and I’ll sit at my desk, staring at my laptop, unable to start writing—even small tasks take forever. My research feels pointless most days, and I keep falling behind. I’ve always been a good student, but now I’m barely keeping up, and I’m scared I’ll fail out.  

I used to love painting and going out with friends, but I canceled on everyone months ago. It’s not that I don’t care—I just don’t have the energy. I isolate, stay in my apartment, scroll mindlessly. I eat out of habit, not hunger. My advisor noticed I’ve been missing deadlines and asked if I was okay. Honestly? I don’t know if “okay” exists for me anymore.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    D. Bipolar I Disorder

question:
I’ve always been a bit high-energy, but over the last year or so—maybe 14 months—it feels like it’s gone into overdrive. At work, I can’t sit through investment committee meetings. I’ll start in my chair, then I’m up pacing at the back of the room or stepping out “to take a call” when really I just can’t stand sitting still. My leg is bouncing constantly; my colleagues joke about it, but it’s not funny to me anymore.

I struggle with waiting for my turn to speak. In client pitches I’ll cut people off, jump in over my team, or blurt out ideas before I’ve thought them through. My wife says I do the same thing at home—talk over her, interrupt, rush ahead in conversations.

It’s starting to affect my performance: I miss parts of discussions because I’m too restless, and I make quick decisions without fully hearing everyone out. I’ve been like this, in some form, since I was a kid—teachers used to move me to the back of the classroom because I couldn’t stay in my seat—but lately it’s really interfering with my work and my marriage.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    D. Bipolar II Disorder

question:
Honestly, I’ve always been kind of “high energy,” even as a kid—I was the one who couldn’t sit still in class—but over the last almost two years it’s been getting in the way of my work and life.

I sit in front of multiple monitors most of the day, and I’m constantly bouncing my leg, tapping the desk, spinning my chair. Half the time I don’t even notice I’m doing it until someone comments in a meeting. Even in longer briefings, I get this really uncomfortable, pent‑up feeling, like I need to move or get up and pace, and it takes a lot of effort not to just walk out for a few minutes.

I also cut people off a lot. On calls with clients or my team, I’ll jump in before they’ve finished because I already think I know where they’re going, or I get excited about an idea and blurt it out. I’ve been told I “steamroll” conversations. It’s starting to cause friction at work and socially, and I’m worried because I can’t seem to dial it back, even though I’m aware of it.
option:
A. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)

question:
I’ve just been completely overwhelmed since my mom passed away three months ago. I know everyone grieves differently, but it’s like I can’t function like I used to. I’m a wedding planner, so my job is all about details, staying calm under pressure, and being present for clients—and right now, I’m failing at all of it. I keep missing small things—deadlines, emails, even appointments—and that’s not me at all. Last week, I burst into tears during a venue walkthrough. The bride was confused, and I felt so ashamed.  

I’ve always been close to my mom, and losing her so suddenly has left me numb most days, and then out of nowhere, I’ll feel this wave of panic, like I can’t catch my breath. I’ve stopped going to coffee with friends, and my husband says I’m distant, like I’m somewhere else. I don’t even want to plan anything anymore, and that scares me because my whole identity feels tied to being organized, capable.  

Even little decisions—like what to have for dinner—feel impossible. Some days I stay in bed until noon. It’s only been a few months, but it’s like I’m not moving forward. I thought I’d be adjusting by now, not falling apart.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    B. Adjustment Disorder
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question:
I’ve been struggling for years, but it’s gotten much worse lately—probably the last five years or so. As a journalist, I’m often on camera or meeting sources, and I can’t stop fixating on how my face looks. The left side of my jaw feels... off. It sticks out more than the right, or maybe it’s smaller—I don’t even know anymore. I check it constantly in reflections, windows, mirrors, anything.  

I spend at least an hour each morning applying makeup, trying to contour it so it looks balanced. I always wear my hair a certain way, partly to cover the side I hate. I bring a hat and oversized sunglasses to work, even on cloudy days, just in case there’s an unexpected shoot. My husband has said, “No one notices that,” but I can’t help it—I *know* they do.  

It’s exhausting. I’ve turned down interviews, avoided team meetings, and once skipped a major conference because I was convinced everyone would stare at my face. I know it sounds irrational, but the thought of being seen as I really am… it makes me feel nauseous. I’ve even considered surgery, but I’m terrified it still wouldn’t be “right.”
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder
    B. Anorexia Nervosa
    C. Body Dysmorphic Disorder
    D. Binge-Eating Disorder

question:
I guess the main thing is how out of control I feel around food. It started maybe a year and a bit ago—about 14, 15 months. I’ll come home after my shift at the plant, and it’s like a switch flips. I’ll stand in the kitchen and just go through everything—leftovers, bread, cereal, whatever I can find—way more than anyone would normally eat for a meal. I keep telling myself “just one more bite,” but I don’t stop until I feel sick. It’s like I’m watching myself do it and can’t hit the brakes.

Then I feel disgusted with myself, so I force myself to “fix it.” I’ll get on the treadmill in the garage or do laps around the neighborhood at night, pushing way past what feels safe—knees aching, sweat pouring—but I won’t stop because I’m terrified of gaining weight.

I weigh myself multiple times a day. If the number is even half a pound higher, my chest tightens, and I can’t focus at work. This whole cycle’s been happening at least once, usually a few times a week for months now, and I’m exhausted by it.
option:
A. Anorexia Nervosa
    B. Bipolar I Disorder
    C. Bulimia Nervosa
    D. Binge-Eating Disorder

question: I'm here because... well, I just haven't been myself for about ten months now. I find myself tearing up at the drop of a hat, even during commercials, which is just not like me. I used to be so active, especially after retiring from nursing. Now, I'm just bone-tired all the time, no matter how much rest I get. My daughter calls, and I let it ring. My friends from the garden club keep asking me to come out, but honestly, the thought of small talk just feels like too much effort. And reading? Forget about it. I used to devour books, but now I can't even follow a simple magazine article. My mind just drifts off. It’s hard to focus on anything, even during a conversation, and it’s become quite upsetting.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question:
I’ve been feeling… off… for about two years now, maybe a little more. Work used to be my safe place—grooming dogs, brushing them down, making them comfortable—but lately, I can’t even stand being touched by a pet. I feel things crawling on my skin, like bugs or fingers, but when I look, nothing’s there. Drives me nuts. I’ll scratch until it hurts. 

There are times when I’m racing—talking so fast my coworkers say I don’t make sense, like my mind’s got its own engine. I’ll take on five dogs at once, even if it’s chaos. I feel invincible, then suddenly, I’m furious at nothing. One minute I’m laughing, the next I’m screaming inside. 

I keep thinking this client—this woman who brings in her poodle—she’s in love with me. She smiles, and I know she means something more. But I don’t talk to anyone else. I stopped going out. Hanging up flyers or chatting with coworkers feels pointless. 

Even when I’m not bouncing with energy, the feelings on my skin… the thoughts about her… they don’t go away. It’s been like this, on and off, for over two years. I don’t feel like myself ever.
option:
A. Schizophrenia
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question:
It’s been about a week since the accident at the restaurant, when that gas line blew and the hood went up in flames. Nobody died, but I keep replaying it in my head like it’s still happening. 

Since then, I feel like I’m moving through a haze. On the line I used to be sharp, juggling ten tickets at once; now my head feels stuffed with cotton. I stare at the order screen and can’t remember what I was about to do next. My sous chef has had to jump in and cover for me more than once.

I’m constantly scanning the room. I keep checking the gas knobs, the pilot lights, the fire exits—over and over, even when I just checked them. If a pan drops or someone slams the walk-in door, I physically jerk, like my whole body flinches before I can think. The guys on the line joke that I’m “jumpier than a cat,” but it’s messing with my work. I’m slower, distracted, and honestly I’m starting to dread going in for service.
option:
A. Bipolar II Disorder
    B. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Acute Stress Disorder
    D. Bipolar I Disorder

question: "Well, doc, it's been a rough few months, maybe half a year or so. I'm a bus driver, married, 41, and lately, my head just isn't right. I'm seeing things, thoughts, really disturbing stuff. Like, when I'm driving, I'll sometimes imagine swerving into oncoming traffic. Or, and this is really tough to admit, but sometimes I'll look at a passenger and have these awful, violent thoughts. I’d never act on them, of course, but they just pop into my head, uninvited. It's like my brain is playing tricks on me. These thoughts, they just replay and replay, and it's exhausting trying to push them away. It's really getting in the way of my job, and my wife's noticed I'm not myself. I'm always on edge, can't concentrate. It's like I'm constantly fighting with my own mind."
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    B. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)

question:
I’ve been this way for about three or four years now, but it’s been getting worse lately. I drive long hauls most weeks, ten to twelve hours a day, but I can’t seem to make it through a shift without pulling over. Every time I touch the fuel pump, the restroom door, or even my own lunch from the cooler, I feel it—like germs are just crawling on my skin. I know it sounds crazy, but I can’t shake the feeling that if I don’t clean it off, I’ll get really sick… or worse, bring something home to my wife.

So I wash. A lot. I keep travel-sized soap, wipes, even a little bottle of bleach solution in my cab. If I don’t wash my hands after the diner counter, I start thinking about dead people, or sewage, or infections spreading through our house. I’ll pull over at a truck stop just to scrub up—even if I just did it ten minutes ago. Showers? I spend twenty, thirty minutes in there each night at a motel, washing the same parts over and over until my skin’s raw. It’s messing with my schedule, and my wife… she doesn’t understand why I won’t let her cook or why I disinfect every grocery bag. But if I don’t do it, the thoughts just get louder, and I can’t focus on the road.
option:
A. Body Dysmorphic Disorder
    B. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    C. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    D. Acute Stress Disorder

question:
I’ve not felt like myself for a little over a year now. It started with this…charge in me, I suppose. I’m on edge all the time. The smallest thing—like the kettle whistling too long—sets me off snapping at my daughter on the phone. I can’t seem to sit still. I’ll start sorting old photos, then halfway through I’m scrubbing the kitchen floor, then I’m out in the garden at dusk pulling weeds, none of it actually finished. This has been going on most days for about ten months, and I’ve stopped going to my seniors’ group because I can’t focus or tolerate people.

Around eight months ago, I began seeing things. Sometimes, late afternoon, I see a man in a dark coat standing in the hallway or by the garden gate, but when I look properly, he’s gone. I’ve also become convinced that a gentleman from the church—much younger than me—has special feelings for me. I see “messages” in the hymns he chooses or how he looks in my direction, even when my daughter insists I’m reading too much into it. Even when my mood settles a bit, that belief and the figures I see can linger for weeks.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Schizophrenia
