question: "I'm here because… well, I just feel like I'm running on empty. It's been going on for over two years now, maybe two and a half. It's not a sadness like grief, just… an emptiness inside. Like a hollow space where my emotions used to be. Every day feels like a slog, especially at work. Even simple decisions that I used to make quickly, now they feel like climbing a mountain. My brain just feels tired, you know? Like I've been running a marathon even when I've just been sitting in a meeting. It's hard to concentrate, to keep all the plates spinning that a principal needs to. My husband has noticed I'm just not myself, and honestly, it’s impacting everything. It’s not just a bad mood; it's like a constant dull ache that never goes away."
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    D. Bipolar I Disorder

question:
It’s been about two years now where I feel like I’m constantly “on.” My mind just doesn’t switch off. I’m always running through worst-case scenarios—about money, my kids, my job at the bank, my parents’ health—things that haven’t even happened. I get stuck in these “what if” loops: what if I made a mistake on someone’s account, what if my husband loses his job, what if something happens to the kids on the way to school. Even when things are fine, it feels like I’m waiting for something to go wrong.

At work, every little noise makes me jump—the printer, someone dropping a pen. I feel wound up and shaky inside most days. By the time I get home, my whole body aches—my shoulders, back, even my legs feel tight and tired, like I’ve been tensing all day without realizing it.

Nights are the worst. I lie in bed, exhausted, but my brain keeps replaying the day or planning for tomorrow. It can take hours to finally fall asleep, and I dread going to bed because I know I’ll just be lying there, thinking.
option:
A. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    B. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    C. Adjustment Disorder
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question:
It’s a little embarrassing to talk about, especially at my age, but this has been going on for about a year now. Since my husband passed, I find myself eating in a way that doesn’t feel normal. 

In the evenings, especially when I’m alone watching television or after I’ve had a hard day missing him, I’ll go into the kitchen and just…keep going. I might finish a whole loaf of bread with butter, then cookies, then whatever leftovers are in the fridge, all in an hour or so. I’m not even really hungry, and once I start, it feels like I can’t stop myself, even though I’m telling myself to put it away. 

This happens at least once a week, sometimes more. I usually do it when no one’s around because I’m ashamed. Afterward I feel awful—physically too full and uncomfortable, but also disgusted with myself and very low, like I’ve failed somehow. Then I promise myself I won’t do it again, but it keeps happening.
option:
A. Bulimia Nervosa
    B. Binge-Eating Disorder
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    D. Body Dysmorphic Disorder

question:
It’s been about four months now where I’ve just felt…flat or worse. I cry a lot, sometimes over nothing specific—like I’ll just be reading an email from my advisor and suddenly I’m in tears. Things I used to enjoy—my research, seeing friends, even just watching stupid shows—feel like they’re behind glass. I go through the motions, but I don’t feel anything good from it.

I keep replaying small mistakes in my head, like missing a deadline or not answering an email right away, and I feel like I’ve ruined everything. I tell myself I’m a burden, that I don’t deserve to be here. I have these thoughts about not wanting to exist anymore, like it’d be easier if I just didn’t wake up, but I haven’t made any plans.

My concentration is terrible. I reread the same paragraph over and over and nothing sticks, so my coursework and thesis have really suffered. 

Before this, there was this three‑week period where I barely slept, felt weirdly “on,” jumping from idea to idea, talking fast, starting projects I never finished. My mood would swing from super excited to snapping at people for no reason, but I was still going to class and getting things done.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder
    B. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    D. Bipolar II Disorder

question:
I’ve always been a bit absent‑minded, even as a kid—I was the one staring out the classroom window—but over the last year and a half, almost two years now, it’s gotten bad enough that it’s affecting my job and my marriage.

At work, I’ll be in a meeting and my team is talking directly to me, and halfway through I realize I’ve got no idea what they just said. It’s like my ears work but my brain checked out. When I have to go through long reports or write up incident summaries, I just sit there, staring at the screen, putting it off because it feels exhausting to hold my focus that long. I find myself inventing excuses to walk the line instead of doing the paperwork.

My mind drifts constantly. I’ll be checking a batch, then some random thought pops in—something at home, a bill, some song—and suddenly I’ve lost my place. My wife complains I do the same thing with her: she’ll be talking and I zone out, then ask something she literally just told me. It’s causing arguments, and I’m starting to worry I’m going to miss something serious at work.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Bipolar I Disorder
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)

question:
I’ve always struggled with focusing, even as far back as elementary school—I’d sit at my desk, hear the teacher talking, but then suddenly realize I hadn’t absorbed a word. That still happens now. My editor will explain a revision over the phone, and I’ll nod along, only to get off the call and think, *Wait, what exactly did she say?* 

Over the past year and a half, it’s gotten harder to keep up with deadlines. I love writing, but sitting down to draft an article feels overwhelming. Once I start, I’ll get distracted—checking emails, then scrolling, then remembering I left something in the other room—only to realize I’ve lost my notebook again. I swear I had it this morning. It’s usually my keys or my phone or a manuscript draft. Last week I turned my apartment upside down looking for a client contract.

I work from home, and being alone means no one’s there to nudge me back on track. I want to do good work, but I’ll open a document and end up rewriting the same paragraph for hours or just shut the laptop and take a nap. I’ve started avoiding projects that require deep focus, which is almost all of them. It’s frustrating because I know I’m capable—I just can’t seem to *stay* on task, no matter how hard I try.
option:
A. Acute Stress Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)

question:
"It's been... well, almost a year and a half, I guess, since things started feeling really off. About eleven months ago, I started hearing things – whispers at first, then full-on voices telling me I'm destined for something big, something beyond just being an EMT. Like, I’m the only one who truly understands the universe’s rhythm, you know? Sometimes I even make up words for these complex ideas because no existing words fit. People at work sometimes say my face is flat, like I’m not reacting, even when I feel a lot inside.

Then, around ten months ago, everything just kicked into overdrive. My mood swings are wild – one minute I’m buzzing, like I could save the whole city by myself, bursting with energy I can barely contain, always on the go. The next, I'm snapping at everyone. My sex drive went through the roof too; it was a lot. That manic energy really messed with my shifts, made it tough to focus when I needed to.

More recently, for about four months now, these crashes hit hard. I just can't get myself to do anything, even simple tasks. Waking up at 3 AM and just staring at the ceiling, feeling this crushing guilt over... everything, even small things that aren't my fault. My mind feels so sluggish, I can barely string a sentence together when people ask me something. The voices and these huge feelings about my ‘destiny’ are still there, even when I’m not in one of those high or low periods."
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Bipolar I Disorder
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question: "Honestly, doctor, I'm at my wit's end. This started about a week ago, right after that huge deal with the Thompson account fell through. I poured everything into that, months of work, and now... it's just gone. I find myself snapping at my team for the smallest things, which is just not me. Then, a few hours later, I'm holed up in my office, close to tears, feeling completely overwhelmed and like a total failure. I've been sleeping terribly, either staring at the ceiling for hours or waking up super early with my mind racing about everything I could have done differently. It's affecting everything – my focus is shot, I'm constantly worried about the next thing to go wrong, and honestly, I just don't feel like myself."
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder
    B. Adjustment Disorder
    C. Bulimia Nervosa
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)

question:
I sell cars for a living, so I’m around people all day and usually I’m fine with that. The problem is this… spiders. It sounds stupid even saying it out loud, but for a little over two years now—about two years and a couple of months—it’s gotten completely out of hand.

If I see a spider, even a tiny one, I freeze. My chest tightens, my heart starts banging like I’ve sprinted, my hands sweat, and I just need to get away. It happens instantly, like someone flipped a switch. I can’t think straight; all I can focus on is getting out of the room. I’ve had to walk out of the showroom in the middle of talking to customers because I spotted one on the wall outside the glass.

At home it’s worse. I won’t go into the basement or the garage unless I absolutely have to. I’ve stopped taking my kid camping or to the lake because I’m terrified I’ll run into spiders and lose it in front of him. I know it’s not rational, but I can’t control it, and it’s really messing with my work and my life.
option:
A. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    B. Specific Phobia
    C. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    D. Generalized Anxiety Disorder

question:
I’ve been having trouble keeping my mind on what’s in front of me, especially at work. I’m a security guard—been doing it for nearly twenty years—and things used to come easier. Now, during shift briefings, my wife says I stare right through her when she’s talking, and I’ve caught myself zoning out even when someone’s giving me important instructions. It’s not that I don’t care; I just… drift.

For the past, oh, almost two years now, I’ve found it harder to focus on reports or filling out incident logs. Anything that takes steady thinking feels like wading through mud. I started avoiding those tasks, putting them off until the last minute or asking a coworker to help. I know it doesn’t look good.

Outside noises bother me more than they used to—a siren down the street, the hum of a vending machine—and my thoughts jump around, like I’ll start remembering something from years ago while I’m supposed to be monitoring the cameras. My wife jokes I’ve always been forgetful, but she also says I’ve been this way since our kids were little, maybe even when I was a boy. School was tough; I could never sit still with my homework. Now it’s affecting my job, and that worries me.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Bipolar I Disorder
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)

question:
I’ve not been myself for over a year now—maybe closer to sixteen months. Lately, I just stay in my room. I don’t want to join the others for meals or bingo. They don’t understand. They *watch* me, you know. The staff, some of the residents—they whisper, move things in my drawer, leave notes I never wrote. I can feel the cameras, even though they say there aren’t any.  

I’m tired all the time. Just lifting my arms feels like carrying bricks. I used to knit, write letters to my granddaughter, but now I can’t even get out of bed most days. I cry for no reason. I feel… gone. What’s the point now that my John’s gone and my mind’s failing me?  

Some nights, I lie there and think how easy it would be to take the pills they leave for my “rest,” all at once, when the shift changes at night. I’ve thought about writing a note, making sure someone finds me before the pills stop my heart.  

Even when I’m not so low, those fears don’t leave. Sometimes, I’m not sad—just scared. Scared they’re watching, plotting. That’s been there, steady, for almost a year now. I don’t trust anyone here. No one sees what I see.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder
    B. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question:
I’ve been struggling for about five months now, and it’s starting to really get to me. I know my wife thinks I’m fixating, but I can feel it—something’s seriously wrong inside my body. Doctors don’t see it, but I *know* my organs are rotting. It started with a dull sensation in my abdomen, like things are shrinking or decaying. I don’t have pain exactly, but a constant awareness, like my body's betraying me. I’ve had every scan you can do—ultrasounds, blood work, even an MRI—and they all come back “normal.” But how can that be? I *feel* the damage.  

Since I lost my job at the warehouse, I’ve had more time to think, maybe too much. I used to stay busy, but now I’m researching late into the night, looking up rare conditions no one’s ever heard of. My wife says I’m worrying myself sick, but she doesn’t live in my body. I still manage the house, help with chores, pay the bills—nothing’s fallen apart. But I can’t shake this certainty: something’s terribly wrong inside me, and if I don’t figure it out soon, it’ll be too late.
option:
A. Schizophrenia
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    D. Delusional Disorder

question:
It started a little over a year ago. At first I just felt “on edge,” but for the past 10 months I’ve been constantly wound up and snappy. The smallest thing sets me off—customers asking for a discount, my staff misplacing an earring—and I’ll explode. I can’t sit still; even at home I’m pacing, rearranging the display trays in my head, getting up at night to “fix” things in the shop. I jump from one task to another and can’t stick with anything for more than a few minutes, so bookkeeping and orders have become a mess. My husband says I talk non‑stop and won’t let things go.

For about 8 months I’ve been absolutely sure he’s seeing someone else. If he’s late from work or glances at his phone, I’m convinced he’s lying. I’ve checked his messages, followed him once, but I still feel certain something’s going on, even though I haven’t found proof. Around the same time, I began feeling like something is crawling or brushing on my arms and legs, especially at night or when I’m alone in the shop. I’ll swat at my skin, but there’s nothing there. Even during a brief period when my mood was more “normal,” those feelings and suspicions didn’t go away.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Schizophrenia

question:
I guess the main thing is this constant, low‑level panic in the background. It’s been going on for about 11 months now. I’ll make some tiny mistake at work—like mislabel a drawing revision—and my brain instantly goes to, “They’ll ship the wrong part, there’ll be an accident, I’ll get fired, nobody will hire me again.” It sounds irrational when I say it out loud, but in the moment it feels inevitable.

Mornings are the worst. I keep waking up around 4 or 5 a.m. and then I’m just…on. My mind starts racing through every possible thing that could go wrong with the projects I’m on, bills, my car, my parents’ health. I can’t fall back asleep, I just lie there replaying scenarios.

Physically, I feel like I’m bracing for an impact that never comes. My shoulders, neck, even my jaw are tight most of the time. Sometimes my hands are clenched without me noticing. I try stretching, deep breathing, even going to the gym, but I never really feel “off duty.” It’s like I can’t find the switch to relax, even when I’m just supposed to be hanging out or watching a game.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder
    B. Specific Phobia
    C. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)

question:
I’ve been struggling with my eating for about the last five months—probably worse in the past few. It’s not that I’m always hungry, but there are these moments, usually late at night after dinner when the house is quiet, where I just… lose it. I’ll go to the kitchen and start pulling out whatever’s there—chips, bread, leftovers, sometimes straight out of the freezer. I’ll eat through half a family-sized bag of snacks without even tasting it. The worst part is the speed; I realize I’m practically shoveling food in, faster than normal, like I’m trying to beat a clock.

I keep eating even when I’m clearly full—way past full, actually. My stomach hurts sometimes, and I think, *Okay, stop now*, but I can’t. That’s the scary part—the feeling like I’ve flipped a switch and can’t turn it off. It’s not about the food, really. I’m married, I’ve got a stable job, no major stressors, but this keeps happening. I’ve started hiding wrappers in the garage so my wife won’t notice. It happens at least once a week now, sometimes more. I don’t know why I can’t control it.
option:
A. Adjustment Disorder
    B. Bulimia Nervosa
    C. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Binge-Eating Disorder

question:
I’ve just felt… heavy… for about five months now. It started around winter semester—I was supposed to teach two courses, but I couldn’t get out of bed most mornings. Just getting dressed takes so much effort, like my body’s made of concrete. I walk slower, talk slower. My wife noticed I keep staring into space during dinner.  

The worst part is this constant weight of guilt. I keep thinking that if I’d just been a better person—better father, better professor—then maybe that student last semester wouldn’t have dropped out. Or that my colleague wouldn’t have had that accident driving home after our meeting. I know it doesn’t make sense, but I can’t shake it—like I’m somehow the reason bad things happen.  

Some days, when I’m on the third floor of the engineering building, I get this sudden urge to step over the railing. It’s not that I want to die, not really—it's more like my body tenses up, and for a split second, I feel pulled toward the edge. Then I snap back and hold the railing tighter.  

I haven’t told anyone until now. I used to take pride in my work—now I can’t focus long enough to grade a quiz. My wife asks if I’m okay, but I don’t even know how to answer.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Major Depressive Disorder
    C. Bipolar I Disorder
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)

question:
It’s a little embarrassing to talk about, because it sounds silly, but it’s really taken over my life.

A bit over two years ago, I had this incident at the restaurant where a customer started choking. We managed to help him, but since then I’ve developed this intense fear of choking myself. It’s gotten worse over the last two years and a couple of months. Whenever I eat anything that’s not very soft, I get this rush of panic, like the food is going to get stuck in my throat. Sometimes I just sit there, fork in hand, completely frozen, unable to take a bite. My body literally locks up.

At home my husband teases me gently about cutting everything into tiny pieces, but I avoid eating with my staff or customers now. I make excuses to skip tastings with the chef, which is ridiculous given that I own the place. I’ll pretend I already ate or say I’m not feeling well.

I know it’s irrational, but in the moment it feels like I could die, and I just can’t get myself to move.
option:
A. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    B. Specific Phobia
    C. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    D. Generalized Anxiety Disorder

question:
"It's been... almost a year now, I guess, since things started feeling off. I'm a grad student, you know, always buried in books and research. But lately, it's like every little thing points back to me. When I'm in the library and I hear snippets of conversation, it's like they're talking *about* me, even if they're across the room. Or when someone clears their throat in a lecture hall, I immediately think it's a reaction to something I did, or just thought.

Even online, if I see a random comment or a post, my mind just goes straight to, 'Oh, that's definitely directed at me.' It’s exhausting, really. I can still go to my classes, do my research – my grades haven't slipped or anything. I'm not, like, falling apart. It's just this constant, gnawing feeling that everything happening around me is somehow centered on *me*."
option:
A. Delusional Disorder
    B. Schizophrenia
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)

question:
I guess the main thing is my eating’s gotten really out of control these past eight months. It’s not just “snacking.” I’ll get back to my hotel after a long flight and suddenly it’s like a switch flips. I’ll order room service, then grab stuff from the vending machine, and just keep going—burgers, fries, chocolate, chips—way more than any of my crew would normally eat. I’ll eat so fast I barely taste it, and I don’t really stop until my stomach hurts and I have to lie down because I feel so stuffed it’s painful.

What’s weird is I’m usually not even hungry when it starts. Sometimes I’ve already had a normal meal with the crew, but as soon as I’m alone, I’ll start again. It’s been happening at least once a week, usually more, and I feel really ashamed afterwards. I hide the wrappers, don’t tell anyone, and then the next day I’ll try to “make up for it” by skipping meals on the plane, but it just ends up happening again.
option:
A. Adjustment Disorder
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Bulimia Nervosa
    D. Binge-Eating Disorder

question: Lately, for about seven months now, things have just felt... off. It started subtly, but now it's like a persistent, stale smell, almost metallic, that nobody else seems to notice. It's truly baffling. The worst part is that nothing brings me joy anymore. My job as a content marketer used to be engaging, coming up with creative campaigns and seeing them succeed. Now, it's just a series of tasks I have to slog through. Even getting together with friends feels like a chore, and I just can't seem to genuinely laugh or feel happy like I used to. It's been like this for over two years, really. I find myself just going through the motions, barely keeping up with work, and my social life has pretty much evaporated. It’s hard to concentrate, and I’m worried about what’s happening to me.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Schizophrenia
    D. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
I’ve been struggling for over two years—almost two and a half, now. Some days, I feel like I can do anything—my mind races, I barely sleep, maybe three hours a night, but I’m not tired. I’ll take on six new clients in a week, reorganize all my files at 2 a.m., call customers nonstop. But then, out of nowhere, I’ll snap—irritable over nothing, then crying for no reason. I don’t know who I am anymore.

For months, I tasted blood when there was none—like sucking on a battery. My mouth fills with this metallic rot, but the doctors find nothing. Sometimes, I catch myself mirroring my coworkers’ gestures without meaning to—reaching for a pen when they do, even if I don’t need it. I used to care about closing deals, promotions. Now, I just sit at my desk. I’ve gained weight, can’t muster the energy to shower most days. I feel… hollow.

I keep thinking the world is ending—like the sun is dimming. Everyone will starve. I don’t answer emails. I should be punished for not doing more. The voices don’t speak, but the taste… it’s constant. Even when I’m not up all night working or collapsing in bed for weeks, the flavors and thoughts don’t leave. They’ve been with me for nearly two years straight.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Bipolar I Disorder
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question:
It’s been about a month now where I just feel…flattened. It’s like this heavy gray filter over everything. I still go to my interpreting jobs, but I’m on autopilot. I catch myself missing signs or lagging behind because I’m so wrapped up in my own head, and that’s never been an issue before.  

I’ve been eating nonstop—snacking between assignments, late at night, anything just to distract myself. My clothes are tighter; I’m pretty sure I’ve put on a noticeable amount of weight in just a few weeks. That just makes me feel worse about myself.  

What’s really scaring me is this overwhelming sense that I’m a terrible person. When I hear about bad things on the news or friends having problems, I feel like somehow it’s my fault, like I’ve caused it by being selfish or not kind enough. I know that doesn’t logically make sense, but it feels true.  

I think about dying almost every day now. Not with a plan, but this constant thought of “Everyone would be better off if I wasn’t here.” My husband notices I’m not myself, but I can’t seem to pull out of it.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder
    B. Major Depressive Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)

question:
I’ve been feeling down for a long time—like, ever since I can remember, but it’s been especially heavy the past two years or so. I wake up every morning already tired of the day before it even starts. Driving my truck used to be something I could count on, just me and the open road, but now it feels like I’m just going through the motions, mile after empty mile. I don’t enjoy much of anything anymore—haven’t for a long time.

I look around and see other guys, even guys my age, and they seem to have things together—families, hobbies, a spark in their eye. I just feel flat. Like I’ve never measured up, never done enough. Even when something goes right, I figure it’s luck or temporary. I keep waiting for things to turn, but deep down, I don’t really believe they ever will.

It’s harder to concentrate on long hauls now. I’ll miss exits, not because I’m not paying attention, but because my mind’s somewhere else—usually on how pointless it all feels. My ex-wife said I was “always so negative,” and I didn’t understand then, but maybe she had a point. I just see the worst in situations before they even happen. Honestly, I’m not sure it’s worth fixing. It’s just... how I’ve learned to live.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Bipolar I Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Persistent Depressive Disorder

question:
It started about eleven months ago, after that car crash on the highway. I walked away with just some bruises, but the guy in the other car didn’t make it. Ever since then, it’s like I’m stuck back there.

Most nights I wake up drenched in sweat from these intense dreams. I’m not always in a car in the dream, but the feeling is the same—pure panic, like everything is about to smash into me. It takes me a while to realize I’m actually in my own bed.

During the day, I can be training a client, music on, gym packed, and suddenly that same fear just hits me out of nowhere. My heart races and I see flashes of the crash. It never really feels like it’s in the past.

I’ve also become really short-fused. I’ll blow up over someone being late to a session or leaving weights out, way out of proportion. Sometimes I scare myself with how fast I go from calm to yelling.

What bothers me too is I feel…flat with people. My mom, my ex, even close friends—I care about them in my head, but I don’t feel it in my chest anymore. It’s like something’s switched off, and it’s messing with my work and my relationships.
option:
A. Schizophrenia
    B. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    C. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    D. Bulimia Nervosa

question:
Lately, it feels like I'm on a rollercoaster I can't get off. For about two months, it was like I was electrified. I was writing new songs, choreographing dances, calling everyone I knew – old cast mates, directors, even people I barely met at auditions. My husband said I was talking a mile a minute, jumping from one idea to the next so fast he couldn't keep up. I even… well, I made some choices then, things I’d never normally do, getting a little too close to people I barely knew. I felt on top of the world, like I was meant for something huge, but then things would flip, and I’d snap at him for the smallest thing. It was all amazing and terrifying at the same time, like the world was ending, and I could smell decay everywhere, even though nothing was there.

But for the past five months… it’s been the complete opposite. I just feel hollow. Like a stage prop, all show and no substance. I used to love rehearsing, the buzz of a live audience, but now? Nothing. Just endless grey, and my brain feels like it’s packed with cotton. I can’t focus on lines; everything’s a blur. It's like the vibrant colors of my world have just faded away.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Bipolar I Disorder
    D. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
answer: D. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
I’ve been struggling for about a year and a half now—maybe even longer if I’m honest. It used to be easier, but working long shifts in the ER, it’s gotten really hard to keep up. I’ll read the same sentence in a chart twice and still not absorb it, or I’ll miss a detail in a patient’s med list, which isn’t like me. Focus just slips, especially during slower periods when I need to document. It’s like my brain drifts, even when I’m trying.

I’ve always been this way, actually. Even in school, I’d start assignments but lose track halfway through. And forgetfulness—I lose pens, phones, even my badge. My coworkers joke that I’m “always moving,” but it’s not really a joke. I can’t seem to sit still, not even after a 12-hour shift. I’ll start watching a movie and end up pacing or scrolling because I can’t relax.

The worst part? In meetings or even with patients, I’ll jump in before they finish asking something. I don’t mean to—I just get the answer and blurt it out. I’ve been told it comes off as impatient, but it’s not that. I just can’t catch myself in time. It’s affecting how I work and how people see me.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    B. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)

question:
I’ve been feeling completely overwhelmed since my studio flooded three months ago—lost almost all my samples, client fittings, everything. I’m a wedding dress designer, so deadlines are non-negotiable, but I just couldn’t keep up after that. It’s only been a few weeks since I realized I wasn’t handling it right.

I’ve never been this reckless before, but lately… I’ve been driving like I don’t care—speeding, running red lights, even got a ticket last week. It’s not me, honestly. I’ve also skipped two client consultations—something I’d *never* do normally. One bride was in tears because I didn’t finish her gown on time. I know it was irresponsible, but I just couldn’t bring myself to go into the studio most days.

A couple of nights ago, I got into a shouting match at a bar, over nothing. I don’t even remember what started it. I’ve always prided myself on being calm, professional, organized—now I feel like I’m falling apart. I’m not sleeping, I’m drinking more, and I’ve lost track of time more than once. I know I’m not coping, but I don’t know how to stop spiraling.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder
    B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Adjustment Disorder
    D. Acute Stress Disorder

question:
I’ve been struggling for almost two years now, ever since… well, since that night. I can’t even say it out loud without my chest tightening. Sometimes a smell, a sound—like brakes screeching—just hits me and I’m back there. I had to stop walking past the bus stop near my lab because a car backfired once and I ended up shaking on the sidewalk, couldn’t breathe. I switched routes, stopped going to conferences, even avoid the lab sometimes when it gets too quiet. It’s like the walls close in.

I used to love my work—figuring things out, the small wins in the data—it meant something. But now nothing feels meaningful. My friends took me out for my birthday last month, laughed, celebrated… and I just sat there, numb. Like I was watching from outside my body. I used to get excited about little things, a good coffee, a sunny day. Now? Nothing lands.

And if something startles me—a door slamming, a sudden noise—I feel my heart jackhammering for minutes, hands trembling. I don’t bounce back like I used to. It takes so long to feel calm, if I ever do. I’m exhausted all the time, but I can’t stop checking the locks, the windows. I live like I’m still in danger.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    B. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    C. Bipolar I Disorder
    D. Schizophrenia

question:
Lately… well, the past few weeks have been rough, but not like the long stretch before that. For about seven months, I just felt empty. Didn’t care if I cleaned the whole building or just sat on a bench. My wife kept saying, “You used to take pride in your work,” but it didn’t matter. I’d wake up two, three times a night and just lie there. Sometimes, standing on the roof during my break, I’d get this sudden urge to step off—no plan, just this pull. Scared myself.

But then it flipped. About three weeks ago, I started sleeping two hours a night, full of energy. I was mopping floors, rewiring light fixtures—stuff not even in my job description. My boss had to tell me to slow down. I snapped at him—he looked at me like I was crazy.

That’s when I started smelling roses everywhere, especially near the elevator. No flowers around, but I could *smell* ‘em. And there’s this woman—Janice, from accounting. I *know* she’s been sending me signs, smiling when I pass. She doesn’t say anything, but I feel it. She’s in love with me. I can’t talk to her without my words rushing out so fast she probably can’t catch ‘em all. My wife doesn’t understand. It’s like she doesn’t see what’s right there.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
answer: C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
It started about a year ago. I’m sorry if this sounds strange, but I’m absolutely convinced something is seriously wrong inside my abdomen. It feels like… not normal organs, like there’s some kind of growth or foreign thing that all the scans are just missing. I’ve had ultrasounds, blood work, even a CT, and they all say I’m fine, but I know I’m not. I can feel it—this fullness, this wrongness—especially at night or when I’m sitting still.

I keep going back to different specialists because I’m sure they’ve overlooked something. My husband and my sister say I’m “obsessing,” but they don’t understand how real it feels. I still go to work, run my team, hit my deadlines. On the surface I’m functioning, but most of my mental energy is spent monitoring these sensations, researching rare conditions, and worrying that time is running out.

It’s affecting my sleep, my mood at home, and I’ve pulled back from friends because I don’t want to keep explaining it or be treated like I’m imagining it. But I’m not. I’m certain something is physically wrong.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Schizophrenia
    C. Delusional Disorder
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)

question:
I’m not here because I’m “depressed” or “anxious.” I’m here because something is going to happen, and no one seems to understand the scale of it.

For a little over two years now—two years and a month, roughly—I’ve been absolutely certain that we’re heading toward total collapse. Not in some vague, political sense; I mean literally nothing will be left. I can’t explain exactly how I know, but it’s as if I can see the pattern underneath the news, the markets, even the way people talk. It all points in one direction: everything is going to be wiped out.

I still go to court, I still handle my cases, and I do fine—my partners would tell you I’m functioning normally. But outside of work, this is all I think about. I’ve sold most of my investments, I’ve stocked my apartment with supplies, and I keep revising my will because, frankly, what’s the point of planning for retirement if there won’t be a world left? My ex-wife says I’m “obsessed,” but to me I’m just facing reality while everyone else is in denial.
option:
A. Schizophrenia
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    C. Delusional Disorder
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)

question:
I’ve been having a lot more trouble the past nine months, but honestly this kind of thing has been going on since I was a kid. Teachers always used to say I couldn’t sit still or wait my turn, and it feels like the same thing now, just in adult life.

On my night shifts at the store, I can’t just stand behind the counter. I’m constantly pacing, tapping, shifting my weight. If it’s slow, I walk laps around the aisles, straighten stuff that’s already straight, check the doors over and over. I feel like there’s a motor running inside me and I can’t shut it off.

I get really impatient in lines or when someone in front of me at work is taking too long. Even with customers, I’ll cut in while they’re still talking, or finish their sentences. With my coworkers or manager, I butt into conversations without really thinking and then realize I’ve talked over them.

I also make dumb mistakes at work, like ringing things up wrong or miscounting change, because I rush and don’t double-check the details. It’s not that I don’t know how; I just seem to miss stuff all the time.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)

question:
I’ve been struggling for about nine months now—things started slipping at work, and it’s only gotten worse. Flying used to be second nature, but lately, I can’t focus. I’ve lost interest in everything, even the simulator sessions I used to love. My wife says I’ve barely touched food in months. I feel like a burden—like I’ve ruined things not just at work, but… everywhere. I once believed I caused that engine failure last year. It wasn’t me—mechanics confirmed it—but I still feel responsible, like it was some punishment.

And then there’s the smell—like burning wires and decay, even when nothing’s there. I know it sounds strange, but I taste it, too. It doesn’t go away. I’ve become convinced my wife is seeing someone—there’s no proof, but I *know* it. She denies it, but the “evidence” is everywhere—in the way she answers her phone, her tone when she says my name.

Sometimes my thoughts just… scramble. I’ll try to speak and the words won’t line up. And there’s been this urge to lock myself into strange positions—standing rigid in the garage, arms out—like I’m defying gravity just to feel real. It's like my mind won’t let me rest.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question: "Honestly, for the past six months or so, it's been a wild ride. Like, I feel amazing, truly on top of the world. I've got this boundless energy, like I could wire a whole skyscraper by myself and not even break a sweat. My boss at the apprenticeship, he's always saying I'm a natural, and I believe him – I just get things, you know? Like I'm smarter at this than anyone else. I've been sleeping way less, maybe a couple of hours a night, but I don't even feel tired. In fact, I feel better than ever. I've also been a bit more, uh, outgoing with women. Just a lot more confident and interested in that sort of thing lately. But it's almost too much sometimes, like I can't quite focus, and my foreman's been on my case about rushing things, which isn't like me."
option:
A. Bipolar II Disorder
    B. Bipolar I Disorder
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)

question:
I’ve been struggling for a while now—like over two years, maybe two and a half. At first, I thought it was just stress from work or being on my own after the divorce, but it’s gotten way worse. I run a used car lot, used to love closing deals, the hustle, talking people through their options—you know, reading them, building rapport. Now, I can’t even get excited about a big sale. Nothing feels good anymore. My buddy invited me to a ball game last month, something I used to live for, and I just sat there, numb.

But the weirdest stuff started happening about seven months ago. I’ll be sitting in my office, have a sip of coffee, and suddenly it tastes like metal—like sucking on a battery—even though the pot’s the same brand I’ve used for years. It happens with water too. No matter how many times I rinse my mouth, the taste stays. And sometimes, outta nowhere, I can’t tell what’s real. I’ll catch myself thinking people from the dealership are talking about me when they’re not—even saying things in whispers I can somehow hear perfectly. I know how that sounds, but it feels so real in the moment. I’ve stopped going to staff meetings because I can’t trust what I’m hearing anymore. It’s messing everything up—sales are down, I’m missing payments, and I don’t even care enough to fix it.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Schizophrenia
    D. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
I’ve been struggling for about a year and a half now—maybe even longer, honestly. It started back in high school, actually. I always had a hard time sitting still during class. I’d be tapping my foot, drumming my fingers on the desk, or just shifting around in my chair. My teachers used to call me out for it all the time.

The worst part is, I can’t seem to stop myself from jumping in during conversations. Like, people aren’t even finished asking a question, and I’m already answering. I don’t mean to interrupt—I just feel this push to get it out before I forget. It’s caused some problems with customers at the gas station. They’ll ask about fuel prices or directions, and I’ll blurt something out before they finish. Some get annoyed.

Leisure stuff? I don’t really do it. I tried playing video games quietly, but I get too loud—yelling, pacing. Watching TV? I end up walking around, can’t just sit and focus. Even organizing the shelves at work is hard. I know where things go, but I end up doing it all out of order, and I forget steps. I’ve had this since I was a kid—like, elementary school. Never thought it was a “thing,” but now it’s affecting everything.
option:
A. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)

question:
Well, it’s been about two weeks now since my daughter moved me out of my house and into this retirement place. I know she means well, but I feel like the rug’s been pulled out from under me.

Most days I just feel low and sort of hollow. I sit in my room and stare at the TV without really watching it. I used to enjoy reading the paper, doing crossword puzzles, but I can’t seem to concentrate. I worry a lot—about losing more of my independence, about being a burden. At night my mind races: “What if I fall? What if they decide I can’t handle my own money?” I wake up early, tense, with this tight feeling in my chest.

I’ve also been more irritable than I care to admit. I snapped at my granddaughter on the phone and told my daughter not to visit if she was just going to “check up” on me. I’ve skipped the group meals a few times on purpose and refused to let the staff help, even when I clearly needed it. That’s not really like me, but I feel angry and cornered.
option:
A. Adjustment Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    C. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question:
"It's been... well, almost two years now, I guess. I just feel so out of control around food. I'll start eating and I can't stop, even when I'm already stuffed to the point of pain. It's like a switch flips and nothing else matters. I inhale everything in sight until I feel absolutely miserable.

I usually wait until I'm alone to eat like that. I work as a pet groomer, and even though I love my job, the clients and the other groomers all see me. The thought of anyone seeing me shovel food in, the sheer amount I can eat… it's mortifying. So I hide. I just feel so ashamed afterward, disgusted with myself. It's been happening at least once a week, and I'm just so tired of it."
option:
A. Bulimia Nervosa
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Binge-Eating Disorder

question: "Well, doctor, it's been a tough year, if I'm being honest. It started subtle, but now… it's just exhausting. Every time the sky even looks a bit gray, my heart starts pounding out of my chest. And don't even get me started on the wind when it howls through the windows here in the nursing home. I get this awful tightening in my chest, a real knot in my stomach, like a storm is going to tear the place apart. I know it sounds silly, an old lady like me, but I can't help it. I try to tell myself it's just the weather, but the fear... it's just too strong. I can't escape it; the windows are everywhere. I just wish I could relax and enjoy my golden years instead of constantly dreading the next cloudy day."
option:
A. Specific Phobia
    B. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    D. Generalized Anxiety Disorder

question:
Lately, things have been… intense. For about two weeks now, I haven’t needed much sleep—like, two or three hours a night, tops—but I don’t feel tired at all. If anything, I feel *sharper*. My mind’s moving so fast I can track five trading charts at once, place dozens of trades an hour, even start coding a new bot while monitoring volatility. I’ve been buying—way more than usual. Pumped almost all my liquid holdings into some new coins, even bought a second Tesla on a whim, just to “flip the energy.” My friends say it’s reckless, but they don’t get it. They don’t see the patterns I see.

And honestly, I’ve been on edge. Little things set me off—like if a trade lags or someone disagrees with my analysis, I snap. It’s like the whole market’s responding to *me*, like I’m the pulse. I’ve been smelling this weird odor too—like burnt wire or chemicals—no one else notices it, but it’s always there, especially when I’m onto something big. Plus, I’ve been feeling it in my organs—like my heart’s expanding, or my blood’s turning digital. I know that sounds strange, but it’s real to me. I can *feel* the blockchain in my veins.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    D. Bipolar I Disorder
answer: A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question: "Honestly, Dr. Sharma, I don't know where to start. For the past year, maybe a little longer, it's just been this... *thing* with my face. I'm a software developer, so I'm used to things being logical, precise. But when I look in the mirror, my face just feels off-kilter. Like, the left side isn't matching the right. It feels visibly lopsided. I spend so much time analyzing it, tracing lines, staring. It takes up hours some days, especially if I have to be on a video call for work. It’s not just in my head, is it? I've asked my colleagues, my friends, even my mom, if they notice it. They all say I look fine, that I'm being silly, but I just can't shake it. It's really hard to focus on my code when this is constantly gnawing at me. It's making it tough to even leave the house sometimes."
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder
    B. Body Dysmorphic Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    D. Generalized Anxiety Disorder

question:
I guess this really started about seven months ago. At first I thought it was just a rough semester, but it’s like something inside me just shut off and never came back on. I don’t really feel sad exactly; it’s more like I don’t feel much of anything. Things that used to matter—a good grade on a paper, positive feedback from my advisor—barely register. I know I should be proud, but it just feels…blank.

I’m exhausted all the time. Even small things, like showering or answering emails, feel like they take way more effort than I have. I used to go to the gym and cook; now I mostly skip meals without really noticing. My clothes are getting loose and people keep commenting that I’ve lost a lot of weight, but I’m not trying to.

My work has really suffered. I miss deadlines, avoid lab meetings, and have started dodging friends’ messages. It feels like I’m moving through molasses while everyone else is on fast-forward, and I just don’t have the energy to care anymore.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question:
"Thanks for seeing me, Doc. Lately, things have been pretty wild. For about a month now, I've felt this surge of energy, like I'm on top of the world. I've been picking up double the delivery shifts, sometimes starting three different routes at once just to see how much I can get done. My mind just races, bouncing from one idea to the next so fast I can barely keep up – it’s like my thoughts have a mind of their own. I’ve been hitting up everyone I know, even people I haven’t spoken to in ages, just to chat and hang out. It feels good, almost too good, and I haven't really had any problems with my job during these times.

But before this, for about six months, it was a completely different story. I just felt… flat. Everything just seemed grey, and riding my bike, which I used to love, felt like a chore. I’d be so tired even after a full night’s sleep; my body felt heavy all the time. Focusing on anything was impossible; I’d try to read a street name and my mind would just drift. Sometimes, I’d just wish I wouldn't wake up the next morning, not like I wanted to hurt myself, but just to make it all stop. Those stretches make it really hard to keep up with deliveries and pay the bills."
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Bipolar II Disorder
    D. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
It’s mostly about my face. I know it probably sounds stupid, but it’s gotten out of hand. For at least the last couple of years, I can’t stop thinking about my nose and around my eyes. When I look in the mirror, all I see is this crooked, swollen-looking nose and these weird, uneven eyes. My wife and my mates say they don’t see anything wrong, but I feel like everyone on site is staring and judging.

I’m in the bathroom a lot, checking different angles, taking photos, zooming in. I’ll stand there for ages before work, trying to “fix” it with lighting or shaving different. I also mess with the skin on my nose and around my mouth, scratching and squeezing anything that looks off. I end up with red marks and scabs, then I feel even worse and pick at those too.

It’s started to mess with my job. Some mornings I call in sick because I can’t face being seen up in the cab all day. Even at home, I avoid family pictures, hate going out, and I’m constantly asking my wife if I look “deformed.”
option:
A. Bulimia Nervosa
    B. Specific Phobia
    C. Body Dysmorphic Disorder
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)

question:
It’s been about a year now that things have felt…off. The heavy, empty feeling started around ten months ago. It’s not sadness exactly, more like I’m hollowed out. I used to be really into my job—campaign ideas, brainstorming sessions—but now just putting a brief together makes my brain feel exhausted, like I’ve run a marathon by only thinking.

I’ve lost a lot of weight without trying; food mostly tastes wrong anyway. For about eight months I’ve been getting these strange tastes in my mouth—metallic, or like something rotten—even when I’m just drinking water. Sometimes I’m convinced it means something bad is happening, like the world is decaying from the inside out. I keep having this strong belief that everything is heading toward some kind of collapse and that there’s no point in planning anything.

My face and voice don’t seem to match what I’m feeling. People at work say I seem “blank” or “checked out,” even on Zoom. I avoid friends, and my performance has dropped; writing a simple caption can take me all day. Sleep is awful—recurring nightmares where everything is destroyed, and I wake up drenched in sweat, heart racing, then just lie there feeling numb.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
"Thanks for seeing me, doctor. It's been... I don't know, about five months now, and things are just getting worse. It's hard to even talk about.

I’m an ER nurse, so I’m used to high-stress situations, but this is different. After a long shift, or if I just feel overwhelmed, I find myself just… eating. Not just a little snack, but huge amounts of food, until I feel sick. It's like I can’t stop myself, even when I know I should.

Then, the guilt sets in. That’s when I start taking extra laxatives. It’s the only way I feel like I can undo what I’ve done, to get rid of it all so I don't gain weight. My job is so physical, I see so many different body types, and I just can't stand the thought of anyone seeing me as anything less than perfect. My whole sense of worth, honestly, feels tied to how I look, how much I weigh. If I'm not in control of that, I feel like I'm not in control of anything."
option:
A. Anorexia Nervosa
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    C. Bulimia Nervosa
    D. Binge-Eating Disorder

question:
I’ve always cared about staying healthy, especially with how active my job is—running around during weddings, lifting equipment, always on my feet. But over the past year, I’ve become really strict about what I eat. I don’t eat anything with sugar or fat anymore—no bread, no pasta, no cheese. I pack plain grilled chicken and steamed veggies, even at family dinners. My husband doesn’t understand—he’ll say, “Just have a bite,” but I can’t. I see a piece of cake and I think, *If I eat that, I’ll gain weight instantly—it’ll show in my arms, my face, everywhere.*

I weigh myself every morning, and if the number goes up, I feel like a failure. Like I’ve let everyone down—my clients, my husband, even myself. Some weeks, I cancel shoots because I don’t feel “ready” to be seen, even though no one’s complained. I know I’m thin—people have started noticing, asking if I’m okay—but to me, I still look… not where I need to be. Being in control of my food makes me feel like I’m doing something right. If I lose weight, I feel proud. But nothing ever feels like enough.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    B. Bulimia Nervosa
    C. Anorexia Nervosa
    D. Persistent Depressive Disorder

question: "Thank you for seeing me, Doctor. It's gotten to the point where I just can't keep living like this. Everything feels... uncertain. For the past year or so, maybe a bit longer, I've been preoccupied with whether I've done things correctly. It's mostly revolved around the house since my husband passed. I'll lock the front door, walk away, and then this nagging feeling starts. Did I *really* lock it? Was it just a click or did I turn the key twice? I'll go back, sometimes two or three times, just to check. The same thing happens with the stove, the gas, even the water faucets. I’ve always been meticulous, of course, comes with the territory of nursing, but this feels different. It's not just thoroughness anymore; it's a constant, gnawing doubt that eats away at my day. It's exhausting, and I feel like I'm losing my mind."
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    B. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)

question:
It’s been about two weeks now where I’ve felt like I’m running on rocket fuel. I barely sleep—maybe two hours a night, sometimes none—and I don’t even feel tired. I’m up all night pacing, planning routes, making lists, scrolling, just buzzing. At work I can’t sit still in the van; I speed, get impatient at lights, snap at customers if they’re slow. The tiniest thing, like someone cutting me off, makes me instantly furious, like I’m ready to explode.

I’ve also been spending like crazy. I blew through my savings on stuff I don’t need—new phone, expensive shoes, random gadgets—and put a bunch on credit cards. I keep thinking, “It’s fine, money will sort itself out,” but now I’m behind on bills.

There’s this girl at one of my regular drop-offs, and I’m convinced she’s secretly into me. Every look, every “thanks,” feels like a sign. I’ve even seen her face in crowds or in my room when I’m alone, like she’s actually there watching me or trying to send me messages. I know this all sounds off, but in the moment it feels completely real and I can’t shut it off.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    D. Bipolar I Disorder
answer: A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
It’s been a weird few months. About three months ago I hit this insane “high” that lasted like a month. I was on fire. I’d wake up after maybe two hours of sleep and feel like I’d had ten. Sometimes I just wouldn’t sleep at all and still head straight to the gym at 4 a.m. I was taking on extra clients, planning online programs, talking nonstop, starting side projects—meal plans, a YouTube channel, a new class format—without actually finishing anything. My mind wouldn’t shut up; ideas were tripping over each other. I’d be in the middle of a session and suddenly be thinking about ten different business plans and lose track of what I was doing with the client.

Then it just crashed. For the last two months, I’ve felt like someone pulled the plug. I don’t really care about my progress, my physique, or my clients’ results the way I used to. The gym feels like walking through mud; even demoing simple exercises wears me out. On top of that, I keep beating myself up—like I ruined people’s training, wasted their time, screwed up my reputation. I feel like I don’t deserve to do this job anymore.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Bipolar I Disorder

question:
It’s been a little over two years since things really started to change. Around that time I began having these spells where I felt unusually “charged up” and on edge. For the past year and a half or so, I’m irritable most days—tiny things set me off, like the neighbor’s dog barking or the TV being slightly too loud. I pace a lot; I can’t seem to just sit and read like I used to. My daughter says I talk nonstop, jumping from one topic to another, and she can’t get a word in.

I’ve also had this embarrassing increase in sexual thoughts and urges that’s very unlike me, especially at my age and since my husband passed. It’s caused arguments with my family.

Alongside that, for about a year and a half I’ve noticed these odd smells—strong perfume or smoke—when no one else can smell anything. I sometimes catch myself doing little repetitive gestures, like tapping my fingers in a certain pattern before I sit or standing in a particular way, almost like a ritual. I used to be very active and organized as a nurse; now I struggle to initiate basic things—bathing, cooking, even calling friends—and some weeks those strange smells and beliefs linger even when my mood isn’t especially high.
option:
A. Schizophrenia
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question:
I’ve been struggling with my eating for about nine months now. It started around the time the market got really stressful and my ex remarried, and it’s just gotten worse.  

In the evenings, especially after a long day of showings, I’ll stop “just to grab something,” and it turns into this… episode. I’ll go through drive-thru, then grab snacks at a gas station, then still eat whatever’s in my kitchen. It’s not like a normal big meal — it’s way more than anyone needs. And once I start, it feels like something takes over. I tell myself “just one more bite,” but I can’t seem to stop, even when I want to.  

I keep eating until I’m so full it actually hurts to sit or lie down. I’ve had to unbutton my pants and just sit at the edge of the bed holding my stomach. This is happening at least once, sometimes several times a week. I’m embarrassed by it, I hide the trash, and in the morning I feel disgusted with myself and promise I won’t do it again… but then I do.
option:
A. Binge-Eating Disorder
    B. Delusional Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Bulimia Nervosa

question:
I’ve been having a real hard time since that accident three days ago. I was driving home from visiting a parishioner in the hospital when a truck ran a red light and hit me head-on. The car was totaled, but somehow I didn’t break any bones—just bruises and a bad cut on my forehead. The paramedics said I was lucky.

But I can’t shake it. I keep trying to remember what happened in those seconds before the crash, but it’s like a blank—it’s gone. I know I was talking to my wife on the phone, but after that… nothing. I woke up in the ambulance, and everything’s fuzzy.

Since then, I haven’t been able to bring myself to get behind the wheel. I canceled my counseling appointments with the youth group because their usual meeting spot is near the intersection where it happened. Even the sight of an ambulance makes my heart race. I can’t sleep, and last night I woke up sweating, thinking I heard screeching tires. I don’t know how I’m going to preach this Sunday—my mind just won’t rest. I keep waiting for God to speak, but all I feel is this numb, hollow fear.
option:
A. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    B. Binge-Eating Disorder
    C. Acute Stress Disorder
    D. Generalized Anxiety Disorder

question:
It feels a little ridiculous saying this out loud, because I design autonomous systems for a living and I know the stats, but for about the last ten months I’ve had this really intense reaction to driving over large bridges.

It started after a business trip where I had to cross the Bay Bridge in heavy traffic. Now, even just approaching a big bridge, my heart starts pounding, my hands get sweaty on the wheel, and my breathing feels shallow, like I can’t quite get enough air. Sometimes my legs actually tremble on the pedals. I get this rush of “I need to get off this thing right now,” even though I know that’s not possible.

I can’t always avoid them—my office is on the other side of the river—so I still make myself drive over, but the whole time I’m white‑knuckling it, barely hearing my wife or the kids if they’re in the car. I’ve started turning down on‑site meetings and choosing projects that don’t require travel, which is starting to cause friction at work. My wife is getting frustrated because we plan family outings around which routes don’t involve big bridges.
option:
A. Specific Phobia
    B. Anorexia Nervosa
    C. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    D. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder

question:
I’ve always kind of struggled to stay focused, even when I was a kid—my wife says I’ve had this “halfway listening” thing forever. But it’s been worse lately, and honestly, it’s starting to affect my job. I’ve been driving a truck for 15 years now, and most of the time, it’s steady work—long stretches on the highway, paperwork at the terminals. But I find myself zoning out, even during the monotonous parts of the drive. I’ll miss an exit or almost drift lanes because I just… stopped paying attention.  

It’s not just driving, though. I hate filling out logs or going over delivery forms—it feels overwhelming, like my brain shuts down. I keep putting it off, even though I know I shouldn’t. Last week, I lost my fuel receipts *again*. Misplaced my wallet twice in one month. My wife’s frustrated—says I don’t listen when she talks, even when it’s something important.  

And I can’t sit still, even when I try. In the cab, I’m constantly tapping my fingers on the wheel, shifting in my seat, bouncing my leg. It’s not on purpose—I just feel restless, like I’ve got energy with nowhere to go.  

This has been going on for about a year and a half, but now I see it’s not just stress. It’s been like this since I was a kid—just followed me into adulthood.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    B. Schizophrenia
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)

question:
For the past three months, it's just been a fog. I'm 48, working at City Hall, and usually, I’m good at my job, detail-oriented. But now, it’s like I’m moving through molasses. There's this hollowness inside, an emptiness, and even when something good happens, like getting a commendation last month, I just don't feel anything. My wife tries to cheer me up, but I'm just… indifferent.

Sleep isn't helping either. I'm sleeping for twelve, sometimes fourteen hours, and I still wake up feeling utterly drained, wanting to just go back to bed. It’s hard to focus at work; things are piling up. Even worse, these thoughts have started creeping in – not just about wanting things to stop, but actual plans. I’ve found myself thinking about how I could just… end it, detailing it out in my head. It's frightening, and I know I need to do something.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question:
I’ve been feeling… off… for about seven months now. At first, it was just this sense that people were watching me—on set, at home, even in my car. I know it sounds crazy, but I’m convinced the studio’s been bugging my phone. They want to sabotage my next film, maybe erase me completely. I had to stop editing at the office. I can’t trust anyone there.

My assistant tried to talk to me a few weeks ago, but I just couldn’t piece it together—her words, the script, the lights on the ceiling. It’s like my thoughts get tangled. I start talking about lens calibration and end up ranting about satellite signals. She looked terrified. I fired her. Not because I wanted to—it’s just safer if I work alone now.

I don’t see friends anymore. Don’t call my daughter. Not because I don’t love her—because I can’t risk them getting caught in whatever’s coming. I stay in the editing suite most days. The voices in the hum of the monitors… they’re clearer, somehow honest. People say I’m disconnected. But maybe I’m the only one who sees things as they really are.
option:
A. Delusional Disorder
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    C. Schizophrenia
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)

question:
It’s been about three months now where I just feel…flattened. I wake up already exhausted, even if I’ve been in bed eight or nine hours. It’s like the sleep doesn’t “take.” I used to love putting together itineraries, talking excitedly with clients about their trips, comparing hotels and little hidden-gem restaurants. Now I just stare at the screen and go through the motions. I don’t enjoy it; I’m just trying not to fall behind or get fired.

Outside of work, I don’t want to do anything. I used to meet friends for wine and plan our own vacations, but I keep canceling, saying I’m busy. Truth is, I just sit on the couch, feeling heavy and useless. Since the divorce I’ve felt low on and off, but this is different—like it’s never lifting.

A few weeks ago, I actually gathered up some old pain pills and put them in a drawer, thinking it might be easier for everyone if I just wasn’t here. That scared me, which is why I finally agreed to come in and talk to someone.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question:
For about six months now, I just haven’t felt like myself. I used to love getting up early, going for a run, and then diving into work. Now, most mornings I just sit on the edge of the bed staring at the floor, trying to convince myself to move. Even walking from my car to the office feels like I’m wading through mud. My staff has commented that I seem “slowed down” in meetings—pauses, long silences, even reaching for a pen feels deliberate.

Things I used to enjoy—golf on weekends, dinners out with my wife, even brainstorming new ideas for the business—feel flat. I go through the motions, but there’s no real pleasure in any of it. I’m exhausted all the time, even on nights when I actually sleep.

I’ve also started worrying about my memory. I misplace my phone and keys constantly, forget what I was about to say in the middle of a board meeting, lose track of emails. It’s affecting the company and my relationship with my wife; I’m withdrawing more, and it feels like everything is slipping through my fingers.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Major Depressive Disorder
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question:
It’s been about a year since things really started going off for me. The low mood hit first, maybe 10 months ago, and it just never really lifted. I cry a lot now, sometimes for no clear reason, even at home in front of my wife, which is not like me. I’m exhausted all the time; even walking around the warehouse or loading a few boxes leaves me wiped out. I’ve put on a lot of weight over a few weeks — my clothes barely fit — but I don’t really have the energy to care. I keep waking up around 3 or 4 a.m. and just lie there, staring at the ceiling, feeling heavy and hopeless.

Around 8 months ago I became convinced there’s something seriously wrong inside my body — like my organs are rotting or shifting. No tests have shown anything, but I can’t shake it. Sometimes I feel things crawling or moving under my skin, especially at night. It’s so real I keep checking, but there’s nothing there. At work and even at home I talk less now; answering people feels like too much effort, so I mostly say the minimum.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
Honestly, this has gotten away from me over the last five months. It started as stress eating after work, but now at least once or twice a week I have these episodes where it’s like a switch flips. I’ll get home late, everyone’s asleep, and I just stand in the kitchen or sit in front of the TV and power through ridiculous amounts of food in a short time—leftovers, cereal, takeaway, whatever I can find. I eat so fast I barely taste it.

Once I start, I feel like I can’t stop, even when I’m already feeling painfully full. I’ll tell myself “just one more plate,” and then it’s another, and another. It’s not that I’m physically hungry; it feels more like I’m trying to kill the anxiety from the markets, performance numbers, expectations.

Afterwards I’m bloated, uncomfortable, and disgusted with myself. I hide how much I’ve eaten from my wife—take the trash out, replace things. During the day I’m controlled and disciplined with everything, but these episodes feel completely out of my hands.
option:
A. Adjustment Disorder
    B. Bulimia Nervosa
    C. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Binge-Eating Disorder

question:
I’ve been having a really hard time lately—this has been building for over two years, maybe even two and a half. At first, I brushed it off as stress from work. The ER is chaotic, but I used to thrive in that. Now, I can’t focus. I keep thinking my boyfriend is cheating on me, even though we broke up eight months ago. We weren’t even together long, but I still feel like he’s out there with someone else, planning things, talking about me. I don’t have proof, but I *know* it’s true. Sometimes I hear his voice when I’m charting at 3 a.m., saying things like, “You don’t matter,” or “She’s prettier, smarter.”  

I used to talk a lot with my coworkers, joke around between codes, but now I just… don’t. I don’t know what to say. It feels like my thoughts get thin, like smoke. I’ll start a sentence and forget why I’m talking. My charge nurse asked if I was okay last week because I just stood there during handoff, staring. I wanted to explain, but the words weren’t there.  

Sleep is shot. I lie awake thinking about how everyone in the unit knows something about me—gossiping in the med room, laughing when I walk by. I don’t trust anyone. Some days, I wonder if coming into work makes anything better or if I’m just… fading.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Delusional Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Schizophrenia

question:
I’ve always prided myself on being in control—on camera, my composure matters. But for the past nine months, something’s been slipping, and it scares me. Usually late at night, after taping the late news, I’ll come home alone and just… lose it. It starts with a small snack, maybe yogurt or a piece of toast, but suddenly I’m tearing through the pantry—chips, cookies, cold pasta, anything. I don’t even taste half of it. I just keep going, way past the point where I feel sick. I’ve eaten entire family-sized bags of chips in one sitting. 

The worst part is the feeling—I can’t stop, even though I want to. It’s like watching myself from the outside, powerless. I tell myself I’ll stop tomorrow, but then the next assignment stress hits, or I’m just so tired and lonely, and it happens again. I’d say it’s happened at least once a week, sometimes more. I don’t purge or anything—I just feel bloated, ashamed, and exhausted afterward. I can’t let anyone on the crew see me unravel like this.
option:
A. Binge-Eating Disorder
    B. Delusional Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Bulimia Nervosa

question:
I’ve been struggling for a long time—since I was a kid, really—but it’s gotten harder to ignore lately. At work, I’m the foreman, so I’ve got guys depending on me, plans to review, safety checks, schedules… but I’ll catch myself zoning out during meetings, missing small details on blueprints, and then someone has to redo work because I signed off on the wrong thing. I don’t mean to, but I’ll start something, get distracted, and forget to follow through—like last month when I assigned a crew but never confirmed the materials were delivered. My wife brings it up too—leaving the garage door open, forgetting to pay bills, half-finished projects around the house.  

I’m always moving—can’t sit still, even when I’m tired. My wife says I’m like a machine that won’t shut off. And I know I interrupt people—during calls, at home—I get an idea and just jump in, even if someone’s talking. It’s like the thought pops up and I’ve already said it before I realize. I’ve had this since I was little—teachers said I was smart but couldn’t focus, always blurting out answers. It’s just… catching up with me now that the job’s got more pressure. I’ve felt this way for almost two years now, but honestly, it’s always been there.
option:
A. Schizophrenia
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)

question:
I’ve always been kind of particular about food, but over the past two years, I guess it’s gotten a little more… structured. I own a flower shop, and mornings are busy, so I just stopped doing breakfast—it’s easier not to worry about it. Lunch usually ends up being a few cherry tomatoes or a piece of plain lettuce, if anything. I pack something small for my husband, but I’m not really hungry by then. 

People keep saying I look too thin, that I should gain a bit, especially since I’m on my feet all day arranging bouquets, but I feel better this way—lighter, in control. My clothes fit perfectly now, and I can finally see my collarbones, which I’ve always wanted.
