My husband’s worried, and my doctor mentioned something at my last visit, but I told her I’m fine—really. I eat enough. I just have a fast metabolism, and I’ve always been small. They don’t get it. I know my body best. Losing a few more pounds might actually help me feel even more focused at work. I just want to feel… right in my skin.
option:
A. Anorexia Nervosa
    B. Acute Stress Disorder
    C. Bulimia Nervosa
    D. Body Dysmorphic Disorder

question: It’s been a rough week, doc. Honestly, I feel like I'm losing it, and it all started right after that layover in Tokyo when my schedule got completely messed up. I know it sounds silly, just a flight schedule, but it really threw me off. Usually, I'm super organized, on top of everything. Now? I'm just… angry. I got into a shouting match with a passenger last flight – completely out of character for me. I even almost missed my crew briefing this morning; I just couldn't bring myself to get out of bed. It’s not like me to be so reckless. I've been feeling this way for about a week now, and it's making my job, which I usually love, feel impossible.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder
    B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Adjustment Disorder
    D. Acute Stress Disorder

question:
For the past year or so, things have felt... off. It started subtly, maybe ten months ago, with this crushing fatigue. I just can't seem to get enough sleep, even after a full night, and getting out of bed feels like a monumental task. My appetite has been through the roof, and I've put on a good 15 pounds. There's this pervasive sadness, a heavy cloud that just won't lift. It’s been tough at work; concentrating on complex designs feels nearly impossible with how my mind wanders and my body just feels so heavy. My wife, bless her heart, notices I’m quieter, less expressive.

Then, about eight months ago, things took a really dark turn. These visions started – glimpses out of the corner of my eye, shadowy figures that disappear when I focus. And the thoughts... it’s like a certainty, a deep-seated belief that the world is crumbling, that some catastrophic event is imminent. The oddest thing is, there was a period of a few weeks where it was just these visions and the feeling of impending doom, without the heavy sadness. It's all just so draining, making it hard to connect with anyone or even feel much of anything anymore.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
It’s a bit embarrassing, to be honest. I’ve been working with trucks and disposal sites for over twenty years, so I’m used to some rough situations, but for the last two years I can’t handle certain places at all.

It started after I was stuck in a lift at a client’s building for about half an hour. Since then, any time I even think about getting into an elevator, or driving through a long tunnel, or across one of those big enclosed bridges, I just freeze up. My chest tightens, my heart starts pounding, my hands get sweaty, and I feel like I can’t get enough air. It hits me straight away, like someone flipped a switch.

At work I now take the stairs, even if it’s ten floors, or I’ll drive the long way round to avoid tunnels. My crew laughs, but I just can’t do it. If my wife suggests a trip that involves flying or trains through tunnels, I make excuses. It’s starting to cause arguments at home and making my job harder, but in the moment the fear feels completely out of control.
option:
A. Specific Phobia
    B. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    C. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    D. Acute Stress Disorder

question:
I’ve been struggling with my relationship with food for about a year and a half now. As a hospital administrator, my days are packed—meetings back to back, constant decisions, people relying on me—but it’s not the stress exactly… it’s more like, I can’t stop myself when I start eating.  

It usually happens at night, after I finally get home. I’ll eat dinner like normal, but then an hour later, I’ll stand in front of the pantry or fridge and just… go. I’ll eat a whole bag of chips, a box of cookies, maybe a frozen meal too—stuff I know I wouldn’t normally eat together. And I do it fast, like I’m racing. Before I know it, I’m painfully full, but I can’t seem to stop. That feeling—like my body isn’t mine in those moments—is what scares me the most.  

It’s happened at least once a week for as long as I can remember now. Sometimes more. I live alone, so no one sees it, but I feel ashamed after. I’ll skip meals the next day trying to "fix" it, but the cycle just repeats. I can’t keep living like this.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    B. Bulimia Nervosa
    C. Binge-Eating Disorder
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question:
Well, it’s been a rough year, doc. For about three months, it was like I was running on rocket fuel. I'd be up at 3 AM organizing archival boxes, my mind racing, making connections no one else saw. I barely slept, maybe two or three hours, but felt completely vitalized. My colleagues probably thought I’d lost it, I was talking so fast, jumping from one idea to the next. Every little noise or movement around the office would pull my attention away, too. It felt like everyone was watching me, like they knew something. I even started seeing shadowy figures in the stacks, just at the edge of my vision – fleeting, but definitely there.

Then, everything crashed. For the last nine months, I just... stopped caring. The archives, my hobbies – nothing brings me joy anymore. My body feels so heavy, so tired, even simple tasks at work drain me. I just replay every mistake I’ve ever made, every awkward conversation, convincing myself I’m worthless. It’s hard to even get out of bed most days.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Bipolar I Disorder
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
answer: B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
It’s been a little over two years now that things have felt… off. About a year and nine months ago it really escalated. I started feeling constantly on edge and snappy, even with patrons at the library over tiny things like overdue books. At the same time I got this burst of energy — I’d stay up until three or four in the morning reorganizing my shelves, planning new programs, starting projects at work and at home, but never finishing any of them. I can’t seem to stick with one task; my mind jumps from idea to idea.

Sleep hasn’t felt necessary. I might get three hours and still feel wired, not tired. It’s been affecting my work — I lose track of what I’m doing at the circulation desk, mis-shelve books, forget conversations.

What worries me most are these experiences where I’m sure people are talking about me. A random laugh in the stacks, a comment on the radio, even a bookmark someone left in a book — it all feels like it’s directed at me. And sometimes I feel things crawling on my skin or like someone is brushing past me when no one is there. Those sensations and the feeling of being “pointed at” have happened even during stretches when my mood wasn’t especially high or irritable.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Schizophrenia

question:
I guess this has been really noticeable for about seven months now, but my wife says I’ve “always been like this,” even as a kid. I remember getting in trouble in school for wandering around the classroom and talking when everyone else was seated, so maybe it goes back that far.

Lately it’s really messing with work. I’m a supervisor on the line, and I’m constantly bouncing between stations. I’ll start filling out a report, then suddenly I’m over checking a machine, then talking to someone about the schedule, and the report is half-done or missing. My desk is a disaster. I try to make lists, but I misplace them, or I’ll put the production sheet down somewhere and spend 15 minutes hunting for it.

At home, my wife gets on me because I say I’ll pay a bill or call someone back and then just forget. I leave the house for errands and come back with only half the stuff. Even when I’m “relaxing,” like watching a game, I can’t just sit—I’m up, pacing, checking my phone, fiddling with stuff. In lines or meetings, I get really antsy and want to jump in or move ahead; waiting my turn feels almost unbearable.
option:
A. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)

question:
It’s been over a year now, maybe a year and two months, since this really took hold. I’m a hospice caregiver, and I’m used to a lot, but this… I just can’t deal with it anymore. It’s this intense, gut-wrenching fear of vomiting. Not mine, but anyone’s. I know it sounds silly, but the thought of it, even seeing someone look a bit green, just paralyzes me. My body just… freezes up. I can’t move, can’t think.

I've started avoiding so many things. Eating out is a nightmare – what if someone gets sick? Public transport? Forget it. I find myself constantly scanning people around me, looking for any sign. I even call in sick to work more often now, which is terrible because my patients need me, but if there's any mention of a stomach bug going around, I just can't bring myself to go. It's truly impacting every part of my life since the divorce; I feel so isolated.
option:
A. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    B. Body Dysmorphic Disorder
    C. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    D. Specific Phobia

question:
"It's been close to a year now, and I just feel... wrong. Like my insides are rotting, you know? Sometimes I catch a whiff of something awful, like something’s burning, but nobody else smells it. It starts small, but it gets to where I swear my blood is turning to sludge. It’s hard to put it into words, my thoughts just get all jumbled up, like a word salad. One minute I'm trying to explain what's happening to my body, and the next, I'm stuck in some strange position, my arm frozen in the air.

I used to love tearing things down, getting my hands dirty, but now? I can barely drag myself out of bed. Even when I do make it to a site, I just stand there, can't motivate myself to do anything. I pace a lot, can't sit still, picking at my cuticles till they bleed. I’m riddled with guilt over everything, things that aren't even my fault. My mind keeps going back to how I could just end it all, clear plans forming, thinking about how easy it would be. This whole thing started around seven months ago, taking all the joy out of life, and it hasn't let up since. But there were a couple of weeks in there, before this heavy fog settled in, where I was only dealing with the smells and that feeling of my body being wrong, without all the sadness and lack of motivation."
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder
    B. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question:
It’s been about two months now, and honestly, it’s been… a lot. One minute I feel like I'm on top of the world, buzzing with ideas for new designs, practically bouncing off the walls, and the next, the smallest thing just sets me off, making me so snappy with everyone. I barely sleep anymore, maybe 3-4 hours if I'm lucky, but I wake up feeling totally fine, sometimes even better than when I actually got a full night's rest.

The energy is relentless. I find myself constantly moving, needing to do something, anything. The downside is I can't seem to focus on one thing. My mind just races, jumping from one project to another, or from a design concept to what I need to buy at the grocery store. It’s impossible to sit still and really dive deep into a task – my attention just scatters. It's really starting to mess with my work, and frankly, my friendships too.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Bipolar II Disorder
    D. Bipolar I Disorder

question:
"It's been… I guess over three years now. Just this constant low hum of sadness. It's not like a deep, dark depression all the time, but it's always there, like a gloomy cloud hanging over everything. My wife, Sarah, she's noticed it too. Said I'm not myself.

Teaching used to be my life, but now, standing in front of my English class, it's a battle. My mind just drifts. I'll be explaining Shakespeare, and suddenly I've lost my train of thought, fumbling for words. The kids probably think I'm losing my mind. Even trying to read a simple article for school, I reread sentences over and over.

And the self-doubt, it's crushing. I used to be so confident in my lesson plans, my ability to connect with students. Now, I second-guess every decision, every syllabus change. I feel like I'm barely keeping my head above water, and I don't trust myself to do even the simplest things well anymore. It's exhausting."
option:
A. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    B. Bipolar I Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question:
"I've been feeling so scatterbrained lately, for over a year now, I'd say about a year and a month. It's really impacting my work – I'm a wedding dress designer, so details are everything, but I keep making these silly, careless mistakes. It's like my brain just… drifts. I'll start a pattern, get an idea, and then completely lose my train of thought. Following through on a design concept from start to finish is a struggle; I'll get halfway and then get distracted by a sudden thought about a new fabric or even just my grocery list.

It's not just work, though. Even when I'm just trying to relax, I feel this almost constant restlessness, like I can't quite settle. My mind just jumps from one unrelated thing to another. I remember even as a kid, I struggled with this, always daydreaming in class. It's just so frustrating now that it's affecting my livelihood."
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    B. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)

question:
It’s been over a year and three months now, and I just feel… off. Like, I go through phases. For about a year now, I've had these surges where I’m incredibly productive, sometimes working on three different design projects at once, barely sleeping, and feeling absolutely fantastic. I'll reach out to everyone I know, even people I haven't spoken to in ages, and sometimes I do things I probably shouldn’t. But then, for the last nine months or so, things get really weird.

I started hearing voices, usually whispers, saying my creative director, Mark, is madly in love with me, even though he's never shown it. It's distracting. My thoughts get jumbled, skipping from one idea to another in a way that doesn’t always make sense to others. And it's hard to even start basic tasks, like getting myself to go to the grocery store.

Then, for the past six months, even with the voices sometimes still there, a really heavy sadness sets in. I lose all motivation, my movements feel heavy, like I'm wading through mud. My sleep is broken and I wake up frequently. The only thing that really changes is my appetite; I find myself eating much more than usual. It’s hard to keep up with work or even my personal life when I'm swinging between these extremes.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    D. Bipolar I Disorder

question:
It started maybe... a little over a year ago? Like a year and four months. I remember the exact day because I woke up and felt like something was off in my skin. Since then, I’ve been absolutely sure there’s something wrong inside my body—like some kind of parasite or growth under the skin that nobody’s picking up.

I know how it sounds, but I can feel it. Sometimes it’s like crawling or pulling under my arms or around my ribs, almost like tiny wires. I’ve gone to urgent care, my PCP, even a dermatologist. Blood work, scans, whatever—they all come back “normal.” They tell me it’s anxiety or something, but they’re not the ones feeling this.

I still work most days; I can focus enough to draw and do tattoos, but I’m constantly distracted, checking my skin in the bathroom between clients, pressing on spots to see if anything moves. I’ve stopped dating because I’m convinced there’s something seriously wrong with me and I don’t want to drag anyone into it.
option:
A. Delusional Disorder
    B. Schizophrenia
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)

question:
I’ve been struggling like this for about a year now, maybe a bit more—around fourteen months—but honestly it feels familiar, like how I was as a kid, just… louder now.

At work, I can’t seem to keep anything organized. My office is piles of half-finished papers, sticky notes, and open tabs. I start prepping a lecture, then suddenly I’m answering emails, then I’m rewriting a syllabus, and nothing actually gets finished on time. Just sitting down to grade or read dense articles feels almost physically painful—I’ll do anything to put it off, even stupid things like color-coding my bookshelf.

My mind jumps constantly. I’ll be in a meeting and realize I’ve been thinking about dinner plans or some random memory instead of the discussion. Every little noise pulls me away.

Physically, I’m restless. I keep bouncing my leg or tapping my pen in class, even when I’m trying not to. In faculty meetings, I blurt things out before others finish, and in conversations—even with my husband—I catch myself cutting people off or jumping in because I can’t seem to wait my turn. It’s starting to cause real problems at work and at home.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    D. Adjustment Disorder

question: The last few days have just been a blur, honestly. It’s only been about three days since I got that news about the project funding falling through, but it feels like weeks. Usually, I'm on top of everything, running a firm like mine, you have to be. But lately? I just… stopped caring. I haven't even wanted to go into the office, which is unheard of for me. I've been cancelling meetings, letting calls go to voicemail. My assistant must think I've fallen off the face of the earth. Even when I am home, I just feel this heavy cloud. No energy, no interest in anything. I've been having these headaches too, dull but constant, and my stomach feels tied in knots. I know it's probably the stress, but it's more than just stress this time.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Anorexia Nervosa
    C. Major Depressive Disorder
    D. Adjustment Disorder

question:
I’ve just been feeling so overwhelmed lately—this whole thing with my mom’s illness really hit me hard. It’s been about two months since she moved in with me after her stroke, and at first I thought I could manage. I’m used to helping others, you know, being a social worker—it’s my job to stay steady. But I’ve been crying all the time, sometimes for no reason, and I just feel… heavy. Like I can’t get back to who I used to be.  

I’ve lost interest in things I actually enjoy—stopped going to my book club, haven’t been out with friends in weeks. Some days, getting out of bed feels pointless. I keep thinking, “If she gets worse, what will I do?” I feel guilty when I feel low because I should be stronger, especially since I help others cope with similar things. But I’m not coping. I’ve been snapping at my coworkers, which isn’t like me.  

It’s not like I’m completely falling apart, but it’s constant—the sadness, the worry. It started shortly after she moved in, and it hasn’t let up. I thought I’d adjust by now, but I just feel stuck.
option:
A. Adjustment Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    C. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question: I’ve been having these awful moments where I’m right back in that trauma bay—it was supposed to be a routine shift, but this young kid came in after a shooting, and we couldn’t save him. I keep *reliving* it—eyes wide open, hands shaking like I’m holding the gauze again, like it’s happening *right now*. Sometimes I don’t even know where I am for a few seconds. It’s like I’m trapped there. I can’t stop thinking I should’ve done something different. I *knew* his pulse was fading, but I froze—now I can’t shake the guilt. Nothing feels good anymore—not my favorite show, not even coffee in the morning. And my temper? I yelled at a patient’s family over a simple question last week. It’s not like me. But the worst change is that I can’t focus like I used to. Charts blur, meds get double-checked three times. I’ve always been sharp on the floor, but since that night… my brain just doesn’t track. Before that event, I never had trouble paying attention—ever. Now it’s like my mind’s underwater.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    D. Acute Stress Disorder

question:
It’s been about two weeks since the accident at the warehouse, and I still feel like I’m not right in the head. I remember bits of it—the screech, someone yelling—but most of it is just a blank. My wife keeps asking me what exactly happened, and I honestly can’t fill in the whole thing; it’s like big chunks are missing.

When I even start to think about that day, my chest tightens, I get sweaty, and my heart starts pounding, so I try not to think about it at all. I avoid driving past the site, avoid talking about work with anyone. If my coworkers bring it up, I shut it down or walk away.

I jump at every loud noise now—if a pallet drops or a car backfires, I flinch so hard people stare. A few days ago, my son dropped a pot in the kitchen and I spun around and shoved him without even thinking. Another time I slammed a box into the wall so hard it cracked. That’s not like me. Even when good things happen—like my daughter bringing home a promotion—I just feel flat, like I can’t feel happy about anything anymore.
option:
A. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    D. Acute Stress Disorder

question: "Lately, I just can't seem to shake this constant feeling of unease. It’s like my mind is a broken record, constantly replaying the same thoughts over and over again. It’s not about what’s going to happen tomorrow or next week, it’s these specific, unwanted thoughts, impulses and images that just pop into my head. They're so intrusive, they interrupt whatever I'm doing, making it impossible to concentrate. I’m finding myself sleeping a lot more than I used to, sometimes for ten or eleven hours and I still wake up feeling utterly drained. This chronic fatigue has been going on for months now, making even simple tasks feel like climbing a mountain. I just want some peace in my own head."
option:
A. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    B. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    D. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
I’ve been feeling completely overwhelmed since my dad passed away six months ago—grief was expected, but this feels like so much more. At first, I thought I’d just adjust, but instead, I’ve lost interest in everything, even things I used to love like gardening or reading. I don’t sleep well—either I can’t fall asleep or I wake up at 3 a.m. and just lie there. I’ve lost about 12 pounds because I barely eat; even forgetting to eat isn’t unusual when I’m at the pharmacy. My focus is shot—I’ve caught myself double-checking prescriptions three or four times, which isn’t like me. I feel worthless, like I’m failing at everything, even though people say I’m doing fine.  

Lately, I’ve started drinking more—usually a whole bottle of wine by myself—and I drove home from a friend’s place after drinking a couple weeks ago. I know it was reckless, but I just didn’t care. I’ve had thoughts that life isn’t worth living, though I haven’t made any plans. This isn’t just reacting to my dad’s death—it’s constant, every day, and it’s taking over.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder
    B. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Adjustment Disorder
    D. Bipolar II Disorder

question:
I’ve been really struggling with my eating the last several months. Since around the spring, at least once or twice a week I’ll have these episodes, usually in the late afternoon or at night, when I just…lose it. I’ll eat an entire frozen dinner, then bread with butter, cookies, ice cream, whatever I can find, all within maybe an hour. It’s far more than anyone my age would normally eat. Once I start, I feel like I can’t stop, even when I’m uncomfortably full. It feels almost automatic, like I’m not in control of my hands.

I usually do this when I’m alone at home. If my daughter is visiting, I wait until she leaves or goes to bed. I’m too ashamed of how much I’m eating to let anyone see. Afterward, I sit there feeling disgusted with myself, guilty, and low, sometimes tearful.

I do have occasional days where I feel a bit more energetic or talkative than usual, maybe get more housework done, but I don’t have those long stretches of being “wired” or overly cheerful for days that people describe. It’s really the eating that’s out of control.
option:
A. Bipolar II Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    C. Acute Stress Disorder
    D. Binge-Eating Disorder

question:
I’m a script writer at a local TV station, and about three months ago something really shifted. I’ll go through these “up” phases that last a week or two where I barely sleep, maybe three hours a night, but I feel wired, like I’ve had ten coffees. During those times I can’t slow down. I’m literally pacing around the writers’ room, jumping from desk to desk, talking a mile a minute. My coworkers joke that I’m “possessed,” but it’s actually hard for me to sit still even for a meeting.

When I’m in that state, I start making stupid mistakes in my scripts—wrong names, missing scenes, continuity errors—things I normally catch easily. My boss gives clear notes, but I’ll start revising, then abandon it halfway and chase a “brilliant new angle” instead, so deadlines get blown. I’ll cut people off constantly in story meetings, talk over them, finish their sentences, or derail the conversation with some idea that just popped into my head.

Between these high periods I’m much more like my usual self: I can focus, sit through meetings, follow directions, and I’m not nearly as restless or intrusive. The inattention and this motor-like energy only seem to show up when I’m in those intense, elevated phases.
option:
A. Body Dysmorphic Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Specific Phobia
    D. Bipolar I Disorder

question: It’s been pretty rough lately, Doc. For about two months now, I keep smelling this really foul, rotten smell, especially at home, but no one else seems to notice it. And with my girlfriend… it’s been really difficult. Things just started shifting. I became completely convinced she was cheating on me. Every late text, every time she was a little busy, it felt like undeniable proof, even though she swore nothing was going on. It’s hard to remember a time before these thoughts started taking over my head. Before all this, I was feeling down, really low energy, and just couldn't enjoy my usual driving routes for a few weeks, but those feelings have kind of come and gone, while the worries about my girlfriend and that smell are constant now.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Schizophrenia
    C. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder

question: I don't even know where to begin, doctor. It's like I’m constantly on edge, like a coil wound too tight. Every little noise makes me jump out of my skin – my husband says it’s like I’m waiting for something bad to happen. And the worry… it’s just always there, a thick fog. It's not just about work, although deadlines definitely make it worse. But it's also about my family, our finances, everything. My mind just keeps replaying things, especially that car accident six months ago. Before that, I wasn’t like this. I used to sleep soundly, but now I’m awake by 4 AM most mornings after just a few hours. I just can’t fall back asleep. It all started after the accident.
option:
A. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    C. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    D. Delusional Disorder

question:
I’ve been feeling incredible lately—like I’m tapped into something way bigger than everyone else. I honestly believe I’ve cracked the market in a way no one else can see. I’ve been telling my wife I’m going to start my own global financial network, maybe even run a country’s economy. I know that sounds crazy, but I *know* I have this special insight—like I was chosen.  

For the past three weeks, I haven’t needed much sleep—maybe two or three hours a night—and I’m still full of energy. I’ve been making big trades, calling investors at all hours, setting up meetings. My mind’s racing, and I feel like I can predict world events before they happen. Even sounds and colors seem more intense—sometimes I hear voices whispering in the static of the TV, saying I’m destined for greatness.  

But here’s the thing—none of this started until this surge of energy and confidence hit. Before this, I wasn’t having these ideas. My wife says I’m only like this now, not before. It’s like these beliefs only show up when I’m in this super-charged state. Once I slow down, I start questioning if any of it’s real.
option:
A. Schizophrenia
    B. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Delusional Disorder
answer: C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
It’s mainly about spiders. I know a lot of people don’t like them, but this feels… extreme. For at least the past three years, any time I even picture a spider—like if someone at work mentions one, or there’s a photo on their phone—I get this rush of panic. My chest tightens, my hands sweat, and I can’t think about anything else except, “What if there’s one near me?”

It’s worse at home. I live alone now since the divorce, and I’ll avoid certain corners, the basement, even the mailbox room if I think there might be webs. I’ll walk an extra block to avoid bushes or trees that look “spidery.” I’m not scared they’re, like, talking to me or that they have some special power or anything; I’m scared they’ll crawl on me or bite me. That’s it. Just that.

If I absolutely have to go somewhere I’m worried about—like my storage unit—I’ll only go if my sister comes with me. I practically cling to her, or I’ll grab a can of bug spray and hold it the whole time, like a security blanket, just to get through it.
option:
A. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    C. Schizophrenia
    D. Specific Phobia

question:
It’s a bit confusing to untangle the timeline, but I’ll try.  

About a year ago, I went through a stretch of maybe two months where I was… not myself at all. I had this huge surge of energy, like I couldn’t sit still at the reference desk, pacing the aisles, reorganizing shelves that didn’t need it. My mood swung around a lot—one minute I’d feel almost giddy, chatting nonstop with patrons and calling old classmates I hadn’t spoken to in years, and then I’d snap and get irritated over tiny things. I was talking so much my coworkers couldn’t get a word in. I also ordered a ridiculous amount of books and vintage editions online—spent way beyond what I could afford and maxed my credit card.

But that phase ended months ago, and I haven’t had that kind of energy since. What hasn’t gone away are these ideas and sensations. I’m convinced one of our regular patrons is secretly in love with me—little things he does feel like secret messages, even though he barely speaks. And almost every day I get these strange tastes in my mouth, like metal or rotten fruit, even when I’m not eating.

Lately, for about five months, I’ve felt mostly flat and hollow. I used to love reading and cataloguing rare texts, but now I just stare at the pages. My thoughts feel slow and murky, like my brain is wading through fog. It’s hard to keep up with work, and I mostly go home to my apartment and sit there, not really feeling much of anything.
option:
A. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    C. Schizophrenia
    D. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
Honestly, I feel like I’ve been “on fast‑forward” my whole life. My parents used to get called into school because I could never stay in my seat, talked nonstop, and blurted things out. That restless, wired feeling never really went away; it’s just more of a problem now.

Over the last three months it’s gotten worse. I’m snapping at clients and my ex over tiny things, like an email phrased the wrong way. I feel constantly revved up: I’ll open five documents at once, start outlining three different books, then get up and pace around the apartment, tapping my fingers, walking back and forth. I can’t just sit and write; I’m always fidgeting or wandering, even when I don’t need to.

When I talk, I barrel through sentences so fast people ask me to slow down, and on calls I cut people off without meaning to. My thoughts jump ahead so quickly that I’m already on the next idea before anyone finishes their sentence. Sleep has never been great—I’ve always felt like I don’t need much—but lately I’m up late working, up early again, still buzzing. It’s starting to really mess with deadlines and relationships.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    B. Delusional Disorder
    C. Bipolar II Disorder
    D. Bipolar I Disorder

question: Lately, it’s been a real struggle to keep my head straight, doctor. I’m 32 now, an archivist, and frankly, my work is starting to pile up because I just can’t focus like I used to. Things feel…off. I try to listen when my boss is talking, but it's like the words just float past me, especially when there are other things going on. I'll realize I've completely missed half of what they said. It's embarrassing. I’ve always been quite organized for an archivist, but now I’m constantly misplacing important documents, my keys, my wallet—things I absolutely need. It just adds to the chaos. Honestly, the thought of tackling a big inventory project, something that requires sustained focus, makes me feel completely overwhelmed and I just avoid it. It’s like my brain just can’t process information the way it used to, and this all really started getting bad around the same time I began hearing those whispers and seeing things in my peripheral vision, you know? It wasn’t like this when I was younger; this inattention, it really became noticeable after these other strange experiences began.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Schizophrenia
    C. Body Dysmorphic Disorder
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)

question:
I’ve been feeling overwhelmed for about six months now, ever since my daughter moved overseas. At first, I told myself I was just adjusting, but it hasn’t settled. I keep thinking, *What if she gets sick? What if something happens and I can’t get to her in time?* But it’s not just that—my worries float to everything: my husband’s health, our finances, even small things like whether I turned off the stove. I can’t seem to shut it off. 

I used to enjoy reading or helping at the church library, but now I get mentally exhausted just trying to follow a paragraph. My mind races even when nothing’s happening. I’ll sit down to pay bills and suddenly I’m trapped in a loop of “what ifs” about the stock market crashing or the house burning down. 

Yes, my daughter moving was hard, but this feels bigger than that. Even when life is calm, my brain acts like I’m in danger. I wake up tired because my mind’s already going. It’s not just reacting to one thing—it’s like the worry lives in me now.
option:
A. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    B. Adjustment Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Schizophrenia

question:
Lately, I’ve just felt completely overwhelmed—like nothing matters anymore. It’s been this way for over two months. I used to love going into work, but now I call in sick more than I show up. My wife keeps asking what’s wrong, but I can’t even explain it. I feel tired all the time, worthless, like I’m dragging my crew down. Some days, I don’t get out of bed until noon. I haven’t slept well in weeks—either I can’t fall asleep or I wake up and just stare at the ceiling. I’ve lost interest in everything, even spending time with my wife.

And then there’s this eating thing—mostly at night, when I’m alone. I’ll eat two or three whole meals’ worth of food, even when I’m not hungry. Just keep going until I feel sick. But it’s not about the food. It’s more like… I don’t care. It numbs me for a second. But then the guilt hits, and it makes the sadness worse. I’ve never been like this before. I’ve always been the one everyone leans on—now I can’t even take care of myself.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Major Depressive Disorder
    C. Specific Phobia
    D. Binge-Eating Disorder

question:
It’s been a rough couple of years, Doc. Since my wife passed, things have just… fluctuated. My body feels heavy, like I'm dragging around cinder blocks. Even little things, like butchering a side of beef, exhaust me. And mentally, I just feel hollow inside, like there’s nothing there. I've become completely indifferent to everything I used to be proud of. I find myself pacing around the house, picking at my skin, unable to just sit still. That’s been going on for over a year now.

But before all that, for about three months, I was a coiled spring. I'd snap at anyone for the smallest thing. I felt like I had boundless energy, started doing all sorts of renovations on the house, even picked up extra shifts, and barely slept a wink without feeling tired. I know all this started after the accident, but my irritability and the crazy energy bursts and sleepless nights, those come and go in waves. They don't feel like they're just tied to what happened. I just get like that sometimes, then crash.
option:
A. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Schizophrenia
    D. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder

question:
I’ve been struggling for about three months now—probably once a week, sometimes more. I’m alone most evenings, working late at the library and then heading home to an empty apartment. That’s usually when it happens. I’ll go to the grocery store after work, not because I’m hungry, but because my mind just *fixates* on food. I’ll buy cookies, chips, frozen meals—way too much—and eat it all in one stretch, even when I’m not hungry. I keep eating until I’m painfully full, slumped on the couch, feeling numb.  

The worst part is what comes after—the shame. I can’t stand the idea of gaining weight, so I make myself vomit. I know it’s unhealthy, but it feels like the only way to “undo” it. Sometimes I’ll over-exercise too, even at night, just to cancel it out. These cycles keep repeating. I don’t want to live like this, but it’s like I’m trapped. The binging and purging happen together, almost like clockwork, and they’ve become *regular*—not just random slips. I feel like I’ve lost control, but only over this one thing, and it’s ruining everything.
option:
A. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    B. Binge-Eating Disorder
    C. Bulimia Nervosa
    D. Persistent Depressive Disorder

question:
About two months ago I was working on a renovation job and a section of old ceiling collapsed. A big chunk narrowly missed my head and smashed the ladder under me. I didn’t get badly hurt, just bruised and shaken, but since then I haven’t really been the same.

For the first week or so after it happened I was jumpy, but it wasn’t like I was reliving it over and over or anything like that. I never had those full-on flashbacks people talk about, and I don’t wake up in a panic or drenched in sweat. It’s more that now, whenever I’m under a ceiling that looks old or unstable, or even just hear a loud crack from a pipe or beam, I get this knot in my stomach and my mind goes straight back to that job. I feel tense and on edge until I leave.

Sleep’s been lousy. I go to bed at a decent hour and stay asleep most nights, but I wake up feeling like I haven’t rested at all, like I’ve been working all night. This has been going on pretty steadily since that accident, and it’s really wearing me down at work and at home with my wife.
option:
A. Adjustment Disorder
    B. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    C. Acute Stress Disorder
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)

question:
I guess this all really started around six months ago. I work remotely doing penetration testing, so I’m on my laptop basically all the time, but lately I just… don’t care. Stuff that used to be fun, like bug bounties or CTFs, feels pointless. I’m exhausted all the time, even if I sleep 10–12 hours, and it’s hard to even start simple tasks. I miss deadlines, then beat myself up over it.

My mood’s just flat or low most days. I feel guilty, like I’m letting my team down and wasting my life. I’ve had a lot of thoughts like, “What’s the point of any of this?” Not exactly planning anything, but I think about not being here more than I’m comfortable admitting.

My appetite basically disappeared. Some days I’ll realize it’s 6 p.m. and I’ve only had coffee. I’ve dropped a noticeable amount of weight because I just don’t feel like eating, or it seems like too much effort. It’s not about trying to be thinner or worrying about getting fat—I honestly don’t care how I look right now. It’s more that nothing, including food, feels worth the energy.
option:
A. Bulimia Nervosa
    B. Bipolar I Disorder
    C. Anorexia Nervosa
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question:
I’ve been struggling for about a year now—maybe longer—with these thoughts that just won’t leave me alone. Every time I touch the crane’s controls, or even shake my wife’s hand, I get this overwhelming fear that I’ve picked up something dirty—germs, chemicals, who knows what. It doesn’t matter if it’s logical or not; the worry hits like a shock. I’m not even sure when it started, but now I can’t stop thinking I’m covered in something dangerous that I might spread to my family.

Because of that, I’ve developed this routine—washing my hands, then disinfecting them, then washing again. If I don’t do it *just right*, I feel like something bad will happen. I’ve even started cleaning the doorknobs and light switches at home multiple times a day. My wife’s noticed, and she’s worried, but I can't explain it in a way that makes sense—even to myself.

The odd thing is, these thoughts come whether I’m feeling down or not. I’ve had low moods, sure, but the hand-washing and cleaning rituals? They’re there all the time, even on days I feel okay. It’s like my brain insists I have to do them to keep everyone safe.
option:
A. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question:
I’ve been having these awful dreams lately—night after night, I’m back at the scene of that car accident from two years ago. I see my husband, the twisted metal, the flashing lights… it feels so real. I wake up sweating and can’t fall back asleep. Since he passed, I just feel on edge all the time. I jump at the smallest noises—the mail slot clinking, the furnace turning on. I’m always braced for something terrible to happen, even though I know logically I’m safe here in my home.

I used to enjoy knitting for the grandkids, going to church potlucks, even gardening. Now nothing gives me joy. When one of my sons told me how proud he was of my afghans, I just stared at them. I didn’t feel anything. I want to feel happy, but it’s like that part of me shut down. And sometimes, I get furious—out of nowhere. Last week, I screamed at my daughter because she rearranged the silverware drawer. I didn’t mean to, but the anger just exploded.

It’s not just sadness—it’s like my whole life is haunted by that moment in the ambulance when I realized he was gone. That accident changed everything. The dreams, the fear, the anger—it’s all tied to it. I’ve felt this way most days for over a year now.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder
    B. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Acute Stress Disorder

question:
It’s been over a year now, about fifteen months I guess, that I’ve felt this heavy shroud over everything. I just can’t seem to stop the tears from flowing; sometimes I’m crying right there on the subway platform, even with people around. All I want to do is sleep. I come home from my night shift and just collapse, sometimes sleeping for 12, 14 hours, and I'm still exhausted. My mind feels so foggy, like I’m walking through a daze all the time. It's hard to think clearly, to even concentrate on simple tasks. Honestly, there are days I just wish I wouldn't wake up at all. It’s not that I’d ever hurt myself, but the thought of just not existing is… appealing.

This deep slump has been the worst for sure, but that's what makes me so confused about everything. Before this, about two years back, there was a period… I felt on top of the world. For a few weeks, I barely slept, maybe two or three hours a night, but I had boundless energy. I was so chatty, talking a mile a minute, convinced I was going to turn my subway cleaning route into a profitable business, making all sorts of grand plans. I spent a lot of money, more than I had, on things I didn't need, feeling incredibly optimistic and impulsive. It felt amazing at the time, like nothing could stop me. Eventually, that just… crashed. This current sadness feels directly connected to that intense high.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder
    B. Specific Phobia
    C. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    D. Bipolar I Disorder

question:
I’ve been feeling completely drained for over a year—about a year and a half now. It started after my dad passed away, and at first, I thought it was just grief, but it hasn’t lifted. I work nights as a lighting tech for theater productions, but lately, I can’t muster the energy to prep for shows. I’ll lie in bed at 3 a.m., wide awake, even after getting off work, my mind racing over little mistakes I’ve made—like that time I missed a cue light last season or the way I snapped at a coworker when I was stressed. I keep turning those moments over and over, hating myself for them.  

I can’t sit still, either. My hands fidget constantly—picking at my cuticles, rubbing my forehead—even when I’m trying to relax. Some days I pace my apartment for no reason. I used to love designing lighting setups, but now I can’t even get myself to answer work emails. I feel guilty about letting people down, but pushing through feels impossible. The low mood, the restlessness, the sleep—none of it’s gotten better, even though the initial stress around my dad’s passing has, in a way, settled. It’s like the sadness took root and never left.
option:
A. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    B. Adjustment Disorder
    C. Major Depressive Disorder
    D. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder

question:
It’s embarrassing to talk about, but this has really taken over my life the past year or so, especially the last 6–7 months. I’m convinced there’s something horribly wrong with how my face looks, especially my nose and skin. When I look in the mirror, I see this crooked, swollen nose and pores that look like craters. I know people tell me they don’t see it, but it feels so obvious and deformed to me that I can’t believe them.

Because of that, I spend forever in front of the mirror before every shift, trying different angles, makeup tricks, taking pictures and zooming in over and over. I’ll redo my makeup multiple times, pick at any tiny bump until I make it worse, and change my hairstyle repeatedly to try to hide my face. I keep checking reflective surfaces at work—med room cabinets, windows—sometimes every few minutes. I avoid bright lights and standing close to patients or coworkers so they won’t notice.

On top of that, I’ve become convinced one of the doctors on my floor is secretly attracted to me—like I’ll read into every casual comment or glance as proof he’s into me, even though logically I know that doesn’t really make sense.
option:
A. Body Dysmorphic Disorder
    B. Delusional Disorder
    C. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    D. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
I’ve been crying a lot lately—sometimes for no reason at all. It’s gotten to the point where I’ll start tearing up while helping a patient, and I have to step out so they don’t see. This low mood… it’s not new. Honestly, I can’t remember a time in the past two and a half years when I truly felt okay. I used to think maybe after the divorce things would get better, but I just stopped believing that would ever happen. It feels like this is just who I am now—like I’m stuck.

I also keep having these awful dreams—my patients calling my name after they’ve passed, or showing up at my door even though they’re gone. I wake up shaky, heart racing, and then I can’t fall back asleep. The exhaustion makes everything heavier.  

I still go to work because those patients need me, but I feel numb most days. I push through, but it’s not like I’m functioning well—I just don’t see the point in trying to feel better because I honestly don’t think it’s possible. This has been my normal for so long… I didn’t even know it could be a problem until my sister mentioned it out of the blue.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    B. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    C. Major Depressive Disorder
    D. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
It’s so hard to even get dressed in the morning, which is a nightmare when you're a stylist. Nothing feels exciting anymore, even new collections. Mostly, I just feel this heavy sadness that’s been hanging over me for over a year now, dragging me down. Sometimes, I have these sudden urges to just step into traffic, or jump from my apartment window, even though I don't really want to die. It's like a fleeting, terrifying impulse.

But… it’s more than just the sadness. There are times, like a few months ago, when I was completely out of that dark mood, feeling almost normal, but I could still feel bugs crawling under my skin. It was so vivid, like tiny pinpricks everywhere. And I felt this terrifying conviction that the world was about to end, that a meteor was coming or something catastrophic. Those feelings, the bugs and the world ending, lasted for a good two or three weeks all by themselves, after my depressive mood had lifted. It's like they have a life of their own, separate from how down I'm feeling. Even now, most of the time, I'm stuck in that low, sad place, but it's important to know those other things happened independently.
option:
A. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    C. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    D. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
I’ve been having these nightmares every few nights since the warehouse collapse last month—reliving the whole thing, seeing my partner trapped, hearing his voice… I wake up drenched in sweat, heart racing. It’s like I’m back there. The worst part is, I can’t remember much of what actually happened during the rescue. Whole chunks are just blank. I keep trying to piece it together, but my mind just shuts down. My wife says I’ve changed. I don’t laugh at things I used to. Even when our baby smiled at me for the first time, I just… didn’t feel anything.  

And these thoughts—I can’t stop them. The beam falling, the smoke, the radio cutting out—they just crash into my head at random. Driving, feeding my son, showering—suddenly I’m back there. I know they’re about the event, not some weird fear or anything, and I don’t do rituals or anything to stop them. I just sit with it. My captain said I should come talk to someone. I’m not going crazy, but I’m not right either.
option:
A. Specific Phobia
    B. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    C. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    D. Acute Stress Disorder

question:
I’ve been feeling pretty good overall, honestly. Work’s been busy—overseeing shipments, managing schedules—but I handle it fine, maybe even better than most. My wife says I’ve changed a bit lately, but I think she just doesn’t understand what I’m going through. I’ve come to realize I’m not like other people. I get these intense thoughts—like messages coming to me through traffic patterns, delivery routes, even radio static. At first, I thought it was coincidence, but now I *know*—there’s a system, and I’m the only one who can interpret it. They’re grooming me for a higher role, guiding me to prevent major logistical disasters no one else sees coming.  

I don’t act on it—no strange rituals or anything. I don’t wash my hands repeatedly or lock doors eight times. I just think—deeply and constantly—about the patterns. My wife worries I’m obsessive, but I’m not doing anything to reduce anxiety. I’m not compelled to perform routines. These aren’t thoughts I fear and try to cancel out. They’re revelations. I function normally; I just know something big, and it’s only a matter of time before others recognize it too.
option:
A. Schizophrenia
    B. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    C. Delusional Disorder
    D. Specific Phobia

question:
I’ve been having these awful dreams—reliving that night over and over. It was six months ago now, when I got caught in that armed robbery at the convenience store near my office. I keep waking up sweating, heart racing, convinced someone’s breaking in. It’s not just the dreams, though. I’m on edge all the time, like danger’s right around the corner. I can’t relax, not even in my own home. I snap at people—my coworkers, my kid—just yell out of nowhere if someone startles me. Last week, I cursed out a client on the phone because they raised their voice slightly. I feel terrible after, but in the moment, I can’t stop it.

The worst part is… nothing feels good anymore. I closed the biggest policy of my quarter last month, and my team was cheering, but I just sat there numb. No pride, no joy—just empty. I used to take so much pride in providing for my daughter, but now it all feels meaningless. I’ve tried to move on, but it’s been half a year, and instead of getting better, it feels like I’m falling apart. I can’t sleep, I’m angry, and I don’t recognize myself.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    B. Acute Stress Disorder
    C. Specific Phobia
    D. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder

question:
I’ve been on edge for what feels like forever, but really intensely for the past year or so. My mind never shuts off. I’m constantly worrying about everything—my patients, whether I missed a dose of morphine, if my ex will try to change the custody agreement, money, even ridiculous things like whether a tiny mistake will get me fired. The anxiety is there from the moment I wake up until I finally crash.

When I get really anxious, it flips into this agitated, snappy version of me. For about a month a few months back, I was so keyed up I could barely sit during visits. I was pacing in patients’ rooms, talking too fast, volunteering to take extra shifts, convinced I could “handle it all” better than my coworkers. Inside it still felt like worry, like if I slowed down something terrible would happen.

Then I kind of collapsed into this two‑week stretch where I felt completely hollow. I’d go to work and feel like a fraud, like I was useless to my patients and my daughter would be better off without me. I kept thinking it’d be easier if I just didn’t wake up, though I didn’t make any plans. Both those times, my work and home life really suffered.
option:
A. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    B. Bulimia Nervosa
    C. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    D. Bipolar I Disorder

question:
I’ve been going over things again and again in my head, no matter what I’m doing. As a wedding photographer, I need to be so precise—double-checking my gear, angles, lighting—but lately, it’s gotten out of control. Even after I’ve locked my studio door, I’ll walk back three or four times because I can’t shake the feeling it’s open. I know it’s irrational, but the doubt is so strong it makes my chest tight.

When I can’t go back to check, I start silently counting in my head—always in sets of four—or repeating a phrase like “everything’s safe” over and over just to calm the anxiety. It started months ago, but now it’s every single day. I do it while editing photos, even during ceremonies. My husband says I’ve been distant, but it’s not sadness—it’s like my mind won’t stop racing with these intrusive thoughts about making a mistake, forgetting something, or someone getting hurt because I didn’t act. The thoughts come whether I’m happy or stressed, and I only feel temporary relief when I do the mental routines. It’s exhausting, but I can’t seem to stop.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    B. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question: I try to eat as little as possible, maybe 500 calories a day. I’m always weighing myself, sometimes ten times a day, to make sure the numbers keep dropping. If it goes up even a little, I start panicking. My husband keeps telling me I look frail, but I just don’t see it; I feel much more in control this way. I did pass out while teaching a lesson last week, and my hair has been falling out quite a bit, but I just brush it off. I’m also constantly thinking about what I’m going to eat, or rather, *not* eat. I meticulously plan my tiny meals, counting every single calorie and often skipping meals if I feel I’ve gone over my self-imposed limit. I know it seems like a lot of focus on food, but it’s all driven by this intense fear of gaining weight and how my body looks.
option:
A. Anorexia Nervosa
    B. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    C. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)

question:
I’ve been struggling a lot at work lately—double-checking designs feels impossible, and I keep missing small errors in schematics, which isn’t like me. I’ve even lost my security badge twice this month. My mind just races with random thoughts, and I get sidetracked constantly, even in meetings. I feel this inner restlessness, like I can’t sit still, even if I’m not moving much. I can’t relax—I tried reading or unwinding after work, but I end up pacing or starting new projects I don’t finish. I interrupt colleagues during stand-ups, and waiting for my turn in conversations feels unbearable.  

But the truth is, this didn’t start gradually. It hit me suddenly about three weeks ago—before that, I was focused, productive. Then I barely needed sleep, felt unstoppable, ideas pouring out, spending hours sketching wild new concepts I knew were risky. That’s when the distraction, restlessness, and impulsivity all flared. My boss pulled me aside because I volunteered the team for extra deadlines without consulting anyone. Now the crash is setting in. I realize how out of control that period felt—not just hyper, but *elevated* in a way I can’t explain. The inattention and hyperactivity weren’t constant before—only during this one intense phase.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Binge-Eating Disorder
    D. Bipolar I Disorder

question:
I’ve been struggling a lot lately, especially with food. I'm a fitness trainer, so people assume I’ve got everything together, but honestly, it’s been really hard to keep up that image. At least once a week—pretty much every week for the past three months—I’ll end up eating a huge amount of food in one sitting, like an entire pizza, a family-sized bag of chips, half a container of ice cream—stuff I wouldn’t normally touch. I don’t even enjoy it. I just feel out of control. I keep going until I’m in physical pain, so full I can barely move.  

I never do this around others. I wait until I’m alone at home because I’m so embarrassed by how much I eat. I feel disgusted afterward, sometimes even cry.  

Lately, there have been times where I feel wired—like I’ve got endless energy, can’t sleep, talk fast, and want to take on a million things at once. But it never lasts more than a couple of days, and I don’t think it’s full mania or anything. It’s not causing me to get into trouble or spend recklessly, and I don’t need someone to intervene. It just comes and goes. But the eating? That’s the real problem.
option:
A. Specific Phobia
    B. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Bipolar II Disorder
    D. Binge-Eating Disorder

question:
I’ve been working in the same factory for 21 years—quality control, keeping the lines accurate, making sure nothing slips. Lately, though, I can’t seem to finish a single inspection. I start checking one batch, then suddenly I’m convinced I need to reorganize the entire storage room, then redesign the workflow on the floor. My brain won’t stop—it’s like I’m running on electricity. I don’t sleep more than two hours a night, but I don’t feel tired. I feel *alive*.  

My wife… she’s been acting strange. I *know* she’s seeing someone. I see the way she answers her phone, the whispers. I can even feel them—his hands on her, touching her where only I should. Sometimes, I feel those same hands crawling up my legs at night, like bugs under the sheets, but when I turn on the light, there’s nothing.  

It’s been like this for about ten months—first the racing, the projects I never finish, the unstoppable energy. And the delusions and the sensations started a couple of months after that. But here’s the thing: whenever I’m not in these high bursts—when my mind slows down—the voices, the jealousy, the creeping skin—all of it stops too. They only come when I’m *up*. I don’t hear them in between. They only exist when the mania does.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
answer: A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
I’ve been feeling overwhelmed lately—just constantly on edge, like I can’t catch my breath. It’s not just one thing; it’s everything. I wake up anxious about choreography, what to eat, even what to wear to practice. Making simple choices feels impossible. Last week, I stood in front of my closet for 20 minutes staring at two jackets. My mind just freezes.

I used to live for dance—now I dread it. I don’t know when I stopped enjoying it, but it just feels like pressure, all the time. I had this dream a few times—I’m on stage, and everyone’s staring, but I can’t move. My body locks up. When I wake up, my heart’s pounding like I’m still there.

I get jumpy if someone taps me from behind—even my manager. I yelled once when he adjusted my shoulders during a photo. I know it’s not his fault, but sometimes I think, *Why did he push me there? Why didn’t he protect me?* But… that’s not really about him. I just feel broken.

The worst part is the anxiety—it’s always there, not just about the incident, but about everything: failing, being replaced, being alone. It’s not tied to one memory. It’s just… everywhere.
option:
A. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    B. Acute Stress Disorder
    C. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    D. Body Dysmorphic Disorder

question:
Lately, things have been really tough, doc. For nearly two years now, I've been feeling this constant buzz, like I'm wired. My wife says I snap at everyone, and she’s probably right, but it feels like the whole world is just moving too slow. I can’t sit still for a minute, always jumping from one thing to another at the plant, never quite finishing anything. Sleep? Who needs it? I’ve gone days without it and felt absolutely fine, even better, like I have endless energy.

The hard part is, with all this manic energy, I've also been getting these awful smells, like something's burning or rotting, but nobody else smells it. And I just know, deep down, that the world is ending. I keep telling my wife, it’s all coming to a catastrophic end, so what’s the point? This has been going on for a long time, the smells and the feeling the end is nigh started around the same time as this wired feeling, almost two years ago. I swear these strange smells and the sense of impending doom lasted for a couple of weeks even when my mood wasn’t racing, I clearly remember feeling just 'normal' for a while but still experiencing those things. Now, my mood is definitely high, but those weird smells and that certainty of the world ending are still very much with me. It’s making it impossible to do my job right; I’m constantly distracted and on edge.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)

question: I'm not sure what's going on, doctor. For the past couple of weeks now, I feel like I'm constantly on the go, like there's a motor inside me. I’m usually so careful with my work, but lately, I’ve been making silly mistakes, dropping dishes or forgetting orders entirely. It’s not like me to miss details. It’s also making it hard to follow through on my duties at the restaurant. My boss gets annoyed because I'll start cleaning one area and then jump to another without finishing the first. I find myself butting into conversations too, just blurting things out, which is embarrassing. My husband passed away a few years back, and I've had my ups and downs, but this feels different. It all just started alongside feeling really elevated and not needing much sleep at all. It feels like these problems with my focus and fidgeting really ramped up when I started feeling this buzzing energy and excitement that’s way more than just being in a good mood.
option:
A. Body Dysmorphic Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Specific Phobia
    D. Bipolar I Disorder

question: It’s been… I don’t even know how long, but things started getting really off before I, well, before I started seeing things, hearing voices that aren't there. I know that sounds crazy, but it’s true. It used to be just feeling a little out of it, like my mind was elsewhere, even when I was trying to focus on my patients. But after I started having these…hallucinations, it got so much worse. I can’t seem to follow instructions at work anymore, simple care plans just get jumbled in my head. My supervisor has had to pull me aside a few times because I've missed things, not finishing tasks. And my apartment? It’s a complete mess, I just can't seem to organize anything. There’s a constant buzz in me, like I’m always ready to jump to the next thing, but also completely drained. I try to avoid anything that needs real concentration because it just feels impossible.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    B. Specific Phobia
    C. Schizophrenia
    D. Bipolar II Disorder

question:
I’ve always been a big guy, but the way I eat now… it’s different. On the road, especially at night, I’ll pull into a truck stop and just… lose it. I’ll order two burgers, a large pizza, wings, a couple desserts, and I’ll keep going even when I know I’m already full. It’s like once I start, I can’t hit the brakes. I tell myself “just one more bite,” and then suddenly everything’s gone and my stomach hurts so bad I have to lean back in the cab and unzip my pants. I feel gross and ashamed afterward, like, “What the hell did I just do?”

This has been happening at least once or twice a week for the last few months, maybe longer. I do get down on myself a lot—feel guilty about the divorce, about my weight, worry I’m screwing up my health—but I’m still getting up, doing my routes, talking to my kids on the phone, handling my bills. I don’t stay in bed all day or stop caring about everything. It’s more that these eating blowouts are my way of numbing out when I’m alone on the road and feeling low.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Major Depressive Disorder
    C. Adjustment Disorder
    D. Binge-Eating Disorder

question: It’s hard to pinpoint exactly when it all started, but I just know things haven't been right for a long time now. After what happened at that recital hall last year—the fire alarm, the panic, the crush of people trying to get out—I just haven't been the same. It’s been well over a year now, and I still feel like I’m constantly looking over my shoulder. Every time I walk into a new building, I'm immediately scoping out the exits, just in case. I can't shake this dazed, foggy feeling in my head; it’s like my thoughts are wading through treacle. Even a little jump scare, like a car horn, sends my heart racing, and it takes absolutely forever for me to calm down. It truly feels like this is my new normal, and it's certainly been longer than just a month or two.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Acute Stress Disorder
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    D. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder

question:
I’ve been having these episodes for about three months now, at least once a week, sometimes more. It’s like something snaps when I get home from work. I’ll stand in the kitchen and just keep eating—whole pizzas, bags of chips, ice cream straight from the tub—way more than any of my mates would eat in one sitting. I eat so fast I barely taste it, and I only really stop when I feel sick or embarrassed.

Afterwards I feel disgusting and panicky about my weight. I’m on the scale all the time—before work, when I get home, before bed—and if the number’s even a bit higher, I freak out. I’ve even tried laxatives a couple of times after really bad nights, thinking it might undo what I ate, but it’s not something I do regularly. Mostly I just lie there feeling guilty and swearing I won’t do it again, and then the cycle repeats the next week. It’s starting to mess with my focus on site and I keep worrying people can tell I’m losing control.
option:
A. Bulimia Nervosa
    B. Anorexia Nervosa
    C. Binge-Eating Disorder
    D. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder

question:
"It's like I've got this constant energy buzzing under my skin, especially now. I just can’t sit still, even during service when I should be focused on plating. My wife always tells me I interrupt her, and I know I do it to my sous chefs too; I just blurt things out before they're done talking, or butt into conversations. I’ll walk away from my station in the middle of prep, just needing to move, even when I know I shouldn’t. People describe me as being 'on the go' constantly, like I'm wired differently.

And my mind... it jumps around so much. Following a recipe from start to finish without getting sidetracked? Impossible sometimes. My kitchen is a disaster zone; I struggle so much with organizing my tasks and ingredients. This isn't new, though; I've always been like this, even as a kid in school before I turned 12. Sometimes I get really irritable if things aren’t going my way, or have these intense bursts of feeling good and productive, but that feeling restless and impulsive, that’s always there, no matter how I'm feeling emotionally."
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder
    B. Delusional Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)

question: Lately, I just can’t stop thinking about my nose. I mean, I’ve always been self-conscious about it, but now it’s all I see. I spend hours looking in the mirror, trying to find the right angle to make it look less… crooked. Or sometimes, I completely avoid mirrors because it just makes me feel worse. I know it sounds silly, but I genuinely believe it’s terribly disfigured, even when my friends say they don't notice anything. This feeling, this constant anxiety, it's all tied to how I look, specifically my nose. I can't really focus on anything else because this worry keeps nagging at me, a persistent ache in my chest every time I think about my appearance. It's not like I'm worried about my job or anything else, it’s just this one thing, my nose, that consumes me.
option:
A. Body Dysmorphic Disorder
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    D. Binge-Eating Disorder

question:
It started about a year and a half ago, maybe longer. I was already under a lot of pressure from studying for the civil service exam, but this feels like something else.

I still get shaken up if something reminds me of that incident on the bus a few years back—the accident. Certain routes, braking sounds, even news about crashes make my heart race, and I go out of my way to avoid those buses or even talking about it. I remember the chaos right after, the shouting and broken glass, but there are blank spots—like I can’t piece together some parts of what exactly happened, only fragments.

But what worries me more now are these voices. They talk about me, comment on how I’m studying, sometimes argue with each other. They don’t have anything to do with the accident; it’s more like they’re criticizing my whole life, my future, even what I’m eating. Sometimes I’m sure my neighbors are plotting against me, whispering through the walls, even when it’s quiet. It’s getting harder to focus on my books, and I’ve been spending more and more time alone in my room, avoiding everyone.
option:
A. Acute Stress Disorder
    B. Schizophrenia
    C. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)

question:
I’ve always had a busy mind, but lately it’s been like my body can’t keep up with my thoughts. For the past three weeks, I haven’t needed more than four hours of sleep and I’m still wide awake at 3 a.m., sketching, redrawing, tweaking panels—I can’t stop. It’s like I’m charged with this energy I can’t shut off. I feel restless all the time, like I need to move, to *do*, even when I’m sitting at my desk. I’ve been interrupting my editor mid-call, blurting ideas before he finishes speaking. Last week I left my seat at a coffee meeting to pace while pitching my new arc—I couldn’t sit still.  

I used to manage deadlines okay, but now organizing my work feels impossible, even though I’m producing more than ever. I start five things at once—new characters, storylines—but struggle to focus long enough to finish. But here’s the thing: this didn’t start out of nowhere. It followed this intense high where everything felt *easy*, like I could draw forever and the ideas were endless. I think… this hyperness only shows up during these episodes. Before that, I’m usually fine. It’s not that I’ve always been like this—it’s that these bursts come with this wild, euphoric energy that everything depends on.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder
    B. Delusional Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)

question:
It’s like I’ve been living on a pendulum for the past year. I had this stretch, about three weeks long a couple months ago, where I felt “switched on.” My mood would flip from feeling on top of the world to snapping at coworkers over tiny things within the same hour. I had this restless energy, like I couldn’t sit still at my lab bench. I’d start processing one set of samples, then suddenly jump to reorganizing evidence logs, then sketching out a new project idea, and none of it actually got finished. My thoughts were racing, I was talking faster than usual, and I barely slept 3–4 hours a night but didn’t feel tired. That kind of thing only happens in those “up” phases—not all the time, and definitely not since I was a kid.

Most of the last four months have been the opposite. I’ve felt constantly low, like there’s this gray film over everything. Even landing a difficult case doesn’t feel good. I struggle to pick what to wear or what to eat. I keep replaying tiny mistakes at work and feel like I should be punished for them. A few weeks ago I hoarded some of my meds and seriously planned to overdose. It’s gotten hard to function at work and I’ve been calling in sick more often.
option:
A. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    D. Anorexia Nervosa

question:
It’s been going on for at least a year, but the last 6–7 months it’s gotten really disruptive.  

I’m an ER nurse, so my job is already chaotic, but my head feels even worse. I get these random, really disturbing thoughts that pop in out of nowhere—like suddenly imagining stabbing a patient with a needle on purpose, or doing something sexual with a coworker in a totally inappropriate way, or thinking I’ve offended God by just having those thoughts. They feel completely against who I am, but once they show up, I can’t shake them. I end up avoiding certain patients or instruments because I’m scared of what I might do, even though I’ve never actually acted on anything.  

To calm myself down, I’ve started lining up supplies at the bedside and in the med room in this really specific pattern and angle. If something is even slightly off, I feel this intense tension until I fix it—sometimes I’ll redo it three or four times, even when I’m short-staffed.
