I also obsess a lot about what I eat and my weight—counting calories, repeatedly checking nutrition labels—but it’s just one part of everything else going on in my head.
option:
A. Bulimia Nervosa
    B. Binge-Eating Disorder
    C. Anorexia Nervosa
    D. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder

question:
Lately, everything just feels… off. I’ve been feeling really down for about six months now, just a constant gloom that I can't shake. I’m always tired, even though I'm waking up hours before my alarm, staring at the ceiling. When people ask me how I'm doing, it’s like my brain just can’t form the words quickly enough. I’ve also put on a good bit of weight, which doesn’t help my mood.

Before this, though, things were different. For about a year, I was flying high. I felt amazing, like I could conquer anything. I was juggling so many clients, taking on new projects that my colleagues thought were impossible. I barely needed any sleep, just a few hours, and I'd wake up feeling completely refreshed, ready to tackle the day. That’s when the ideas started really flowing, but my speech sometimes got a bit wild. I’d be talking to clients, and the words would just connect based on their sound, like "client, reliant, defiant," rather than what I meant to say. I know I also spent way too much money during that time, just buying things impulsively without thinking about my already strained finances. It was hard to keep up with work sometimes because I felt so detached from others; I just wasn’t interested in socializing at all.

This past year, for about nine months of it, things got really strange during that high period. I started seeing figures lurking in the corners of my vision and became convinced that the world was going to end, and that a grand catastrophe was coming. It was all so vivid and real to me at the time, but the sightings and those chilling thoughts, they only really happened when I was feeling that intense energy, that elevated mood. They never popped up when I was feeling alright or even during this current low period. It's like they were part of the whole manic rush, if that makes sense.
option:
A. Binge-Eating Disorder
    B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
answer: B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
It’s been a really up‑and‑down year. For the last nine months or so I’ve mostly felt like I’m dragging myself through mud. I cry a lot, sometimes over nothing obvious—I'll just be sitting at my desk trying to read an article and suddenly I’m in tears. My body feels heavy, like I’ve run a marathon even if I’ve only walked to the kitchen. I’ve had to cut back a lot on my lab hours because I just don’t have the energy.

Sleep is awful. I get these really vivid, disturbing dreams—sometimes about failing my thesis defense or something happening to my family—and I wake up terrified and exhausted. That’s most nights.

But about three weeks this spring were completely different. I suddenly felt amazing—almost too good. I barely slept, maybe three or four hours, and still felt wired. My thoughts were racing so fast I could hardly keep up; I was talking quickly, jumping from one idea to another. I was super distractible but convinced I could redesign my entire project and finish two papers at once. I was way more outgoing than usual—emailing senior academics, organizing socials, constantly texting people. It didn’t really cause problems, but it was very unlike my normal self. Outside of those few weeks, I don’t have that kind of energy or those rapid thoughts at all.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Specific Phobia
    D. Persistent Depressive Disorder

question:
Honestly, doc, it's getting unbearable. I’m usually super logical, dissecting systems and solving complex problems, but this… I've tried to just push through it. Seeing blood, even just a tiny cut on my finger, sends me into a complete tailspin. I freak out at the thought of needing a shot for anything, and medical procedures? Forget about it. It makes my heart race and I get all lightheaded.

The worst part is, it's not even about some grand conspiracy or anything bizarre. I’m not imagining things in the shadows. It’s the actual blood, the needles, the thought of someone poking me, that absolutely terrifies me. I know it's irrational to some extent, but that reality is what gets to me. Even just thinking about getting a check-up next month has me constantly on edge, my stomach in knots. It’s been like this for as long as I can really remember, but lately, it’s really impacting my daily life. I've even avoided going to the dentist, which I know is stupid, but the anxiety just won't let up.
option:
A. Specific Phobia
    B. Schizophrenia
    C. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    D. Bipolar II Disorder

question:
I’ve always been the “too much” person, even as a kid. My teachers used to send notes home because I never stopped talking, blurted things out, and could not sit still. That same restless, wired feeling has basically been my baseline my whole life. My brain runs like 20 tabs open at once, I jump from topic to topic, and I talk fast even when I’m just in a normal mood. I’ve never really needed much sleep; since grade school I’d be up half the night reading or pacing, then still buzzing the next day.

The last couple of months, though, that usual restlessness went into overdrive. At work I can’t stay in meetings, I’m roaming the halls, constantly fidgeting. I’ve been more flirty and impulsive than usual, even hooked up with a coworker at a conference, which isn’t like me and has already complicated things at the hospital.

Then about three months ago, I crashed. I started pulling away from friends and skipping family calls. I dread going to social events. I get awful nightmares several nights a week and wake up exhausted. Even picking what to wear or what to order for lunch can paralyze me. Sometimes I find myself thinking it would be easier if I just didn’t wake up, though I’m not planning anything. It’s all really affecting my work and relationships.
option:
A. Delusional Disorder
    B. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    D. Bipolar I Disorder

question:
I’ve been struggling lately—mostly with my eating. Over the past few months, I’ve had these episodes where I eat a huge amount of food in just a couple of hours, like an entire pizza or a big box of cookies, even when I’m not hungry. I know it’s more than most people would eat, and I can’t seem to stop. I keep going until I feel really uncomfortable, sometimes even bloated and in pain. It’s embarrassing, so I usually wait until my husband goes to bed or pretend I’ve already eaten. I’ve been doing this at least once a week for about three months now.

I’ll admit, I’ve also had periods where I feel unusually energetic, like I don’t need much sleep and I’m cleaning or organizing for hours late at night. But those don’t last long, and I wouldn’t say I’ve been reckless or had extreme mood swings, or anything like that. It’s not like I’m spending money wildly or having racing thoughts for days. The eating is what’s really bothering me—it’s out of control, and I feel so guilty afterward.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    C. Binge-Eating Disorder
    D. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
Lately, it feels like I'm stuck in a never-ending loop, doctor. For over a year now, I just haven't been myself. Things I used to love doing, like my weekend gardening or even just catching up with friends, they just don't spark joy anymore. Getting through my administrative work at the office has become a Herculean task; my mind feels so heavy and sluggish. Even walking down the hall, I feel like I'm moving through molasses. And the fatigue… it’s not just physical, it’s like my brain just can’t keep up with even the simplest thought process. My emotions have completely flatlined, too – I just feel numb to everything. This has definitely started affecting my work performance; I'm missing deadlines and getting called out for minor errors.

Then, about three months ago, I had this strange burst of energy. I felt incredibly up, almost euphoric, like I could take on the world. I barely needed any sleep, just a few hours, and I'd wake up completely refreshed, practically bouncing off the walls. I felt this intense need to constantly be doing something. During that time, I was incredibly anxious, more so than usual. It felt like my mind was constantly racing, flitting from one thought to another, and I'd often make impulsive decisions, all stemming from this underlying knot of worry about everything, absolutely everything.
option:
A. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    D. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder

question: I’ve been feeling really down and on edge since my husband had his heart attack three months ago. Before that, I was managing just fine—working my part-time census job, keeping up with our routines. But ever since that day in the hospital, I can’t seem to shake this heavy sadness and constant worry. I cry over small things, have trouble sleeping, and my mind races with fears—what if he has another one? What if I’m not there? I’ve lost interest in gardening, which I’ve loved for years, and I don’t feel like seeing friends. But even though I’m low, I don’t feel completely hopeless—I know it could be worse, and I haven't had thoughts about dying or felt like I’m worth nothing. I just feel overwhelmed by it all. The anxiety comes in waves—I get tense, shaky, like I can't catch my breath when I'm filling out forms or driving. I knew something was off when these feelings started right after the hospital stay, and they’ve stayed, though they do ebb and flow. I want to get back to normal, especially for him, but right now, it feels just out of reach.
option:
A. Adjustment Disorder
    B. Major Depressive Disorder
    C. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    D. Binge-Eating Disorder

question:
"It's been a tough year, Doc. I started hearing voices about eight months ago, whispering things, sometimes flat-out telling me what to do. Most of the time they're just background noise, but sometimes they get real clear, talking about me, making me feel like everything on the news, every car passing by, is a signal just for me. My thoughts jump around like crazy too; one minute I'm thinking about a traffic stop, the next I'm planning my grocery list, and then I'm wondering if the voices are being broadcast through my radio.

Around the same time, maybe ten months ago, my mood began to really swing. I’d be on top of the world, full of energy, ready to take on the whole precinct, then suddenly I’d crash, feel utterly miserable and just want to isolate myself. This high-gear feeling has usually only lasted a few days, maybe a week tops, before I come back down. I haven’t been able to finish anything, always bouncing from one task to another, whether it's paperwork or even just cleaning my apartment. For the last three months, though, the low has been really bad. I’ve pulled away from everyone, even my buddies from the force. Sleep seems pointless and sometimes I just think about ending it all, how I'd do it, where, when... My job's on the line; I can't keep this up."
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Schizophrenia
    C. Delusional Disorder
    D. Binge-Eating Disorder

question:
I’ve been feeling down pretty much every day for the past three years—just a constant heaviness, like a cloud that won’t lift. I still go out to feed the animals and tend the fields, but everything feels harder now. I don’t take pride in the farm like I used to, and I doubt myself all the time—like I’m not sure I can handle simple decisions about crop rotation or repairs. It’s not that I can’t work, but I feel like I’m just getting by, not doing a good job. I’ve also been eating more, especially snacks and sweets—my wife’s noticed, too. I used to never touch that stuff.  

About a year back, there was a short stretch—maybe three or four days—where I felt a bit more energized, slept less, and was talking more at the market. But it wasn’t like I was reckless or couldn’t stop talking—just slightly more upbeat. It faded fast, and I haven’t had anything like it since. It didn’t feel like a full episode, and my wife said it wasn’t out of the ordinary. So, I don’t think it meant much. But this low mood? That’s been steady.
option:
A. Delusional Disorder
    B. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    C. Bipolar II Disorder
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question:
I’ve always been kind of “all over the place,” even as a kid. My teachers used to write on my report cards that I was smart but never finished what I started, constantly daydreaming or blurting things out. I remember in elementary school I’d start my homework, then get up, forget what I was doing, start something else. That scattered, restless feeling has pretty much been there my whole life.

Now, with my job running multiple social media accounts, it’s a mess. I’ll open my laptop to schedule posts, then end up scrolling, jumping between tabs, forgetting deadlines. My mind jumps so fast I lose track mid-sentence, and I’m fidgety in meetings, interrupting people without meaning to.

On top of that, after what happened last year—the assault—I keep getting these episodes where it’s like I’m back there. I zone out and suddenly my body feels like it’s reliving it; it’s not just a memory. Since then, I feel constantly on edge and snappy over tiny things. My friends say I “go off” over nothing. I also carry this heavy guilt, like somehow it was my fault, and even when something good happens at work or in my life, it’s like I’m numb—I can’t actually feel happy.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    D. Acute Stress Disorder

question:
It’s been over two years now, this constant grey cloud hanging over me. I just feel so sad most of the time, like there’s no real joy, just this persistent gloom. It makes it hard to even face going to work sometimes, though I push through. I compare myself to the other flight attendants who seem so effortlessly cheerful and put together, and I just feel utterly inadequate, like I'm falling behind everyone else. My memory has taken a hit too; I’m always misplacing things – my keys, my tablet, sometimes even forgetting passenger requests mid-flight. It’s pretty embarrassing.

But lately, there have been these bursts of energy that are totally out of character. A couple of months ago, I was so wired I barely slept for four days straight, planning this elaborate solo trip to Thailand, which I usually wouldn’t even consider. I bought flights and hotels impulsively, spent way more than I should have, and felt amazing, like I could do anything. I was talking a mile a minute, and my friends even commented on how unusually upbeat and grandiose I seemed. That phase eventually crashed, leaving me more exhausted and down than usual. It was a clear shift, something more than just mood swings. It made me wonder if there's something else going on beyond just feeling low.
option:
A. Bipolar II Disorder
    B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Schizophrenia
    D. Persistent Depressive Disorder

question:
It’s been going on for a little over a year now, maybe closer to a year and a half. I’m a freelance voice actress, I work mostly from my apartment studio, and on the surface things are fine — I still meet deadlines, pay my rent, keep in touch with a few friends. But there’s this… fixed idea I can’t shake.

I’m absolutely convinced that my voice is not just “good,” but uniquely powerful — like, literally capable of influencing people on a different level than normal. Not just emotionally: I believe producers, casting directors, even people hearing me in ads are subtly guided by my voice in ways they don’t realize. I feel like I’m meant to be discovered and become a huge, almost legendary figure in the industry, and that there are already people “behind the scenes” tracking my work because of this.

Everyone keeps telling me it’s unrealistic, but it doesn’t feel unrealistic to me; it feels obvious, like a fact. I don’t hear voices, I don’t see things, I don’t think the TV is talking to me. My thoughts feel clear and organized. I just can’t get rid of this belief about my voice and my destiny, and it colors how I interpret pretty much everything that happens in my career.
option:
A. Schizophrenia
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Delusional Disorder
    D. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
Lately, it’s been like my body won’t let me stop—even when I’m exhausted. For about three months now, I’ve been rehearsing nonstop, taking extra classes, and even trying to choreograph my own pieces while still doing full company rehearsals. I barely sleep, but I don’t feel tired. My mind races—ideas just flood in, one after another—and I have to act on them immediately, like if I don’t, something terrible will happen. I’ve snapped at dancers over tiny mistakes, and I can’t stand when things slow down. I talk constantly, even in the middle of stretches or between cues—my words just spill out.  

I do feel anxious sometimes, especially when I think I won’t finish everything, but the racing thoughts and the need to *do, do, do*—they don’t come from the anxiety. It’s the opposite—my thoughts are just *there*, loud and fast, forcing me forward, whether I’m worried or not. The anxiety is kind of along for the ride, but it’s not *driving*. And honestly, it’s starting to scare me—last week I almost quit the company on impulse. I know this isn’t normal.
option:
A. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    B. Bipolar I Disorder
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    D. Bipolar II Disorder

question:
It’s kind of hard to explain because it feels like two different versions of me.

About a year ago, I started having these stretches where I barely sleep, like 3 hours a night for a week or more, and I’m still wired. I feel unbeatable, like my brain is on fast‑forward. I’ll get these huge ideas about opening my own restaurant right now, start calling suppliers at 2 a.m., signing up for extra classes, spending way too much on fancy ingredients and knives I can’t afford. I talk nonstop in class, jump from idea to idea. My friends say I’m “on one,” and my mom says I sound like I’m high, but I’m not. I just feel supercharged and kind of invincible, until I crash later into these really low, heavy phases.

The food stuff happens more in both phases, honestly. I’ll raid the pantry and eat an entire family‑size bag of chips, leftover pasta, half a cake we made in lab—way more than anyone else would. Once I start, it’s like a switch flips and I can’t stop, even when my stomach hurts and I’m painfully full. I feel totally out of control while it’s happening, then disgusted and ashamed afterward.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    B. Binge-Eating Disorder
    C. Bipolar I Disorder
    D. Schizophrenia

question:
I sell residential property, so being “on” all the time is kind of part of the job, but I feel like my brain has always been stuck on fast‑forward. Since I was a kid, I’ve talked too fast, jumped from topic to topic, and had this constant stream of thoughts that makes it hard to focus on one thing. Teachers used to say I “blurted out” and couldn’t sit still; that never really went away. Even now, in client meetings, I’ll catch myself zoning out mid‑sentence or interrupting because something else popped into my head. I’ve always needed less sleep than other people and still wake up feeling wired.

Over the past two months, I’ve been extra upbeat and buzzing with energy. I’m pacing around the house, can’t sit through a TV show, constantly checking listings and ideas. I even put a big chunk of our savings into a new development deal without really researching it—I just felt so sure it would work. Before that, for a couple of months, I was down, dragging myself to showings, lying awake for hours before I could fall asleep. That low period really hurt my work and my marriage; this current “up” phase actually helps me get things done, even if I’m scattered.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Bipolar II Disorder
    D. Persistent Depressive Disorder

question: I’ve been feeling really down for the past five months—just empty, tired, like nothing matters. I don’t enjoy driving anymore, and that’s saying something after 20 years on the road. My wife keeps asking what’s wrong, but I don’t know how to explain it. I barely talk to her now. I’ve lost weight, not because I’m trying, but because I either don’t eat at all or end up eating whatever’s around—sometimes two fast food meals back-to-back, even when I’m not hungry. I know it’s a lot, way more than most guys would eat in one sitting, especially on the highway with no one around. I keep it hidden. I won’t even stop at diners anymore because I’m embarrassed—so I eat alone in the truck, shoving food down until I feel sick. But it’s not about the food. It’s the numbness I’m after. The truth is, I feel hopeless. Some mornings, I don’t see the point in starting the engine. I’m not just overeating—I’m drowning, and I’ve felt this way nearly every day for months.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder
    B. Binge-Eating Disorder
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    D. Anorexia Nervosa

question:
I’ve been feeling real low for the past six months or so—just no joy in anything anymore, not even the farm, which used to be my pride. I keep thinking I’m a failure, that I haven’t done enough for my family, and that God is punishing me for not being a better man. These thoughts circle in my head all day, especially in the morning before chores. I don’t want them, but they’re not random or shocking—they fit how I feel inside: heavy, guilty, broken.

My wife keeps saying she sees me stopping to straighten tools on the rack, or lining up the bottles in the medicine shed just so, but I don’t do it to calm a thought or to follow a rule—I don’t get any urge to fix things unless they’re bothering me visually. That’s just habit, I think. But the truth is, I spend hours sitting at the kitchen table, going over the same thoughts: that I’ve let everyone down, that my work doesn’t matter, that my marriage is just a burden now. I wake up early, tired, and the day feels pointless. I don’t have the energy to care.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Major Depressive Disorder
    C. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    D. Bulimia Nervosa

question:
It’s really just this one thing that sets me off: bridges. Driving over them, walking under them, even seeing one on the news. The rest of my life is fairly calm—I’m retired, I read the paper, putter around the garden—but if I know we have to cross a bridge, I start going to pieces.

For the past couple of years, any time my wife suggests visiting our son across town, my mind jumps straight to disaster. I picture the bridge collapsing, the car going over the edge, us trapped in the water. It feels ridiculous, but in the moment it seems completely certain. I start sweating, my heart races, and I can’t think about anything else until I find a way to avoid that route.

Otherwise I’m not lying awake worrying about my health or money or the grandchildren. In fact, I’ve been sleeping more than usual—dozing off in the afternoon and still sleeping long at night, especially after I’ve had a scare about a bridge. I’m jittery too: a car backfiring or the creak of the house makes me jump, but it’s worst if it reminds me of traffic on a bridge.
option:
A. Body Dysmorphic Disorder
    B. Acute Stress Disorder
    C. Specific Phobia
    D. Generalized Anxiety Disorder

question:
I’ve been struggling with my eating for about the past three months—probably around once a week, sometimes more. There are these moments, usually late at night after a double shift, when I’ll find myself eating massive amounts of food—like half a pizza, a family-sized bag of chips, cookies, just nonstop. I know it’s way more than anyone would eat in one sitting, and the worst part is, I feel like I can’t stop. It’s not even about being hungry. I just... lose control.

I always do it alone, locked in my apartment. I’ve canceled plans with friends before because I knew a binge was coming. Afterward, I feel so disgusted with myself—ashamed, sad, guilty, like I’ve failed. I’ve tried to "make up for it" by skipping meals the next day, but I don’t purge, and I don’t make myself throw up or use laxatives. That’s not really my thing. I did try fasting once or twice, but not regularly. It’s more that the shame keeps me stuck, not any routine I follow to undo the eating. The binges keep coming back, especially when I’m stressed from work.
option:
A. Bulimia Nervosa
    B. Delusional Disorder
    C. Binge-Eating Disorder
    D. Specific Phobia

question:
I’ve been really off for the past six or seven months. Ever since that fire in the restaurant kitchen last year, I know something changed, but it’s not just about the fire anymore.

When anything reminds me of it—like the smell of burning oil or the sound of a smoke alarm—my heart races, my hands sweat, and I feel like I might throw up. I try hard not to think about that day at all. If my wife brings it up, I shut down or change the subject. The weird thing is, I can’t actually remember most of what happened during the fire itself—just little fragments, like flashing lights and someone yelling, but the rest is just blank.

What scares me most now are these voices. They started months after the fire. They’re not about the fire; they talk about random things, like criticizing how I plate dishes or telling me my coworkers are plotting against me. Sometimes I see shadows moving in the kitchen that no one else sees. I’ve started to feel like the staff is in on some plan, so I avoid them and keep to myself, which is hard when you’re supposed to be running a team.
option:
A. Acute Stress Disorder
    B. Schizophrenia
    C. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)

question:
It’s been going on for about a year now, maybe a bit more. I get these thoughts that just slam into my head out of nowhere—like that I might suddenly stab someone with a kitchen knife, or that I’ve secretly insulted a client in the code and somehow ruined their business. They’re not about anything that’s actually happened to me; they’re just…random and awful. I know they don’t make sense, but I can’t shake them.

When they show up, I feel this urgent need to “neutralize” them. I’ll delete and rewrite the same lines of code over and over until it “feels right,” or recheck if I locked my door 6–7 times before I can leave for work. I wash my hands repeatedly after touching my phone or keyboard because I’m scared I’ll contaminate someone else.

It’s not like something specific traumatized me recently—I don’t have flashbacks or anything like that. I’m not jumping at noises or constantly on edge about danger. It’s more like I’m stuck in these mental loops, and my whole day gets eaten up by checking, cleaning, and going over things again and again.
option:
A. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Acute Stress Disorder
    D. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder

question:
It really started after that shoot in Syria, about a year and a half ago. Before that, I could spend 12 hours in an edit bay, completely locked in. Now my attention is all over the place. In the studio, I’m staring at the timeline, but my mind keeps jumping back to that night—the checkpoint, the explosion. I didn’t have these focus issues as a kid or early in my career; it’s only been since I got back from that assignment.

Any little cue sets me off—helicopter sounds in a film, a certain smell, even the clack of a camera shutter—and my body just reacts. My heart races, my palms get sweaty, my stomach drops like I’m right back there. Sometimes I have to leave the room.

I feel this constant sense of disgrace about what happened, like I failed the people I was filming. Even when my last documentary did well, all I could think was, “You don’t deserve this.” I can’t feel proud of anything anymore.

And I snap at people. My editor, my ex-wife, even my kid—I’ll suddenly be shouting, swearing, blaming them for nothing, then feel awful afterward but still on edge.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    D. Acute Stress Disorder

question:
For the past couple of months I’ve just felt hollow, like someone scooped everything out of me. I go to work, but I’m basically on autopilot. I run a big hotel, I’m supposed to be sharp and on top of a thousand details, but lately I’ll just stare at the computer screen or drift off in the middle of staff briefings and realize I didn’t register a word people said. Even at home, I’ll try to watch a show or read and my mind just slides off it.

I sleep 7–8 hours, sometimes more, but I still wake up feeling like I’ve been up all night. No matter how much I rest, I’m exhausted and dragging myself through the day. I’ve actually had to cut my hours because I can’t keep up.

I’ve also been thinking about ending things in a very concrete way—where I’d do it, how I’d make sure no one interrupted, writing notes in my head. It scares me how detailed those thoughts have gotten.

This is very different from times in the past when I’ve gone in the opposite direction—barely sleeping for days, feeling invincible, talking nonstop, making big, risky plans for the hotel and my own life, and people around me saying I was “on a high” or “over the top.” Those spells got me into trouble at work and financially, and they’ve happened more than once.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder
    B. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    C. Bipolar I Disorder
    D. Anorexia Nervosa

question: "It started subtle, but now it's taken over. I drive a subway, and even underground, I feel like I'm constantly on edge. I used to enjoy my food, but lately, just thinking about certain things, especially anything fatty or sugary, makes my stomach clench. It's not a fear of *the food itself*, not like I'm worried about choking or getting sick. It's more... the fear of what it will *do to me*. The thought of gaining weight, even a pound, fills me with dread. It’s like a paralysis; I’ll freeze up, unable to eat when everyone else is, making excuses. Sometimes, the anxiety around it is so intense, it’s like a physical threat, like encountering a giant spider. Even the smell of certain foods can make me just shut down. This has been going on for over six months now, and my wife is really worried."
option:
A. Anorexia Nervosa
    B. Specific Phobia
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

question: I’ve been struggling for years, but it’s gotten much worse lately—probably over the past six months or so. As a dietitian, I’m supposed to help people feel good about their health, but I can’t stop thinking about how my face looks. I’m convinced my nose is misshapen and too wide, and that my skin is covered in scars and bumps no one else seems to see. I spend hours in front of the mirror every day, checking from different angles, and I pick at my skin constantly, especially around my nose and cheeks, trying to smooth it out or remove what I think are scabs or imperfections. I know it sounds extreme—even my ex-husband said I was obsessed—but the flaws feel so real. I’ve tried hiding them with makeup, rescheduling appointments if I feel worse that day, even thinking about surgery. But even when the skin heals, I still see the flaw. I *know* people tell me I look fine, but I just can’t believe them. The picking and checking—that’s the only way I can calm down, even if it doesn’t last.
option:
A. Body Dysmorphic Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    D. Delusional Disorder

question: I'm here because things have been pretty strange for the past couple of months. I keep seeing things that aren't there—shadowy figures, people walking by the corner of my eye when no one's there, even full-on people sometimes. It’s unnerving. And then, everything feels like it's a message for me. Like, if I see the same car twice, it means something specific for my day. Or if an apprentice jokes about a tripped breaker, I think they’re really trying to tell me something about myself. It wasn't always like this. I did have a pretty rough time a while back—a nasty fall from a ladder on a job site, broke my arm pretty bad. But these new things, the visions and the feeling that everything is about me, they didn't really start until much later, and they don't feel connected to that accident at all. It's just all this other stuff that's happening now.
option:
A. Bulimia Nervosa
    B. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    D. Schizophrenia

question: It's been a few weeks now, ever since… that accident. I can't seem to get a proper night's sleep. Every time I close my eyes, I'm back there, reliving it – the screeching tires, the twisted metal, the screaming. It's not just bad dreams; I wake up in a cold sweat, my heart pounding, feeling like it's happening all over again. During the day, it's not much better. If I hear a loud bang or even just see a car that looks similar to the one involved, my stomach churns, and I get this intense feeling of dread, like I'm right back in that moment. Sometimes, it’s like I can even see pieces of it, just for a split second, replaying in front of my eyes, right when I’m feeling most stressed about what happened. It’s always bits and pieces of the accident itself, nothing else. Everything feels so distant, and I just can't focus on my studies, which is a disaster since I’m only a freshman.
option:
A. Acute Stress Disorder
    B. Schizophrenia
    C. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    D. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder

question:
It started after my husband died in that car accident about three years ago. I cleaned up what was left of the car myself, and ever since then, it’s like part of me is stuck there. When I hear tires screech outside the hotel or even see a car bent up on TV, my whole body freezes. My heart pounds, I feel sick, and it’s like I’m right back on the road that night.

Sometimes I don’t just remember it—I see it, like it’s happening again. I’ll be folding sheets at work and suddenly I’m seeing the crashed car, the blood on his shirt. I can hear his voice calling my name, but it’s that night, not some random voice. I know it’s not really happening, but in the moment it feels real.

Since then I walk around with this heavy shame, like I should’ve done something different, been there sooner. Even when something good happens—my granddaughter was born last year—I just feel numb, like I’m watching someone else’s life. Little things set me off; if someone drops something loud behind me or questions how I’m doing my job, I’ll snap, yell, curse at them before I can stop myself. Then I feel even worse afterward.
option:
A. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    B. Acute Stress Disorder
    C. Schizophrenia
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)

question:
For about three months now, I’ve been convinced someone is watching me. Not just in a vague way—I’m sure there are cameras in the streetlamps outside our house and that my publisher is somehow involved, tracking what I write and say. It feels organized, targeted. I know my wife says it isn’t possible, but it feels absolutely real to me.

I also hear a man’s voice commenting on what I’m doing, like narration over my day—when I’m at my desk, when I go to the bathroom at night. Sometimes I catch myself making odd faces in the mirror, twisting my mouth or raising my eyebrows in ways that don’t match how I’m feeling. My wife points it out at dinner; I don’t even notice I’m doing it.

Most of this time I’ve also been deeply low—no interest in writing, hardly leaving the house, feeling useless and guilty, like a burden. That heavy, bleak feeling has been there more often than not. But there was a stretch, maybe three weeks, when the sadness lifted a bit, yet the voice and the certainty about being monitored stayed just as strong.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Anorexia Nervosa
    C. Schizophrenia
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question:
I’ve been feeling really low for about three months now, pretty much every day. I sleep way more than I used to, but I still wake up drained. I’ve stopped going out with friends, stopped applying to jobs most days. I just sit in my room scrolling, feeling useless and guilty, like I’ve already ruined my life and there’s no point trying.

The weird part is what started happening a few weeks into this slump. I became convinced I have some kind of hidden power, like I’m meant to be this world-changing figure. It doesn’t even make sense because I can barely get out of bed, but I’ll catch myself thinking the government or big companies are quietly watching me online because they know I’m special. I know how that sounds, but it feels real in the moment.

Before I got this depressed, I never thought like that. When my mood briefly lifted for a few days, those beliefs kind of faded, and I could see they were off. But when the depression slams back, the “I’m chosen” stuff comes back too, along with the hopelessness and thoughts that maybe everyone would be better off without me.
option:
A. Delusional Disorder
    B. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
answer: C. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
It’s been a little over a year now, since my husband died in that car crash, and I just feel like I’ve gone flat inside. I’m tired all the time, like moving through mud. I used to joke with the girls at work, even as I washed dishes; now I just keep my head down, can’t be bothered talking. Nothing feels enjoyable anymore, not even seeing my grandkids. I wake up at 3 or 4 in the morning most days, and then I just lie there thinking how pointless everything is. My appetite’s gone off and I’ve lost some weight without trying.

I do get thoughts and images of the accident sometimes, they just pop into my head when I’m on the bus or standing at the sink, but it’s not like I’m reliving it over and over. I avoid going past the junction where it happened and I try not to talk about it with his old mates. I catch myself blaming the other driver a lot. For a while I’d double-check the doors at night and look out the window, but that’s eased off. Mostly, I’m just stuck in this heavy, miserable feeling that doesn’t lift, no matter what I do.
option:
A. Specific Phobia
    B. Major Depressive Disorder
    C. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    D. Acute Stress Disorder

question:
I’ve been struggling for years, but it’s gotten worse lately—probably five or six years now. I can’t stop thinking about how my face looks, especially my nose. It feels like one side of my face is sinking or pulling downward, even though nobody else sees it. I know people tell me I look fine, but I *feel* lopsided, and it eats at me all the time. At first, I’d check myself in every mirror I passed—on my phone, shop windows, even the shine on tools at work—just trying to "get it right." But after a while, I started avoiding them altogether because it just made me more upset.  

I’m a welder, so I’m around reflective metal all day, and it’s torture. Even at home, I’ll rearrange the furniture so I don’t catch my reflection. My wife says I’ve become distant, and she doesn’t understand why I won’t take pictures with her anymore.  

The thoughts are constant—about my appearance only, not anything else. I don’t worry about germs or numbers or safety like some people do. It’s all about how I look, how unbalanced I appear. It’s not like I’m checking things for any reason besides my looks—I just can’t stand the way I see myself.
option:
A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    B. Body Dysmorphic Disorder
    C. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question:
I’ve been having a really hard time since that train accident last year—I was on the platform when it happened, and I saw things I can’t unsee. Afterward, I couldn’t sleep, jumped at every sound, and stayed on edge constantly—like my body’s still back there. It never really let up.  

About three months ago, I hit this strange stretch where I was working 16 hours straight, editing nonstop, starting like five projects at once. I was snapping at coworkers over tiny things—totally unreasonable. But I wasn’t high or euphoric—just wound tight, irritable, like my nerves were exposed. It wasn’t a mood high—it just felt like trauma on overdrive. And then it faded back into this heavy fog.  

The last four months have been the worst. I can’t feel anything, not even films I used to love. My thinking’s slow, like wading through mud. I keep believing—*knowing*—that I could’ve stopped the accident, like I was meant to be on that train, like it’s my fault everyone died. Reality bends there—I know it doesn’t make sense, but it *feels* true. I’ve planned out how I’d do it—rope, the attic at my place, when no one’s around.  

The anger, the sleeplessness, the hyper-vigilance—they’re not coming and going randomly. They started *after* the trauma—and they’ve never really left.
option:
A. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    B. Binge-Eating Disorder
    C. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    D. Bipolar II Disorder

question: I’ve been struggling for over a year now—things just feel out of control, even though I’m constantly trying to keep up. As a freelance writer, my job used to be manageable, but now deadlines slip because I can’t follow through, even when I start strong. I’ll organize my work at first, but then the plan falls apart because my mind is so busy with “what ifs.” What if the client hates it? What if I’m not good enough? It’s like my brain won’t shut off. I avoid starting big projects because the thought of focusing for hours makes me feel jittery and overwhelmed—I’m always on edge, like I’ve had too much coffee, even when I haven’t. People say I seem restless, always moving, tapping my pen, pacing. But it’s not excitement—it’s tension. The inattention isn’t about getting distracted by new things; it’s that my thoughts keep spiraling into worry. I know I should just focus, but my mind feels hijacked by fear, not by boredom or distraction.
option:
A. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    C. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Binge-Eating Disorder

question:
It’s been about three months now that things have been…off. It started with this feeling that something’s wrong inside my body. I’m sure there’s some kind of parasite in my blood from being out at sea, like it’s crawling through my veins and changing my organs, but every test they’ve run keeps coming back “normal.” That doesn’t make sense to me. I can feel it.

Around the same time I started hearing voices, mostly when I’m alone on the boat or trying to sleep. They’re not my thoughts. Sometimes they argue with each other about me, sometimes they tell me my body is rotting from the inside. They’ve been there pretty much every day, whether I’m feeling up or down.

There were a couple of weeks when I barely slept, felt wired, talking fast, pacing around with all these big ideas about buying my own fleet, feeling on top of the world. But even when that “high” faded and I crashed back down, the voices and the body stuff just kept going.

I don’t really hang out with the guys anymore after a trip. I mostly stay in my bunk or at home, not talking much, even with my wife. It just feels easier to stay away.
option:
A. Schizophrenia
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    C. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    D. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
Lately, I’ve been feeling like I’m on top of the world—like I *know* things others don’t. For months, I’ve been certain that a famous crypto analyst, someone I’ve never met, has been sending me secret signals through her livestreams. She doesn’t have to say it outright—I *feel* the connection. At first, it felt exhilarating, like we shared a hidden bond. I’d stay up for days analyzing her videos, convinced she was building a secret roadmap just for me to follow.  

But over time, I stopped talking to friends. I stopped caring. I’d just sit and watch the market, not responding when people messaged me—not because I was depressed, but because nothing else mattered. Even when my roommate tried to get me to eat or take a break, I’d just stare. No urge to move. No point.  

This has been going on for about seven months. The craziest part? All these thoughts, the intensity, the energy—it only flared up during this one stretch a few months back where I felt unstoppable, like my mind was faster than everyone else’s. I didn’t sleep for almost a week, made huge trades on impulse. That’s when the delusions started. Now that the high’s faded, the paranoia’s gone too. But the isolation and negativism… those stuck around.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Delusional Disorder
    C. Schizophrenia
    D. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
answer: A. Bipolar I Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
It started about three weeks ago, right after the car accident. Since then, my sleep has completely fallen apart. I keep having these really vivid dreams where I’m back in the car—hearing the crunch of metal, seeing the headlights coming at me. I bolt awake drenched in sweat, heart racing, sometimes screaming. It happens most nights now, so I dread going to bed and end up staying up late “working” on articles but not really getting anything done.

During the day, it’s like the accident sneaks back in. If I hear tires screech, or even just see a similar intersection, it’s like I’m transported right back there. I’ll see the other car out of the corner of my eye, feel the impact again, and for a few seconds I honestly can’t tell if it’s happening now or just in my head. I know it sounds like I’m seeing things, but it’s always the crash—no random voices or bizarre stuff—just that same scene replaying itself. When that happens, I get shaky, my chest tightens, and I have to stop whatever I’m doing and just try to breathe.
option:
A. Acute Stress Disorder
    B. Schizophrenia
    C. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    D. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder

question:
I’ve been struggling a lot since that night about six months ago—there are things I can’t stop remembering, like flashes of what happened. I get these sudden images that pop up when I’m just working at the café, and sometimes I break down while steaming milk. I try not to think about it, but it’s like my mind keeps going back, and I've started avoiding talking to people about how I feel. I don’t trust anyone anymore—I feel like everyone’s hiding something, like the world is just full of danger and nothing really matters. I’ve been angry too—last week I threw a plate when my roommate raised her voice, and I didn’t even mean to.  

But honestly, most days I’m just… numb. I don’t enjoy anything anymore—not music, not hanging out, not even coffee. I can’t get out of bed most mornings, and when I do, it’s like every task is impossible. I feel worthless, like I’m broken. I cry a lot, sleep too much, and I’ve lost weight because I don’t want to eat. It started after the trauma, but it’s not just about that one night—it’s everything now. I don’t have nightmares or feel “on edge” all the time, and I wouldn’t say the trauma fully took over my life the way it might in PTSD—I just feel deeply, persistently sad.
option:
A. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    B. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    C. Acute Stress Disorder
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question:
For a little over three years now, I’ve felt like I’m just…empty inside. Not sad exactly, just numb, like nothing really touches me. I go through the motions at work and at home, but it’s like I’m watching myself on camera instead of actually being there. My wife says I’m distant. I used to be proud of being a good cop, good husband, but now I just feel like I’m worse than everyone else on the force, like I’m always the weak link.

I’ve also been really forgetful. I’ll set my radio down and can’t remember where, or I’ll park the cruiser and have to think twice where I left it. It’s not like me. My head just feels foggy and unfocused a lot of the time, and it’s made paperwork and even basic patrol stuff harder.

But then I have these stretches—like last spring—that are the complete opposite. For about a week, I barely slept at all, maybe two hours a night, and still felt wired. My thoughts were racing, I was talking nonstop, taking crazy risks on calls, like I was invincible. My wife said I was “on fast‑forward” and not myself. It got bad enough that my supervisor pulled me off duty and told me this wasn’t just stress. Those high periods are a lot shorter than the low, empty feeling, but they’re intense and have happened more than once.
option:
A. Specific Phobia
    B. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    C. Bipolar I Disorder
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question:
I’ve been through such extreme ups and downs over the past few months—each lasting about two months, I’d say. A couple months ago, I was suddenly full of energy. I wasn’t tired, even when I barely slept. I threw myself into work projects, reorganized half the library on my own time, and started reaching out to old acquaintances, even going on dates. I felt sharp, charming—almost invincible. But my moods were all over the place: one minute I’d be laughing and planning trips, the next I’d snap at coworkers for small things. I did some things I wouldn’t normally do—I got involved with someone I barely knew, physically—just impulsive, unlike me.

Then it shifted. That spark vanished. Now, I feel… empty. Like I’m going through motions but nothing means anything. I sleep eight hours and wake up exhausted. I can’t focus. I’ve missed work, stopped replying to friends. But during the high phase, I wasn’t hospitalized, I didn’t lose my job—just felt restless, driven. No voices, no delusions. It wasn’t psychosis—just too much energy, too much *me*, until it all crashed.
option:
A. Bipolar I Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    C. Bipolar II Disorder
    D. Persistent Depressive Disorder

question:
I’ve been struggling for about three years now, mostly fixated on my hairline. I know it’s receding a bit—nothing extreme—but in my mind, it looks like a huge, jagged canyon across my forehead. I check it constantly in mirrors, phone screens, windows—any reflective surface. Sometimes I’ll spend over an hour in the bathroom in the morning adjusting my part, using styling gels, even trying to grow it out unevenly to cover it. I wear hoodies or caps almost every day, even indoors at the office, and if I forget my sunglasses, I feel exposed. At work, during video calls, I’ll angle my camera just right or turn my laptop so my profile isn’t visible. I’ve thought about quitting because it’s so exhausting.

I know people say “it’s not that bad,” but they don’t see what I see. I’ve tried changing my routine, cutting down on the mirror checks, but I can’t stop myself. I’ll still run my fingers over my scalp dozens of times a day, measuring the thin spots. It’s not just belief—it’s something I have to *do*, over and over, or I feel like I’m falling apart.
option:
A. Body Dysmorphic Disorder
    B. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    D. Delusional Disorder

question:
I’ve been struggling for over a year now—since early last year, actually. At first, it was the voices. I’d hear whispering when no one was around, like neighbors gossiping about me, saying things like “She knows he loves her” and “He’s coming to get her.” I knew it was true—this man, a client I showed a house to, he’s in love with me. He doesn’t say it, but the voices confirm it. He sends signs through the radio, too, little rhymes and sound patterns, like “keys to the city, destiny’s sweet—if you listen, you’ll meet!” That’s how I know he’s trying to reach me.

For about 11 months, I felt unstoppable—I’d work 20 hours straight, couldn’t sleep, but didn’t need to. Sirens, lights, a leaf blowing—everything grabbed my attention. But that phase passed. The last five months were the worst—empty, like I’d destroyed the world. I believed I caused that tornado last spring—God made it happen because of my sin.  

But here’s the thing: the high energy? It was intense, but not nearly as long as the voices and beliefs. That’s been constant—over a year. The rest? It came and went.
option:
A. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    B. Schizophrenia
    C. Delusional Disorder
    D. Binge-Eating Disorder

question:
I’ve been hearing voices for about three months now—voices that tell me the world is already ending, that everything’s腐烂 and beyond saving. I can’t shut them out. Sometimes I catch myself making faces, like grimacing, even when I don’t feel anything—my coworkers noticed, and I can only mumble an excuse.  

For the past two months, I barely slept, not that I needed to—I was too charged, racing through casework, convinced I could solve every unsolved homicide in the database by myself. I was texting detectives at 3 a.m., sending wild theories. My mind felt like it was on fire. But here’s the thing—it didn’t stop when the energy faded. Even after the racing thoughts quieted, the voices stayed. For two weeks last month, I wasn’t manic—no rush, no grand plans—just sitting in my apartment, hearing whispers that the lab’s been poisoned, that I’m already dead.  

That’s when I knew it wasn’t *just* the high. The psychosis didn’t wait for my mood. It outlasted it.
option:
A. Schizophrenia
    B. Bulimia Nervosa
    C. Schizoaffective Disorder (Bipolar Type)
    D. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features

question:
I’ve been feeling really down for the past four months—since I kind of crashed after my final exams. Nothing gives me energy anymore. I used to enjoy sketching in my notebook while at work, but now I can’t even focus long enough to draw a simple line. I’ll space out in the middle of making a latte and forget what drink I was making. Mistakes keep happening, and my manager noticed.  

It’s not like I’m restless or talking over people—I don’t even want to speak most days. But sometimes when my coworkers ask me questions, I’ll blurt out an answer before they finish, just to get the interaction over with. It’s not because I’m hyper or impatient—it’s because my brain feels so heavy, and I just want to stop thinking.  

The thing is, none of this was a problem before I started feeling like this. I never had trouble focusing in school, and I could sit and read for hours. But now, even watching TV feels exhausting. I can’t concentrate, but it only got this bad *after* I stopped sleeping, lost my appetite, and stopped caring about everything. It’s like the sadness came first—and the lack of focus followed.
option:
A. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    B. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Combined Presentation)
    C. Major Depressive Disorder
    D. Schizoaffective Disorder (Depressive Type)

question:
I’ve struggled with my moods and focus for as long as I can remember—like, seriously, since I was a kid, maybe even elementary school. I always felt like I was buzzing inside, like I couldn’t sit still during class, and I’d blurt out answers before I even thought them through. My parents said I was “always on the go” and teachers complained I couldn’t finish assignments because I kept jumping to new things.  

The past three weeks have been even more intense—like my brain won’t shut off. I’ve been bouncing between feeling on top of the world and snapping at small things. I stayed up for days organizing my entire apartment and starting five different research drafts, but never finished any. It feels like my thoughts are racing nonstop, and I talk so fast people ask me to slow down.  

Now, looking back, I realize this isn’t new behavior—it’s been like this since before I was 12. It’s not like the mood swings come out of nowhere. The hyperactivity, the impulsivity, the distractibility—they’ve always been there, just now worse. And honestly, last year, after my mom died, I was in such a dark place for months—couldn’t feel anything, felt guilty all the time, even planned how I’d end it. That was different. That was truly down. This? This is just... me, sped up.
option:
A. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    B. Bipolar II Disorder
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Hyperactive/Impulsive Presentation)
    D. Anorexia Nervosa

question:
It really started after the accident at the warehouse about eight months ago, when the shelving collapsed and that guy was killed right next to me. Since then, my head just doesn’t shut off. I keep getting these thoughts and images of it, over and over—his body, the noise, the screaming. They pop in when I’m doing normal stuff, like washing dishes or trying to watch TV. It feels like my brain is forcing me to relive it, not like I’m choosing to think about it, and I’m not doing any rituals or anything to make them stop—I just kind of freeze or try to push them away.

At night I hardly sleep. I have these nightmares where it’s happening again, or I’m the one getting crushed. I wake up sweating, heart racing, sometimes I’ve scared my wife. I go out of my way not to drive near the old workplace or even talk about it with anyone; if a news story comes on about accidents, I change the channel immediately.

I can’t relax, even at home. I’m constantly tense, scanning for sounds, jumping at every little noise. I keep thinking, “If I had spoken up about those shelves, maybe he’d still be alive,” and I can’t shake this feeling that it’s my fault.
option:
A. Acute Stress Disorder
    B. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    C. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    D. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder

question: I’ve been feeling completely on edge since it happened—just over two weeks ago. I was at a wedding venue finalizing setup when a groom’s brother attacked me in the storage room. I managed to get away, but ever since, my mind hasn’t been the same. I can’t focus long enough to go over contracts or even look at design sketches—simple things like reading an email feel impossible. My husband says I jump whenever he walks into the room, even if he calls my name. I know I’m safe at home, but I can’t relax. I keep replaying that moment, and every sudden movement—a door swinging, someone reaching for something—sends my heart racing. I’ve started avoiding places where people move around too much. It’s only been, what, 16 days? But it feels like I’m breaking in real time. I’m scared that if this keeps going, I won’t be able to work. But right now, it’s all still so fresh—I wake up tense every morning like I’m bracing for it to happen again.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    B. Acute Stress Disorder
    C. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (Predominantly Inattentive Presentation)
    D. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder

question:
I’ve been having these awful flashbacks—sometimes on the train, out of nowhere. I’ll suddenly feel like I’m back in that tunnel during the derailment, hearing the screeching, the passengers screaming. It only lasts a few seconds, but I lose focus, and that scares me. I don’t talk about it much, not even with my wife. I just shut down when it comes up.  

People keep asking if I’m okay, but they don’t get it. I used to trust my crew, my dispatcher—now I feel like everyone’s just waiting for me to fail. I even blamed the maintenance team for weeks, thinking they missed something. Still do, sometimes.  

And the anger… I blew up at a coworker last week over nothing. One minute we’re talking, the next I’m yelling. It’s not like me.  

But honestly, the worst part is how low I feel all the time. I don’t enjoy driving anymore. Can’t sleep, no appetite, just… empty. I thought this would pass, but it’s been six months. The flashbacks and avoidance are there, sure, but it’s not *just* the trauma—my whole life feels dark now. I went to counseling, and they said I don’t quite meet the full criteria for PTSD, but the depression… that’s real, and it’s crushing me.
option:
A. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    B. Generalized Anxiety Disorder
    C. Acute Stress Disorder
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question: It’s been, gosh, at least three years now, maybe more, where I just feel this heavy cloud hanging over me. It’s not constant, but most days, I’m just sad, like a dull ache that never really goes away. It’s not intense enough to completely debilitate me, but it’s always there, a persistent gloom that makes everything a bit harder. My thesis… it’s been a struggle. I used to be so driven, but now I just feel like everything I write is worthless, that I’m not smart enough to finish. I’ve gained a good amount of weight too, probably about ten pounds in the last month alone, and that just makes me feel even worse about myself. It's like I’m in this steady state of low-grade misery that just stretches on and on. I haven’t had a break from feeling this way, not a sustained period of feeling 'normal' in what feels like forever.
option:
A. Persistent Depressive Disorder
    B. Major Depressive Disorder with Psychotic Features
    C. Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
    D. Major Depressive Disorder

question: I’ve been really struggling since my divorce was finalized about two months ago—everything’s just been too much. I didn’t think it would hit me this hard, but some days, I’ll be in the middle of a client session and suddenly feel like I’m right back in that last fight with my ex, yelling in the kitchen. It’s like I can see it, hear it—I freeze up for a second, and my heart races. It only lasts a moment, but it’s terrifying. I’m on edge all the time now; little things set me off—a missed deadline, a snide comment from a coworker—I snap like I never used to. I’m a nutritionist, so I pride myself on balance, on being calm and in control, but now I feel like I’m falling apart. My sleep’s off, I’ve lost my appetite, and I avoid talking about it because I don’t want to break down. I knew it would be hard, but this started within weeks of the divorce being official, and it hasn’t let up. I don’t feel detached or numb, and I’m not having nightmares every night—just this constant tension and those sudden flashbacks. It’s not that I can’t function, but I’m not the person I was.
option:
A. Major Depressive Disorder
    B. Adjustment Disorder
    C. Acute Stress Disorder
    D. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder

question: It's been a tough few months, almost half a year now, actually. My eating feels completely out of control. When I get home, especially after a long flight, I just can't stop myself. I'll eat huge amounts of food – entire pizzas, boxes of cookies – until I'm so full it hurts. I always eat alone because I'm so ashamed of how much I'm consuming. Sometimes, after a particularly bad episode, I'll try to do something to make up for it, like going for a really long run or cutting back drastically the next day. But honestly, those efforts are sporadic, just a one-off thing, not really a regular pattern. It's the bingeing that happens at least once a week, and it's making me feel so awful about myself. My husband tries to be understanding, but it’s hard for him to grasp.
option:
A. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    B. Major Depressive Disorder
    C. Binge-Eating Disorder
    D. Bulimia Nervosa

question: Lately, things have been really tough, doc. It’s like I get this uncontrollable urge to just… eat. I’ll polish off an entire pizza, a whole tub of ice cream, and a bag of chips in one sitting. It's definitely way more than anyone else I know would eat. I keep going until I'm so full it hurts, even when my stomach's not actually rumbling. Afterwards, the guilt just washes over me. I’ve been trying to make up for it by making myself throw up, or sometimes I'll over-exercise, running for miles even if I’m exhausted. This has been happening at least once a week, for the past three months now. It's exhausting, and I just can't seem to stop the cycle.
option:
A. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
    B. Binge-Eating Disorder
    C. Bulimia Nervosa
    D. Persistent Depressive Disorder
